News

TKO Group Holdings, Inc. is an American sports and sports entertainment company. Established on September 12, 2023, the public company was formed by a merger between Endeavor subsidiary Zuffa—the parent company of mixed martial arts promotion Ultimate Fighting Championship —and the professional wrestling promotion World Wrestling Entertainment. TKO is led by CEO Ari Emanuel and president Mark Shapiro, both of Endeavor; Dana White and Nick Khan retained their roles as CEOs of UFC and WWE respectively upon the merger, while WWE co-founder Vince McMahon served as executive chairman until resigning from the company in January 2024 amid a sex trafficking scandal. The merger marked the first time that WWE has not been solely and primarily majority-controlled by the McMahon family, which founded the company and owned it for over 70 years. As of 2024, the UFC and WWE were the two most valuable combat sports organizations in the world according to Forbes. UFC was listed as the most valued mixed martial arts company with a revenue of $1.406 billion and WWE being the most valued professional wrestling promotion with a revenue of $1.398 billion in 2023. (Wikipedia)

TKO owns iconic properties including UFC, the world’s premier mixed martial arts organization; WWE, the global leader in sports entertainment; and PBR, the world’s premier bull riding organization. Together, these properties reach 210 countries and territories and organize more than 500 live events year-round, attracting more than three million fans.

TKO also services and partners with major sports rights holders through IMG, an industry-leading global sports marketing agency; and On Location, a global leader in premium experiential hospitality. (Credit: TKO Group)

News Flashback

September 2, 2025

Market regime change: Microsoft weakening whilst Alphabet strengthens Nvidia and OpenAI have become synonymous with the AI revolution, each offering its own breakthrough solutions. This has made Nvidia the most valuable company on the market. OpenAI remains private for now. However, the old guard of IT giants, such as Microsoft and Alphabet, are not standing on the sidelines of the AI race, although they are conducting it in different ways, which is affecting their shares differently.

Microsoft owns a stake in OpenAI, giving it access to the latest developments, but integrates them into its own programmes, including chatbots. For a long time, betting on Microsoft was an indirect bet on OpenAI with their well-known ChatGPT. This approach paid off earlier this year, as the share price recovered faster than many competitors after the April slump. From its lows at the start of April to its highs at the end of July, the stock soared 55%, already making its way to historic highs since the beginning of June.

For a long time, Alphabet shares lagged their competitor in terms of share price growth over the past five years. They were also weaker in their recovery after the April correction, adding 40% to their lows before peaking at the end of July.

However, since August, the markets have clearly shifted into a different mode, with MSFT falling 7.5% against GOOG's 13% growth. This divergence began even before the release of GPT-5, the latest model, which faced widespread criticism from users, forcing the company to revert to GPT-4, originally announced over two years ago. Negative sentiment was also fuelled by comments from OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, who acknowledged that the market is currently in a bubble due to inflated expectations.

Google Gemini is steadily developing and gradually gaining consumer support due to its convenient integration into the company's extensive ecosystem. This aspiration resonates with investors. It seems that market participants are seeking to diversify their bets on AI agents, creating demand for Alphabet shares at the expense of Microsoft.

September marks the end of the financial year in the US, and investors often use August and September to switch to new trends or restart existing ones. September is historically the worst month for stock indices, but it can also be a good entry point during a downturn.

It is only important to understand whether we are seeing the start of a trend reversal or a temporary correction. Signals of this should be sought in MSFT's dynamics. Technically, with the stock trading at $506, it remains within a corrective pattern as long as it stays above the $450–$470 range. The upper bound aligns with last year's peak and the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of the rally from the April lows to the July highs, while the lower boundary corresponds to the 200-day moving average. A break below this level would signal a deeper trend reversal.

GOOG shares are close to local overbought conditions, as the RSI on daily timeframes is approaching 75, near which the shares have experienced local corrections over the past six years. Therefore, there is a high chance that both shares will soon experience increased selling pressure; the only question is how deep this correction will be. (FxPro)

News/Profile

Polymarket

Polymarket is an American cryptocurrency-based prediction market platform that allows users to place bets on the outcomes of real-world events, such as political elections, sports, and economic indicators. It operates on the Polygon blockchain network, using the stablecoin USDC for transactions.

Key Insights

Mechanism: The platform uses binary outcome markets ("Yes" or "No" answers) where users buy shares in an outcome. Share prices fluctuate based on demand, reflecting the market's collective assessment of probability. Winning shares are redeemed for $1.00 after the event concludes.

Transparency: All trades are executed on the blockchain via open-source smart contracts, ensuring transparency and resistance to manipulation by a central authority.

Regulatory Environment: Polymarket has faced significant regulatory scrutiny in various countries, including the U.S., Australia, Portugal, Singapore, France, and Belgium, where it has been blocked or ordered to cease operations due to violations of gambling or derivatives trading laws.

U.S. Re-entry: After a 2022 settlement with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), Polymarket acquired a CFTC-licensed derivatives exchange in July 2025, allowing it to legally operate in the U.S. under regulatory compliance.

Recent News: The company has been in the news recently for a new partnership with the owner of Foxtel, DAZN, despite being banned in Australia. Polymarket also faced criticism in January 2026 for allegedly spreading false information on its official X account, which investor Jeff Bezos publicly denied.

Valuation & Investors: In October 2025, Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) invested up to $2 billion in Polymarket, bringing the company's valuation to $9 billion. Notable investors also include Vitalik Buterin and Peter Thiel's Founders Fund.

Stock/Token Information: Polymarket is a privately held company with no public stock price. The associated cryptocurrency token (POLY) is not the primary mechanism for betting on the platform and its price has experienced extreme volatility.

You can find more information about the platform on the official Polymarket website.

Spotlight

Wynn

Analysts at Goldman Sachs just added Wynn to its conviction buy list. The firm is confident with Wynn’s “Wynn Al Marjan in the UAE in 1Q27, plus WYNN’s best-in-class Las Vegas assets, leverage to a higher-income consumers, a strong 2026 Las Vegas event calendar, and an improving backdrop in Macau should drive transformative upside at WYNN,” as quoted by CNBC.

Nvidia

Analysts at Morgan Stanley just reiterated an overweight rating on Nvidia, with a $250 price target. The firm says NVDA will maintain a dominant market share and that threats are becoming overstated.

“We continue to see NVIDIA maintaining dominant market share, as threats are becoming overstated, though we aren’t sure exactly what will turn sentiment around,” they said, as quoted by CNBC. “Customers’ biggest anxiety for the next 12 months is their ability to procure enough NVIDIA product generally, and Vera Rubin specifically.”

Alphabet

Analysts at Guggenheim say Alphabet could run even higher. The firm raised its price target on GOOG to $375 from $330 with a buy rating.

The firm is confident in Alphabet because of strong cloud backlog growth, which is being supported by enterprise AI demand. It’s also confidence with Google Gemini’s rise as a leading AI platform with rapidly growing adoption metrics, as noted by CNBC.

News

Polymarket Partnership Could Be a Game Changer for TKO Group Holdings (TKO)

Nov 17

(In Case You Missed It)

Polymarket/UFC/TKO

Polymarket recently announced a multi-year partnership making it the Official and Exclusive Prediction Market Partner for UFC and Zuffa Boxing, introducing real-time fan prediction metrics directly into UFC broadcasts and social media experiences.

This collaboration marks the first time major sports organizations have integrated prediction market technology into the live fan experience, creating new ways for audiences to interact and for TKO's sports brands to differentiate themselves.

We'll explore how this innovative fan engagement initiative could strengthen TKO Group Holdings' investment narrative by deepening audience connection and content value.

What Is TKO Group Holdings' Investment Narrative?

To see the value in TKO Group Holdings as a shareholder, you really have to believe in its ability to keep building out top sports entertainment brands like UFC and WWE into global, multi-platform franchises. A lot of the story is about continuing to grow earnings and revenue faster than the overall market, attract loyal audiences, and evolve with changes in media rights, streaming, and digital fan engagement. Recent announcements, such as the multi-year partnership with Polymarket, reflect TKO’s push to unlock new monetization avenues and deepen fan involvement.

However, it’s unlikely this news will materially change the core short-term catalysts, which are still dominated by performance in key international deals, broadcast partnerships, and the rollout of new events. The biggest risks remain legal challenges, compression of media rights value, and questions about board experience. The new fan engagement efforts add differentiation, but don’t directly reduce these core risks for now. On the flip side, investors should pay close attention to ongoing legal proceedings against TKO and UFC.

TKO Group Holdings' shares have been on the rise but are still potentially undervalued by 15%.

Ten retail investors in the Simply Wall St Community produced fair value estimates for TKO ranging from US$63.92 to a very large US$37,618.47. While community views can be widely split, ongoing legal challenges remain a topic several market participants keep circling back to as a concern for TKO’s future performance. Explore these diverse perspectives and see how the risks and opportunities stack up.

News

TKO Group Holdings, Inc. is an American sports and sports entertainment company. Established on September 12, 2023, the public company was formed by a merger between Endeavor subsidiary Zuffa—the parent company of mixed martial arts promotion Ultimate Fighting Championship —and the professional wrestling promotion World Wrestling Entertainment. TKO is led by CEO Ari Emanuel and president Mark Shapiro, both of Endeavor; Dana White and Nick Khan retained their roles as CEOs of UFC and WWE respectively upon the merger, while WWE co-founder Vince McMahon served as executive chairman until resigning from the company in January 2024 amid a sex trafficking scandal. The merger marked the first time that WWE has not been solely and primarily majority-controlled by the McMahon family, which founded the company and owned it for over 70 years. As of 2024, the UFC and WWE were the two most valuable combat sports organizations in the world according to Forbes. UFC was listed as the most valued mixed martial arts company with a revenue of $1.406 billion and WWE being the most valued professional wrestling promotion with a revenue of $1.398 billion in 2023. (Wikipedia)

TKO owns iconic properties including UFC, the world’s premier mixed martial arts organization; WWE, the global leader in sports entertainment; and PBR, the world’s premier bull riding organization. Together, these properties reach 210 countries and territories and organize more than 500 live events year-round, attracting more than three million fans.

TKO also services and partners with major sports rights holders through IMG, an industry-leading global sports marketing agency; and On Location, a global leader in premium experiential hospitality. (Credit: TKO Group)

News Flashback

September 2, 2025

Market regime change: Microsoft weakening whilst Alphabet strengthens Nvidia and OpenAI have become synonymous with the AI revolution, each offering its own breakthrough solutions. This has made Nvidia the most valuable company on the market. OpenAI remains private for now. However, the old guard of IT giants, such as Microsoft and Alphabet, are not standing on the sidelines of the AI race, although they are conducting it in different ways, which is affecting their shares differently.

Microsoft owns a stake in OpenAI, giving it access to the latest developments, but integrates them into its own programmes, including chatbots. For a long time, betting on Microsoft was an indirect bet on OpenAI with their well-known ChatGPT. This approach paid off earlier this year, as the share price recovered faster than many competitors after the April slump. From its lows at the start of April to its highs at the end of July, the stock soared 55%, already making its way to historic highs since the beginning of June.

For a long time, Alphabet shares lagged their competitor in terms of share price growth over the past five years. They were also weaker in their recovery after the April correction, adding 40% to their lows before peaking at the end of July.

However, since August, the markets have clearly shifted into a different mode, with MSFT falling 7.5% against GOOG's 13% growth. This divergence began even before the release of GPT-5, the latest model, which faced widespread criticism from users, forcing the company to revert to GPT-4, originally announced over two years ago. Negative sentiment was also fuelled by comments from OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, who acknowledged that the market is currently in a bubble due to inflated expectations.

Google Gemini is steadily developing and gradually gaining consumer support due to its convenient integration into the company's extensive ecosystem. This aspiration resonates with investors. It seems that market participants are seeking to diversify their bets on AI agents, creating demand for Alphabet shares at the expense of Microsoft.

September marks the end of the financial year in the US, and investors often use August and September to switch to new trends or restart existing ones. September is historically the worst month for stock indices, but it can also be a good entry point during a downturn.

It is only important to understand whether we are seeing the start of a trend reversal or a temporary correction. Signals of this should be sought in MSFT's dynamics. Technically, with the stock trading at $506, it remains within a corrective pattern as long as it stays above the $450–$470 range. The upper bound aligns with last year's peak and the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of the rally from the April lows to the July highs, while the lower boundary corresponds to the 200-day moving average. A break below this level would signal a deeper trend reversal.

GOOG shares are close to local overbought conditions, as the RSI on daily timeframes is approaching 75, near which the shares have experienced local corrections over the past six years. Therefore, there is a high chance that both shares will soon experience increased selling pressure; the only question is how deep this correction will be. (FxPro)

title: The query asks for an analyst-style overview of Elon Musk's projected production and profit figures approximately **5 years from now** (around **2031**, given the current date of March 23, 2026), with
content:
The query asks for an analyst-style overview of Elon Musk's projected production and profit figures approximately **5 years from now** (around **2031**, given the current date of March 23, 2026), with him controlling **X** (formerly Twitter), **SpaceX**, and implicitly **Tesla** (as the core of "Tesla Motors" production focus).

Projections for these companies are highly speculative, driven by ambitious goals in EVs/autonomy/robotics (Tesla), satellite internet/launch/Mars (SpaceX), and platform monetization/AI integration (X). They rely on execution in emerging tech like autonomy, humanoid robots (Optimus), Starlink expansion, and regulatory/political factors. Analyst views range from bullish (e.g., ARK Invest, Wedbush) to cautious (e.g., conservative revenue models).

### Tesla (Primary Production Focus)
Tesla remains Musk's flagship for mass production and profit scaling, shifting toward autonomy (Robotaxi/Cybercab), energy storage, and Optimus humanoid robots.

- **Production Projections**:
  - Vehicle production: Original 20 million vehicles/year goal by 2030 has been de-emphasized (dropped from impact reports), with focus on affordable models, Cybercab ramp (starting ~2026), and slower growth. Analysts expect multi-million annual output by early 2030s, but not necessarily 20M; some models project 3-10M+ units/year by 2030, depending on Robotaxi scaling.
  - Optimus robots: High-volume production ramp expected post-2025 prototypes; some forecasts suggest 1M+ units/year by 2030, contributing to internal/factory use and external sales.

- **Profit/Financial Projections** (around 2030-2031):
  - Revenue: Bullish models ~$400 billion (e.g., analyst YouTube breakdowns), with contributions from vehicles, energy storage (fastest-growing, potentially $10B+ soon), Robotaxi/FSD monetization, and Optimus.
  - Gross profit: ~$140 billion in optimistic scenarios.
  - Operating/EBIT/profit: ~$90 billion EBIT in high-end forecasts; net profit highly variable due to AI/robotics upside.
  - Key drivers: FSD subscriptions/Robotaxi could add significant high-margin revenue; energy storage provides a "valuation floor" with massive margins.
  - Valuation implications: Could support $2-3 trillion+ market cap in bull cases, with some extreme views toward higher if autonomy scales.

Risks include margin compression, competition, and delayed autonomy rollout.

### SpaceX
SpaceX focuses on launch cadence (Starship), Starlink broadband, and Mars ambitions. Musk controls it privately, with potential IPO discussions (~2026 at high valuations).

- **Production/Key Metrics**:
  - Starship launches: Rapid reuse could enable high cadence (daily+ by late decade).
  - Starlink: Subscriber base potentially hundreds of millions; direct-to-cell expansion key.

- **Revenue/Profit Projections** (around 2030):
  - Revenue: ~$200 billion in some ARK models (Starlink dominant); other estimates $65 billion (Morgan Stanley-like), or lower conservative figures (~$20-50B).
  - Profitability: High margins from Starlink (50%+ EBITDA possible); net income could reach $16 billion+ in growth scenarios.
  - Valuation: ARK's expected enterprise value ~$2.5 trillion by 2030 (from ~$350B in late 2024), with bull case ~$3.1T and bear ~$1.7T. Other views range $1-5T+ depending on Starship success.

Starlink cash flow funds Mars/Starship; massive upside if orbital infrastructure scales.

### X (Formerly Twitter/X Corp)
X has stabilized under Musk but remains smaller-scale compared to Tesla/SpaceX.

- **Revenue Projections**:
  - Recent recovery: ~$2.9 billion in 2025 (up ~10-17% YoY after declines), driven by ads rebound and subscriptions (still minor, ~$200M/year).
  - Long-term to 2030: Limited specific projections; growth via payments, video, AI (Grok integration), but historical ad drops make high scaling uncertain. Could reach several billion annually if user/monetization rebounds, but far below Tesla/SpaceX scale.

title:
PART-TIME YOUTUBER ACADEMY BY ALI ABDAAL: THE FRAMEWORK THAT FLIPS HOW YOU MAKE VIDEOS

Ali Abdaal's Part-Time YouTuber Academy (PTYA) Cohort 6 costs $1,995 and delivers 41 lessons on building a YouTu
content:
PART-TIME YOUTUBER ACADEMY BY ALI ABDAAL: THE FRAMEWORK THAT FLIPS HOW YOU MAKE VIDEOS

Ali Abdaal's Part-Time YouTuber Academy (PTYA) Cohort 6 costs $1,995 and delivers 41 lessons on building a YouTube channel as a systematic, part-time operation. It teaches production systems, audience growth frameworks, and the specific sequencing that turns inconsistent creators into consistent publishers. The course is cohort-based, built for beginners and early-stage creators, and anchored by one of the most counterintuitive ideas in YouTube education: you should write your titles before you film anything.

Before dropping $1,995, read the full PTYA breakdown on Course To Action (https://t.co/l1CQQu11UP) — every framework extracted, every gap identified. Course To Action covers 110+ premium courses with full summaries and audio, starting free with 10 summaries and AI credits. No credit card required.

---

The idea that changes everything about how you create:

Most creators follow a natural sequence. Come up with an idea. Film it. Edit it. Then figure out a title and thumbnail at the end.

Ali Abdaal says this is backwards. And he has the data to prove it.

---

THE ITT FRAMEWORK: WHY TITLES COME FIRST

The ITT Framework — Idea, Title, Thumbnail — is the single most counterintuitive and most practically valuable system inside PTYA. It inverts the entire content creation workflow.

Here is the logic. Netflix research shows viewers decide whether to click a thumbnail in under two seconds. Thumbnails and titles account for roughly 80% of a video's performance. The distribution layer — not the content layer — determines whether anyone sees your work at all.

The implication is severe: if you build the video first and title it later, you are spending hours on production before validating whether the concept has any distribution potential.

ITT reverses this. You write titles first. You brainstorm thirty title candidates per week. You evaluate each one with a single question: would I click on this? The titles that generate genuine curiosity become your content calendar. The ones that fall flat get discarded before you waste a single hour filming.

This is not about clickbait. It is about creative discipline. When you write the title first, you are forcing yourself to articulate the value proposition before you invest production time. Ideas that cannot generate a compelling title probably cannot generate a compelling video.

The thumbnail component extends this logic. Each thumbnail is treated as a conversion asset — visual hierarchy, contrast ratios for small-screen readability, cognitive triggers like curiosity gaps and pattern interrupts. Ali teaches thumbnail testing methodology with the same analytical rigor a growth team applies to landing page optimization.

The practical application Ali teaches: sit down on Monday. Write thirty title candidates. Not outlines. Not scripts. Titles. Rank them by click desire. The top five become your production queue for the month. The rest go in a graveyard file you revisit quarterly.

This workflow eliminates one of the most common failure modes for new creators — spending an entire weekend filming a video that was never going to perform because the concept had no distribution hook. ITT catches that failure at the cheapest possible point: before you press record.

There is a deeper implication here that the course does not state explicitly but that becomes obvious once you use the framework. Writing titles first changes what you think about. Instead of asking "what do I know enough about to make a video?" you start asking "what would make someone stop scrolling?" The first question produces content that satisfies the creator. The second produces content that serves the viewer. ITT forces the shift.

Ali also teaches a specific thumbnail methodology as part of the ITT sequence. Thumbnails are not afterthoughts or decorative assets. They are conversion interfaces. The course covers visual hierarchy principles, contrast optimization for mobile screens, and the cognitive triggers — curiosity gaps, social proof signals, emotional expressions, pattern interrupts — that determine whether a viewer's thumb stops or keeps moving. Ali cites the 1.7-second decision window from Netflix research to underscore the stakes: your thumbnail has less than two seconds to earn a click.

For a $1,995 course, ITT alone justifies serious consideration. It changes not just how you produce, but what you produce and in what order.

---

The supporting architecture:

ITT does not operate in isolation. It sits inside a larger system of frameworks that PTYA teaches across its 41 lessons. Here is what surrounds it.

Get Going / Get Good / Get Smart — the three-phase growth model that organizes the entire course. Get Going means publish before you are ready. Get Good means use data to iterate. Get Smart means systematize and delegate. The critical insight is sequencing: most beginners try to Get Smart before they Get Going. This is premature optimization.

The Niche Equation — your niche is not a topic. It is a target audience plus a specific value delivered. This forces every content decision through a specificity filter.

HIVES Framework — the structural template for scripting videos that hold attention. Hook, Intro, Value, End Screen. Each section has a defined job and maps to a specific retention metric.

Proof of Work — a framework for building on-camera credibility before you have audience validation. Surface evidence of your expertise rather than claiming authority.

MILES Framework — an inventory system for discovering what you actually have to offer. Money, Intelligence, Location, Education, Status.

Primal Branding — channel identity through values, rituals, and icons. Not aesthetic decoration. The signals that tell a viewer whether your channel is for them.

Homework for Life — Matthew Dicks' daily storytelling practice. Identify one story-worthy moment each day. Over time, this builds a personal content library and trains your attention to notice material in ordinary experiences.

Refactoring Framework — Eliminate, Remove Friction, Automate, Delegate. The production optimization system that solves "I don't have time to make videos."

Viral Replication Method — systematic reverse-engineering of successful videos in your niche. Not copying. Analyzing structural and psychological decisions, then applying those principles to original material.

---

The honest gaps:

No review is worth reading unless it tells you what is missing. Here is what PTYA does not ship.

Editing instruction is Final Cut Pro only. If you use Premiere Pro or DaVinci Resolve, the step-by-step editing walkthroughs will not match your workflow. You can extract the concepts, but you are doing translation work the course should have done for you. For a $1,995 product, supporting a single editing platform is a real limitation.

Monetization is surveyed, not taught. The course covers AdSense, brand deals, sponsorships, courses, and memberships — but at a conceptual level. There is no deep instruction on how to price a sponsorship, structure a course funnel, negotiate a brand deal, or build recurring revenue. If your primary goal is turning an audience into income, PTYA covers half the journey and leaves you to figure out the other half.

The frameworks assume educational and informational content. Ali built his channel in the productivity and self-improvement space. The examples, case studies, thumbnail breakdowns, and scripting templates all reflect that orientation. Entertainment creators, vloggers, gamers, and personality-driven channels will find the structural principles transferable but the specific applications requiring significant adaptation.

Advanced analytics is absent. No deep YouTube Studio training. No audience retention curve analysis. No A/B testing methodology beyond the thumbnail module. If you already have a publishing history and need to diagnose why growth has stalled at a data level, PTYA does not provide those diagnostic tools.

YouTube Shorts gets surface treatment. If short-form content is your primary growth strategy, the course does not have the depth you need.

---

The $1,995 question — and a different way to answer it:

The price is real. The cohort format — live sessions, community accountability, structured pacing — is what separates PTYA from a video library. If external accountability is what converts your knowledge into published videos, the cohort structure may justify the cost.

But here is the thing. You do not need to spend $1,995 to access the frameworks.

Course To Action (https://t.co/l1CQQu11UP) breaks down every PTYA framework in detail — and it covers 110+ other premium courses the same way. The price: $49 for 30 days, or $399 per year. No auto-renewal. Every summary includes audio so you can listen on your commute. The AI feature "Apply to My Business" lets you take any framework and get a personalized implementation plan — 3 credits free on the free tier.

That is $1,995 for one course versus $49 for access to the extracted frameworks from 110+ courses. The math is not subtle.

Start free. Ten summaries and AI credits. No credit card.

---

The bottom line on ITT:

The ITT Framework is the kind of insight that changes your creative process permanently. Once you internalize "title first, then film," you cannot go back to the old way. Every video idea gets stress-tested for distribution potential before you invest production time.

Whether you learn it through PTYA at $1,995 or through Course To Action's breakdown at $49, the framework itself is worth internalizing. The question is not whether to learn it. The question is how much you want to pay for the delivery mechanism.

Read the full PTYA breakdown — start free on Course To Action (https://t.co/l1CQQu11UP)

title:
Case studies of successful creators

Here are some real-world case studies and examples of creators who've successfully monetized on X (especially highlighting trends from late 2025 into 2026). X decl
content:
Case studies of successful creators

Here are some real-world case studies and examples of creators who've successfully monetized on X (especially highlighting trends from late 2025 into 2026). X declared 2026 the "Year of the Creator," with record payouts in 2025 carrying over—doubling the revenue-sharing pool, heavier weighting for long-form Articles, verified impressions driving earnings, and features like a $1M prize for top Articles.

While exact top-earner names aren't always public (X doesn't release full leaderboards), screenshots, self-reports, and platform updates show clear patterns. Many combine ad revenue sharing (now paying better, often 2–3x previous cycles for mid-tier creators) with subscriptions, digital products, affiliates, and services for real income.

1. MrBeast (Jimmy Donaldson) — Massive Scale Video Test

One of the most high-profile experiments: Uploaded a long-form video directly to X ("$1 vs $100,000,000 Car!") in early 2024, but the model evolved into 2025–2026 payouts.

Result: ~156 million impressions → roughly $263,655 in ad revenue from that single post alone (early data point that influenced higher RPMs).

Strategy: Leveraged existing YouTube fame for cross-platform virality, high-engagement video content.

Key takeaway: Platform-native long videos/Articles now earn more heavily; MrBeast noted X can't yet match YouTube's 9-figure ad revenue but shows huge potential for viral hits.

2026 relevance: With doubled pools and timeline impression weighting, similar high-impact posts could scale even higher.

2. Mid-Tier Creators Seeing 2–3x Payout Spikes (Anonymous/Screenshot Reports)

Many everyday creators (10k–100k followers) reported dashboards showing double or triple previous bi-weekly checks in early 2026—even with flat impressions—after X's revenue pool expansion and Musk's directive for higher rewards.

Examples: One shared frustration at 4.9M impressions with low/no payout pre-change; post-update, similar creators hit $1,200+ per cycle from ~12M impressions (half from Premium users).

Another: Mid-size political commentary accounts averaging $1,200 bi-weekly from consistent threads sparking replies.

Strategy: Focus on verified home timeline impressions (Premium users count more), long threads, and authentic conversation over spam.

Takeaway: 2026 changes made ad revenue feel like "real income" vs. pocket change—many hit eligibility fast (500+ followers, Premium, impressions) and scaled to sustainable side income.

3. Indie Hackers & Niche Builders (e.g., Alex Nguyen @alexcooldev)

Built AI apps organically via X → 370k users, $24k/month recurring (plus another app at $4k/month).

X role: Grew to 45.5k followers in 2025, used threads/shares for distribution (no paid ads), quit 9–5 job.

Monetization mix: Primarily off-platform (app revenue), but X drove traffic/leads + some direct earnings.

Strategy: Value-first content on indie hacking, shipping updates, and distribution lessons → turned passion into full business.

4. Niche Creators Hitting Quick Wins (e.g., Yash @yashhq_22)

Monetized account in just 25 days → earned $1.6k from X so far (likely ad revenue + tips/subscriptions).

Strategy: Built audience fast, now planning to drop a growth playbook (potential digital product/consulting add-on).

Takeaway: Fast eligibility → early payouts possible with consistent, niche-focused posting.

5. Crypto/Web3 Voices (e.g., bigray0x.eth @bigray0x)

10 months of alpha-sharing, teaching, and narratives → built 11k community.

Results: ~$2k+ from X monetization directly + $60k+ airdrops, first paid KOL gig, 100+ subscribers.

Strategy: Threads on insights/alphas → authority → diversified earnings (X revenue + external opportunities).

Common Patterns from Successful 2025–2026 Creators

Platform earnings alone: Top reported ranges $37k–$107k+ annually from ad revenue (for high-engagement accounts), but most mid-tier hit $1k–$5k/month combining features.

content:
90 Days to Your First Paycheck: I Analyzed 136 Cases and Found 10 Real Ways Ordinary People Are Making Money with AI

Most people don’t fail at AI because they can’t use it.
They fail because they don’t know how to turn it into income.

I analyzed 136 real monetization cases, ranging from $400 to $170,000.
By the end of this article, you’ll have 10 actionable paths you can start immediately.

⸻

[Information Gap]

Most people ask: Which AI tool is the best?
People who make money ask: Who doesn’t know this yet?

I saw a college student using ChatGPT to write product listings for cross-border sellers.
He charged $7 per listing and wrote 20 per day.

He couldn’t code. He just spotted demand.

Conclusion: Find the information gap before you pick the tool.

⸻

[Upgraded Ghostwriting]

Writing isn’t disappearing. It’s evolving.

I tested 47 niche accounts.
The fastest-growing ones used AI-assisted content creation.

Someone I tracked charges $120 per viral script.

Conclusion: Sell outcomes, not word count.

⸻

[Editing Acceleration]

Short-form video production is underestimated.

I helped a stay-at-home mom test AI for script generation, voiceover, and subtitles.
She posted 60 videos in 30 days and landed a $700 brand deal.

Conclusion: Speed beats perfection.

⸻

[Prompt Products]

Prompts are the new recipes.

A creator overseas sells Midjourney prompt packs for $29.
He sold 1,200 copies in one month.

I optimized an e-commerce poster prompt for a client.
They came back three times.

Conclusion: Package your experience.

⸻

[Digital Avatars]

I tested AI-powered virtual hosts for live selling.

A small-city merchant ran 8-hour live sessions using a digital avatar.
Monthly cost: under $30.
A real host would cost $2,000+.

Conversion was 10% lower.
Profit was higher.

Conclusion: Do the math, not the emotion.

⸻

[AI Consulting]

90% of people don’t know how to ask better questions.

I helped a law firm redesign its debt consultation workflow using AI.
Customer acquisition cost dropped from $40 to $6 per case.

They weren’t selling AI.
They were selling solutions.

Conclusion: Industry knowledge multiplied by AI creates leverage.

⸻

[Automated Side Income]

I tested automated data aggregation.

Using AI to collect public data and generate niche reports.
Price: $14 each.

First batch sold 37 copies.
Not viral. But validated.

Conclusion: Test small, scale fast.

⸻

[Translation Arbitrage]

Language is a built-in opportunity.

Someone repackaged English podcasts into structured Chinese insights.
Grew to 20,000 subscribers in 3 months.

The content wasn’t original.
The structure was.

Conclusion: Translate + reorganize = new value.

⸻

[Skill Amplification]

AI won’t replace experts.
It will amplify them.

A designer I know generates 10 AI-assisted drafts per project.
Client decision speed improved 4x.

He raised his fee from $400 to $1,100.

Conclusion: Higher efficiency justifies higher pricing.

⸻

[Sell the Path]

Courses don’t sell theory. They sell clarity.

I analyzed a 3-hour AI beginner course.
Price: $29.
Units sold: 2,300.

The content was simple.
The examples were concrete.

Conclusion: Sell the roadmap, not the buzzwords.

⸻

📌 Save This Monetization Framework:
1.Identify a high-frequency demand
2.Use AI to increase efficiency by at least 3x
3.Standardize your service
https://t.co/7QfNKiYvt9 a 30-day small-scale test
5.Scale only after data validation
6.Always sell results

⸻

At first, I thought AI was just a tool.
Now I’m certain it’s a multiplier.

If this helped you, share it with someone still waiting on the sidelines.
In the next piece, I’ll break down how to build your first AI cash-flow loop in 30 days.

content:
How to Pick a Niche That Actually Makes Money

1. Validate demand before creating anything

Most people pick niches based on:
"I'm interested in this"
"This seems popular"
"I saw someone making videos about it"
All terrible reasons.

Your interest doesn't predict demand. Popularity doesn't predict profitability.

The validation checklist:

Test 1: Search volume exists
Go to VidIQ (https://t.co/1I3Im9I6fm). Search your niche topic.
Look for: Monthly search volume 10,000+
If it's under 5,000, demand is too low.

Test 2: Recent videos are succeeding
Filter by "last month." Are videos still getting 50k+ views?
If top videos are all from 2+ years ago, the niche is dying.

Test 3: Small channels can win
Find channels under 20k subs in this niche getting 100k+ views on videos.
If only 500k+ sub channels succeed, barrier to entry is too high.

Pass all three tests? The niche has validated demand.

2. The RPM reality check
Views without revenue is a hobby, not a business.
Different niches have wildly different RPMs:

High RPM ($10-25):
Finance and investing
Business and entrepreneurship
Tech and software tutorials
AI and automation
Real estate

Medium RPM ($4-10):
Self-improvement and productivity
Health and fitness
Education and learning
History and documentaries

Low RPM ($1-4):
General motivation
Entertainment and memes
Gaming compilations
Music and lyrics

The math:

100k monthly views × $15 RPM = $1,500/month

100k monthly views × $2 RPM = $200/month

Same effort. 7.5x revenue difference.

Don't pick low-RPM niches unless you can scale to millions of views.

3. Find the gap, not the crowd

"Saturated" niches aren't the problem. Lack of differentiation is.

The gap-finding process:

Step 1: Pick a broad niche
Example: Personal finance

Step 2: List all sub-niches
Budgeting
Investing
Debt payoff
Side hustles
Retirement planning
Credit cards
Real estate

Step 3: Check saturation for each
Search YouTube. How many channels are focused on each sub-niche?

Budgeting: 200+ channels (saturated)

Investing: 500+ channels (very saturated)

Debt payoff: 80 channels (less saturated)

Retirement for freelancers: 12 channels (gap found)

Step 4: Add your unique angle

"Retirement planning for freelancers" → "Retirement planning for freelancers under 35"

Now you're not competing with 500 investing channels. You're one of 3 channels serving this specific audience.

4. The outlier detection method
This is how you find proven topics before committing to a niche.

Step 1: Find 5 channels in your potential niche

Use VidIQ or manually search. Pick channels with 5k-50k subscribers.

Step 2: Sort each channel by "most popular"

Look for videos that got 5-10x more views than their channel average.

Example:

Channel average: 8k views per video
Outlier video: 120k views

Step 3: Analyze what made the outlier work

Was it the topic?
Was it the angle?
Was it the format?
Was it the timing?

Step 4: Find the pattern

If multiple channels have outliers on similar topics, you found a validated winning topic.

Build your content strategy around these proven topics, not guesses.

5. The audience income test
High views don't matter if your audience has no money.

Audience income indicators:

High-income audience signals:

Content about tools and software (people willing to pay for solutions)
Professional development and career advancement
Investment and wealth building
Business operations and scaling

Low-income audience signals:

Free entertainment and memes
General motivation without actionable strategy
Content targeting teenagers and students
Pure consumption content (no buy intent)

Why this matters:

High-income audience → Better sponsor deals, higher product conversion, premium AdSense rates

Low-income audience → Difficult sponsorships, low product sales, bottom-tier AdSense

Pick niches where your audience has disposable income and intent to spend it.

6. Evergreen vs trending: the balance
Evergreen content: Relevant forever. Compounds over years.

Trending content: Massive views now. Dead in 3 months.

The ideal niche has both:

Evergreen foundation (70%):

"How to start investing in your 20s" — always relevant
"Psychology of habit formation" — timeless
"Basics of rental property analysis" — fundamentals don't change

Trending opportunities (30%):

"New AI tool released this week"
"Fed rate decision impact on stocks"
"Latest algorithm update breakdown"
Evergreen builds your catalog value. Trending gives you viral spikes.

Niches with no evergreen = constant content treadmill.

Niches with no trending = slow, steady growth only.

Best niches have both dimensions.

7. The affiliate opportunity filter
Can you naturally recommend products in this niche?

If no, your only revenue is AdSense. That's limiting.

High affiliate opportunity niches:

Tech (software, tools, hosting)
Finance (platforms, courses, services)
Fitness (supplements, equipment, programs)
Business (SaaS, templates, education)
Productivity (apps, systems, frameworks)

Low affiliate opportunity niches:
General motivation (nothing specific to recommend)
Pure entertainment (no natural product fit)
News commentary (difficult to integrate products)
Before committing to a niche, list 10 products/tools you could naturally recommend.

If you can't hit 10, pick a different niche.

8. Content sustainability check

Can you make 100+ videos on this topic without burning out?

The 100-video test:

Sit down. List 100 specific video topics in your chosen niche.

Not generic ideas. Specific, distinct topics.

If you can't hit 100, the niche is too narrow.

Content depth indicators:

Deep niches (sustainable):

Multiple sub-topics to explore
Ongoing developments and news
Different formats possible (tutorials, analysis, case studies)
Evergreen fundamentals + trending updates

Shallow niches (burnout risk):

Only one way to talk about the topic
No ongoing developments
Limited format variety
Everything covered in 20 videos

You're building a multi-year business. Pick a niche with multi-year content potential.

9. The niche validation scorecard
Before committing, score your niche on these factors (1-10 scale):

Demand validation:

Search volume (10k+ monthly = 10 points)
Recent success (videos from last 30 days doing well = 10 points)
Small channel wins (under 20k subs getting 100k+ views = 10 points)

Monetization potential:

RPM range ($10+ = 10 points, $5-10 = 6 points, under $5 = 3 points)
Affiliate opportunities (10+ natural product fits = 10 points)
Sponsor potential (companies actively marketing to this audience = 10 points)

Sustainability:

Content depth (100+ video ideas = 10 points)
Evergreen percentage (70%+ evergreen = 10 points)
Competition level (gaps exist = 10 points, overcrowded = 4 points)

Scoring:

80-90: Excellent niche, move forward immediately
65-79: Good niche, validate with test videos
50-64: Risky niche, consider alternatives
Under 50: Pick a different niche

content:
Below is the summary of my ebook, titled " AI side hustles"
If you need the complete ebook just comments below 👇 I will send it to you free of charge.
There's a link for the audio book also, I will leave the link below.
Everything free of charge.

Chapter 1: ChatGPT Hustles
The Power of AI in the Creator Economy: Traditionally, online income required complex skills like web design or physical sales, but AI has transformed it into a more accessible writing and productivity partner.  
The ChatGPT Goldmine: ChatGPT is a top tool because it is affordable, has no steep learning curve, and meets a high demand for quality content and scalability.  
YouTube and Podcast Scripting: One primary hustle involves writing scripts or hooks for creators. While AI generates the core structure, adding personal creativity helps the work stand out.  
Resume and LinkedIn Services: AI can assist job seekers by rewriting resumes for specific roles and optimizing LinkedIn profiles for a professional edge.  
Email Marketing and Copywriting: Small businesses need consistent promotional content; AI can draft sequences and newsletters that freelancers then refine for tone.  
Personalized Business Plans: Freelancers can use AI to quickly draft professional documents like investor pitches or SWOT analyses for entrepreneurs.  
Setting Up the Hustle: Success requires identifying a specific niche (like small businesses or job seekers), building a portfolio, and offering tiered pricing packages.  
Real-World Success: A case study of Nina from Kenya shows how a creator earned $1,200/month by using AI to produce scripts quickly while focusing on delivering results for clients.  
The Path Forward: The chapter concludes with a 7-day challenge to launch a ChatGPT-based service, emphasizing that the goal is to work smarter, not harder.  
Chapter 2: AI Content Creation
Leveling the Playing Field: In the past, content creation was time-consuming, but AI tools now allow anyone to produce SEO-optimized blogs and scripts in minutes.  
The Content Boom: The demand for daily content from brands, coupled with the ability for freelancers to scale their output, has made this a high-growth sector.  
SEO Blog Writing: AI can generate outlines and drafts for blogs, which can then be optimized with SEO tools to help them rank on search engines.  
YouTube Script Writing (Revisited): This section details how freelancers can earn over $1,000 monthly by writing scripts and pairing them with AI stock video tools.  
Social Media Planning: Businesses often lack the time for consistent social media; freelancers can fill this gap by selling "done-for-you" content packs.  
Repeatable System: A five-step workflow—Research, Outline, Write, Optimize, and Publish—is recommended to ensure high-quality and efficient production.  
Leo’s Case Study: A writer from the Philippines tripled his income and eventually built a content agency by using AI to increase his daily article output.
  
Chapter 3: Faceless YouTube Channels
The Faceless Revolution: You can now run a successful YouTube channel without a camera or studio by using AI to handle scripting, voiceovers, and editing.  
Low Barriers and High Potential: Faceless channels are popular because they are scalable, have massive earning potential through ads and sponsorships, and require no on-camera presence.  
Workflow Steps: The process involves researching trending niches, writing scripts with ChatGPT, and generating realistic voiceovers with tools like ElevenLabs.  
Visuals and Editing: Stock video platforms and AI editors like Pictory can turn scripts into full videos, while AI design tools create high-contrast thumbnails.  
SEO and Monetization: Beyond ad revenue, channels can be monetized through affiliate marketing, sponsored content, and selling digital products.  
"AI Explained" Case Study: A creator earned $3,800 monthly within months by focusing on value-driven storytelling and consistent AI-assisted uploads.

title:
Humans deserve to be judged by HUMANS. 🚨 An open letter to @YouTube and @TeamYouTube
I am a 3D artist, not a bot. But for the past few months, my livelihood and mental health have been held hostage by
content:
Humans deserve to be judged by HUMANS. 🚨 An open letter to @YouTube and @TeamYouTube
I am a 3D artist, not a bot. But for the past few months, my livelihood and mental health have been held hostage by a broken automated loop. It is time to speak up about how AI-driven decisions are destroying creators.

Here is the "Death Loop" of my studio:

1. Baseless Accusations vs. Creative Freedom: YouTube’s own policies state: YouTube is a place for creators to share their unique expressions with artistic context .  My three channels were demonetized with zero specific evidence. If a video is deemed unsuitable for children, YouTube has the right to age-restrict or remove specific content, but not to groundlessly accuse  an entire channel.

2. The 0.001s Rejection: My first appeals were rejected within hours without any human arguments or explanation of which rule was allegedly broken.

3. The Policy Paradox: I was denied re-monetization simply because "other channels are banned," while those channels are banned because they are linked to this one. A perfect automated trap.

4. The Ghost Review: My final appeal video, showing my 3D production process in Blender, has 0 VIEWS in analytics. They rejected me without even clicking "Play."

5. Deliberate silence: @TeamYouTube Thank you for opening a private chat, but that's all there is to it. My responses to customer support messages remain unread for over 24 hours after they explicitly requested my data. This seems like a deliberate delay to create the illusion of a "deep review," while in reality, the case is simply gathering dust until the next AI decision or a formal refusal without specifying specific reasons or specific areas of violation.

Our Right as Humans:
We have a fundamental right to human communication and fair treatment under the Digital Services Act (DSA). Being judged by an algorithm that doesn't even view the evidence is a violation of creator rights.

This situation has caused months of extreme stress and a total loss of income. If this automated loop is not broken by a real human specialist, this becomes a matter for the European Court of Human Rights. We will be forced to join forces and file a class action lawsuit to seek compensation for lost profits, moral damages, and harm to my health, as determined by the European Court of Human Rights.

@TeamYouTube, @YouTube, @YouTubeCreators — I am not just a Channel ID. I am a person. I demand a MANUAL REVIEW by a human specialist.

#YouTubeSupport #HumanRights #CreatorEconomy #CGI #Blender3D #AppealLoop #DSA

FINAL SCORE: 73/100

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
🚙 Under the Hood 🛠️

Look, Amazon is firing on all cylinders operationally — AWS at 24% growth, advertising printing money at 22%, and the whole machine generating $80B in operating income. The problem? Management just committed to spending $200B on capex in 2026, a 56% jump that sent the stock into a 9-day losing streak not seen since 2006. With a 1.385 beta and the stock sitting 23% below its ATH, this is either a generational entry or a value trap disguised as AI ambition. I'd size this at 0.5-1.5% max and layer in on any dip toward $180-185 — the FTC antitrust trial in October 2026 adds another wildcard. If you have a 12+ month horizon and can stomach the capex drag on FCF, this is a reasonable entry for a world-class compounder.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

INVESTMENT THESIS:

Amazon enters 2026 as a diversified technology platform with accelerating cloud growth (AWS +24% YoY), a rapidly scaling advertising business ($85B+ annual run rate), and expanding retail margins from logistics automation and AI integration. The core thesis is that $200B in planned capex will position Amazon to dominate enterprise AI infrastructure through custom silicon (Trainium/Graviton at $10B+ run rate), while the FTC antitrust trial in October 2026 and near-term FCF compression create uncertainty that has pushed the stock to historically low valuations.

ANALYST PERSPECTIVE / RISK-REWARD DEEP DIVE:

The risk-reward setup at $198.79 is unusual for Amazon — the stock is trading at a forward P/E of 21.4x against projected 15-20% annual EPS growth, a PEG ratio around 1.3-1.5, and sits at EV/EBIT multiples near decade lows. The catch is the $200B capex commitment, which will reduce FCF from an already-declining $11.2B (TTM per Amazon's earnings report) and won't generate measurable returns for 12-24 months. AWS backlog growth of 40% YoY to $244B supports the investment thesis, but the market is right to question whether this cycle's infrastructure spending will produce better returns than prior cycles. The FTC trial, while unlikely to result in a breakup, could impose structural remedies on marketplace operations.

MOAT / EDGE:

Amazon possesses one of the widest moats in global technology through interlocking network effects (marketplace flywheel of buyers-sellers-logistics), massive switching costs (AWS enterprise contracts, Prime ecosystem with 315M+ ad-supported viewers), and cost advantages from scale (America's lowest-price online retailer for 9 consecutive years). The AWS platform benefits from efficient scale and an expanding ecosystem of services that make migration costly for enterprises. The moat is wide and self-reinforcing, though regulatory action represents the primary threat to its durability.

title:
⚠️ Attention creators!!! 🚫 Shock & devastation!

‼️The mass wave of AI bans is destroying creators’ lives – channels, if restored, come back with shadowbans and almost no earnings, creators lose their
content:
⚠️ Attention creators!!! 🚫 Shock & devastation!

‼️The mass wave of AI bans is destroying creators’ lives – channels, if restored, come back with shadowbans and almost no earnings, creators lose their entire creative work and finances, and face serious mental and health problems due to this utter injustice and total lack of transparency.

As a woman and single mom, I feel cheated and robbed by Google/YouTube. My hard-earned income, the security of our family, and my son’s future have been completely ignored.

My story breaks my heart every day:

An unjust ban took everything from me. After months of appeals, my channel and monetization were finally restored… but my earnings from June 2025 (~$4,870 USD / 20,000 PLN) are still withheld after 8 months.

YouTube claims: “sent to advertisers,” “wait 50 days,” “apply again” – but they provide no evidence, invoices, or documentation. Shadowban destroyed my reach – from my main (and only) source of income, I dropped to a few cents per month.

@YouTube @TeamYouTube @YouTubeCreators  ‼️

This was my only source of livelihood. I ran a registered business, working 12-hour days: editing, video processing, sourcing materials – all of it wiped out. YouTube repeatedly refused to cooperate, refused to provide explanations, and blocked all meaningful discussion.
 Then they claim I didn’t appeal successfully – despite months of denial of collaboration and evidence.

Please – someone with a real name and a heart – check my case and release my earnings. I beg for simple justice. 🙏

#YouTubeScam #WithheldEarnings #CreatorRights #SingleMom #Shadowban #AIAbuse #FairPay #YouTubeBanWave
