content:
This one post is all you need to read if you're new or thinking of starting youtube automation (faceless channel) 

This is a long post so bookmark and repost🔖

youtube has completely changed in the past year

and if you're trying to grow a faceless channel, you need to understand what actually works now

what worked 2 years ago:
copy viral video
similar title
similar thumbnail
upload
profit

what works now:
everyone is copying viral videos
competition is 10x harder
algorithm is smarter
viewers are pickier
you need a system

the skill gap tripled

everyone has access to:

ai scriptwriting (chatgpt/claude)
ai editing tools (Veo3 etc)
thumbnail templates (canva)

this means:
average video quality is way higher
"just good enough" doesn't cut it anymore
you need to be top 10% to get views

where you actually win:
better research (choosing what to make)
better packaging (thumbnail + title)
better execution (retention tactics)

so do this:

step 1: keyword circle research

build a systematic research process

for faceless finance channel example:
core keywords:
how to save money
passive income ideas
investing for beginners
budget strategies
side hustle ideas
debt payoff methods
credit score improvement

the research process:
search each keyword on youtube
filter by "this year"
note videos with 100k+ views from channels under 100k subs
these are your proven ideas

what to analyze:

performance indicators:

editing style (fast cuts? text overlays? pacing?)
examples used (real stories? case studies? data?)
video length (sweet spot for this topic?)
packaging clarity (thumbnail + title immediately clear?)
information density (too much? too little?)

improvement opportunities:

comment section requests 
missing depth
missing simplicity
implementation gaps 

step 2: pattern recognition across winners

analyze 20-30 in your niche

look for patterns:

title formulas that repeat:
"how to [result] in [timeframe]"
"[number] ways to [benefit]"
"why [surprising claim]"
"[thing] explained (finally)"

thumbnail styles that work:
bold text + simple image
before/after splits
number callouts
color schemes that dominate

content structures:
intro length (30 sec? 2 min?)
how many main points (3? 5? 7?)
example frequency (one per point?)
cta placement (middle? end?)

the content gap strategy
this is where you actually win
the framework:

identify winning topic (research from step 1)
find most popular videos on that topic
read ALL comments (looking for gaps)
note repeated requests/questions
create video that fills those gaps

example:
popular video: "how to start a youtube channel"

500k views
15 minutes
explains basics well

comments say:

"but what do i actually film first?"
"how do i get over camera anxiety?"
"what if i have no ideas?"
"what equipment do i actually need?"

your video opportunity:
"what to film for your first 10 youtube videos"
or
"youtube for camera-shy people (complete guide)"
or
"the $0 equipment guide for youtube"
you're not competing with the popular video
you're filling the gaps it left
this works because:
audience already proved interest in topic
you're serving them at different stage
or solving problem original video missed

the execution advantage
research and targeting get you the idea
execution determines if it works
the new execution standards:
editing:

scene change every 5-8 seconds minimum
text on screen every 10-15 seconds
music bed throughout (low volume)
pattern interrupts every 2 minutes
no dead air or slow moments

scripting:

hook in first 10 seconds validates title
roadmap within first 30 seconds
clear structure with 3-5 main points
specific examples for every point
strong call to action

retention tactics:

curiosity loops ("more on this in a minute")
callbacks to earlier points
visual payoffs (show don't just tell)
strategic reveals (save best for 70% mark)

packaging:

thumbnail passes mobile test (readable at small size)
title creates curiosity gap
both align perfectly with hook
clear value proposition

the competitive moat
everyone can copy one video
not everyone can systematize this
your moat is:

comprehensive research system

keyword tracking spreadsheet
competitor analysis database
gap identification process
audience feedback collection

content production system

proven script templates
editing checklist
quality control process
batch creation workflow

testing and iteration

thumbnail a/b testing
title variation testing
hook testing
data-driven improvements

audience relationship

comment engagement
community building
consistent delivery

you must:

compete on content quality alone
nail packaging 
deliver exceptional value consistently
systemize everything

your action plan
this week:
day 1: choose your niche
day 2: keyword research (create list of 10)
day 3: analyze top 30 videos
day 4: identify 5 gaps you can fill
day 5: create audience avatar
day 6-7: make first video

this month:
upload 8-10 videos
test different angles
track all data
refine based on results

this quarter:
30-40 videos uploaded
patterns identified
system refined
monetization in sight

this year:
100+ videos
monetized channel
proven formulas
scalable business
author: ytmanifesto

title:
Amazon Investment Case: $310+ Target

As previously mentioned, Amazon has been on our watchlist throughout 2025. We believe it will be an interesting play in 2026.

Today we share our investment thesi
content:
Amazon Investment Case: $310+ Target

As previously mentioned, Amazon has been on our watchlist throughout 2025. We believe it will be an interesting play in 2026.

Today we share our investment thesis for $AMZN at $231 (P/E 32.7).

Not a bargain, but a quality compounder at a fair price, entering its critical $125B CapEx inflection for AI dominance.

The Three Flywheels  
☑️ Retail: $147B revenue, 6-7% margins, 62% third-party mix  
☑️ AWS: $132B run-rate, 34.6% margins, generates 66% of operating profit on 18% of revenue  
☑️ Ads + Prime: $62B combined, 20%+ margins  

Each funds the others.

Valuation Tell  
☑️ P/E 32.7 current, 28.6 forward, 57.4 historical average  
☑️ Trading 43% below historical despite higher growth = market discounting CapEx execution risk  

If executed: 15-18% annual returns via multiple expansion + earnings growth.

AWS: The Profit Engine  
☑️ Q3: $33.0B revenue, 34.6% margin, $11.4B operating income  
☑️ Backlog: $200B (18 months revenue visibility)  
☑️ Supply-constrained, not demand-constrained  
☑️ Funding entire $125B AI expansion while maintaining $14.8B FCF, expanding to Mexico/Thailand/India/Middle East, building custom chips (40-60% cost advantage vs $NVDA)

Competitive Moats  
☑️ Network effects: 200M+ Prime locked-in  
☑️ Switching costs: Deep AWS integration + custom chips  
☑️ Breadth: 200+ services, years ahead of competitors  
☑️ Azure grows faster (26% vs 20%) but AWS adds $26B annually, more than GCP's total revenue

Advertising Upside  
☑️ $17.7B Q3 (+23.5% YoY)  
☑️ 20%+ margins  
☑️ 300M+ ad-supported users  
☑️ TAM: Google $280B, Meta $120B  

Amazon monetizing less despite being closest to "purchase intent." 5-10x runway.

Prime Ecosystem  
☑️ 200M+ subscribers  
☑️ $44.4B annual (+10.4%)  
☑️ 22% streaming share  

Video + Music + Reading + Shipping = ecosystem lock-in. $400B+ hidden business.

Regulatory Path  
☑️ $2.5B FTC settlement paid (Q3)  
☑️ Antitrust trial October 2026  
☑️ Worst case: AWS separation  

Upside: Standalone AWS trades 8-10x EV/Revenue vs consolidated 5.5x.  
Market may be undervaluing breakup scenario.

Capital Allocation Signal  
☑️ Could maximize profits by cutting CapEx  
☑️ Instead: $125B 2025, higher 2026. Why? Belief in 10-year AI demand + scarcity = pricing power  
☑️ +38-40% AWS margins by 2027. Binary bet  
☑️ Jassy's comp: $29.2M (2023) → $40.1M (2024) on 40% stock appreciation. Incentives aligned.

Valuation Scenarios  
✔️ Base Case $310–$330: EPS $7.11 (2025) → $9–10 (2027), 14.2% CAGR, 30-32x multiple. 34% upside  
🟢 Bull Case $420+: AWS margins 38-40% → EPS $11–12 → 35-40x multiple. Probability 40%  
🔴 Bear Case $155–$175: AI plateau, regulation breakup. Probability 10%  

Risk/Reward: 2.5:1 favorable.

Competitive Reality  
✔️ AWS 30% ($132B, 34.6% margin)  
✔️ Azure 20% ($40.9B, 25-28%)  
✔️ GCP 13% ($30B, 20-22%)  
✔️ Market 18% CAGR  
✔️ 92% use multi-cloud  
✔️ AWS backlog protects revenue 18 months even if bookings collapse

Risks  
❗️ AI CapEx uneconomic: capacity > demand, pricing pressure  
❗️ Azure catches up  
❗️ Antitrust breakup forces separation  
❗️ Macro recession hits enterprise + advertising  
❗️ China tariff escalation on US tech

The Thesis: Not a bargain, but a compound machine.  

Three integrated businesses at different growth stages:  
- Retail = moat factory  
- AWS = profit engine  
- Ads = monetization play  

Each de-risks the others.  

If $125B CapEx delivers and AI scarcity persists through 2027, this compounds wealth not by timing knives, but by owning the knife factory.

BUY | 18-24 Month Horizon  
Target: 
- $310–$330 Base  
- $420+ Bull  

Entry: <$220  

Key metrics: AWS margins, ad RPM, CapEx efficiency, FCF stability  

Catalysts: Q4 holiday sales, Q1 AWS backlog conversion, H2 antitrust news, 2027 pricing power visibility
author: Achilles_Cap

title: $ORCL $CRWV @RealJimChanos Executive Summary: Jim Chanos argued that the AI infrastructure buildout has embedded a materially higher fundamental and financing risk profile than is generally discounted
content:
$ORCL $CRWV @RealJimChanos Executive Summary: Jim Chanos argued that the AI infrastructure buildout has embedded a materially higher fundamental and financing risk profile than is generally discounted in equity prices, with the highest vulnerability concentrated in business models that monetize AI primarily by owning or hosting compute and power rather than by monetizing AI output. The interview’s central thesis asserted that “hosting GPUs is inherently a low margin, low return on capital business” and that “the magic and the money is going to come from what the chips produce ultimately, not where they reside,” implying structurally weak economics for legacy data-center REITs, “neoclouds,” and bitcoin miners repositioning into GPU hosting. Oracle was identified as the most exposed large-cap hyperscaler because the interview characterized Oracle’s current AI-driven balance-sheet expansion as outpacing its cash generation and value creation, creating a “bet your company bet” if AI monetization timing slips beyond 2027-2028; the interview further argued that a demand shock driven by funding constraints at unprofitable AI model companies could rapidly cascade into reduced infrastructure spend. Several key numerical and event assertions in the interview are corroborated by public reporting: Oracle disclosed remaining performance obligations rising from $138B at FY2025 Q4 to $455B at FY2026 Q1, and multiple reports describe Oracle’s trailing free cash flow turning materially negative in the same period, consistent with the interview’s concern about cash conversion pressure. Microsoft’s extension of server and network equipment useful lives to 6 years is also corroborated, relevant to the interview’s emphasis on depreciation assumptions as a core swing factor in GPU/compute economics. A reported 28% year-over-year decline in a Hopper GPU rental-price index provides partial external support for the interview’s contention that GPU rental rates are already exhibiting deflation consistent with shorter economic lives and intensifying supply. Meta’s use of a Blue Owl-financed joint venture structure for a Louisiana data-center project supports the interview’s claim that hyperscalers are increasingly exploring off-balance-sheet or partner-capital approaches for infrastructure funding. Several other statements were either explicitly opinion-based (forward monetization timing, relative “worseness” vs 1999-2000) or not directly corroborated from accessible primary disclosures in the cited sources (specific incremental return calculations such as Oracle at 8.5% vs Microsoft near 40%, “20% vacancy rates” for Digital Realty/Equinix, and specific contract-level return claims for a named bitcoin-miner-to-datacenter deal), and should be treated as thesis assertions rather than established facts.

The interview framed the AI infrastructure complex as a 3-layer stack: hyperscalers monetizing workloads, semiconductor/platform vendors supplying the buildout, and owners/operators hosting the compute. Chanos’s skepticism was positioned primarily on the 3rd layer and “2nd or 3rd derivatives” of the AI buildout: legacy colocation/data-center REITs and newer “neocloud” intermediaries described as landlords and GPU lessors. Chanos stated that legacy data-center businesses are “a very low return on capital business, highly capital intensive,” asserted that data-center companies “capitalize a lot of things that we believe should be expensed,” and described them as “negative free cash flow,” with the short thesis dating to mid-2022. The interview’s core economic critique was that infrastructure ownership and GPU hosting are commodity activities in a supply-driven market, with limited durable pricing power once capital floods in, and with returns highly sensitive to depreciation lives and refresh cycles.

Chanos’s differentiation between hyperscalers and infrastructure landlords was explicit. The interview noted that short exposure is typically hedged with index longs, and that large index weights can create an “in effect” long exposure to mega-cap AI winners while targeting weaker, more levered, or structurally lower-return plays. Within mega-cap hyperscalers, Oracle was singled out. Chanos characterized Oracle as a “hybrid” hyperscaler with a profitable legacy software base but comparatively weaker incremental economics and cash flow versus peers funding massive AI capex. The interview asserted that among the 5 hyperscalers named (Microsoft, Meta, Google, Oracle, Amazon), Oracle and Amazon were described as not earning their incremental cost of capital, while Microsoft and Meta were described as earning above cost of capital on incremental capital spend. These relative ROIC statements were presented as the product of Chanos’s internal analytics rather than company-reported metrics, and direct corroboration of the exact computed percentages is not available from the cited public sources; however, the broader claim that Oracle’s incremental AI/cloud expansion is driving material cash flow pressure aligns directionally with reports describing Oracle’s trailing free cash flow turning negative.

A quantitative anchor for Oracle risk in the interview was the claim that Oracle is already free cash flow negative and that extended delays in AI monetization could create “fundamental financial problems,” potentially forcing reconsideration of capital structure and even equity issuance to protect the balance sheet. External reporting describes Oracle’s trailing 12-month free cash flow shifting to approximately negative $13.18B versus positive $11.27B in the prior year, consistent with the interview’s observation that cash generation has deteriorated while capex ramps. The interview’s framing emphasized path dependency: if AI output monetization inflects in 2027-2028, the buildout may be financeable; if pushed to 2030 or beyond, leverage and balance-sheet expansion could become destabilizing for comparatively lower-cash-flow operators.

The interview also addressed Oracle’s remaining performance obligations as a key market narrative. The interviewer cited a surge in Oracle’s RPO from $137B to $455B as a catalyst for prior equity strength, and Chanos responded that RPO is “not a GAAP metric,” “not a real backlog,” and should be treated as conditional (“if everything goes right”) rather than as an order book. Oracle’s disclosures confirm the magnitude and timing of the RPO step-change: Oracle reported RPO up 41% to $138B in FY2025 Q4 and later reported RPO of $455B in FY2026 Q1, up 359% year-over-year, matching the interview’s numerical comparison. The interview’s skepticism toward RPO as a valuation anchor remains an analytical judgment rather than a fact claim; however, describing RPO as non-GAAP is consistent with how the metric is typically presented by software and cloud vendors.

A central macro/credit assertion was that AI infrastructure demand is increasingly downstream of unprofitable AI application/model companies, and that “the unprofitable companies as a percent of the spend is higher in this cycle than it was in the telecom cycle.” The interview argued that as long as these entities “can keep raising money to keep paying those bills,” spend persists, but that a credit crunch or “2001-2002 pullback in sentiment” could cause spending to “drop pretty quickly.” This is fundamentally a causal and compositional claim about end-demand rather than a single-point fact, and the interview did not provide a dataset decomposing hyperscaler capex into end-customer profitability buckets. The plausibility of the “unprofitable AI demand” channel is directionally consistent with the interview’s own examples and with contemporaneous reporting highlighting very large external funding needs for leading AI model developers. HSBC analysis reported by major publications has projected large cumulative funding shortfalls and deep losses for OpenAI through 2030 under certain modeling assumptions, which supports the interview’s broader point that leading AI model companies may remain heavily cash consumptive for years even under aggressive revenue growth assumptions.

The interview discussed OpenAI primarily as an example of the center-of-gravity for AI infrastructure spend rather than as an immediate short candidate, with Chanos stating that a view on OpenAI’s long-term profitability is not required to be skeptical about lower-return infrastructure intermediaries. Claims about OpenAI’s fundraising environment and scale were referenced indirectly through the interviewer’s mention of “HSBC forecasts” and the practical question of how such businesses sustain capex and operating losses. A contemporaneous major-news report confirmed a $1B Disney investment in OpenAI as part of a partnership that includes licensing Disney characters for OpenAI’s Sora video tool, consistent with the interview’s point that extremely large checks are being written to fund leading model platforms. The existence of substantial new strategic capital does not resolve the interview’s central risk framing, which was about the stability of the funding regime under adverse market conditions.

On “neoclouds” and bitcoin miners pivoting to AI infrastructure, the interview’s argument was that these entities are “landlords,” not differentiated technology franchises, and that the market’s implied scarcity premium for land and power is overstated because returns observed in announced deals are not monopoly-like. Chanos cited a specific Microsoft-related deal for a named bitcoin-miner-to-datacenter entity and described the economics as “mid single-digit return on capital.” The interview did not provide deal documents, and no corroborating primary source for that specific deal-level return was identified in the cited materials; accordingly, the specific “mid single-digit” return figure remains unverified. The broader claim that data-center capacity is being built rapidly by many entrants is consistent with widely observed capex acceleration across the sector, but the interview’s strongest, testable claims in this segment were about GPU rental price deflation and depreciation life sensitivity rather than any single contract.

Depreciation and economic life of GPUs were treated as the pivotal micro variable for GPU lessors and “GPU middlemen.” Chanos’s modeling assumption was a 5-year useful life with 20% residual value, while noting market debate around 3-6 years and the asymmetry of outcomes: if lives are 2-3 years, many business models fail; if lives are 8-12 years, lessors benefit disproportionately. The interview claimed hyperscalers have “increased” depreciation assumptions “from 5 years to 6 years,” and that Microsoft uses 6 years. Public reporting confirms that Microsoft extended the estimated useful life of server and network equipment from 4 years to 6 years in 2022, reducing depreciation expense; this substantiates the direction (life extension to 6 years) but suggests the interview’s “5 to 6” characterization is imprecise on the starting point for Microsoft specifically. The interview also cited a market “spot rental” check, describing the Bloomberg Hopper GPU rental index as down 28% year-over-year; a Bloomberg opinion column referenced a 28% year-over-year decline in Hopper rental prices, consistent with the interview’s directional claim that rental economics are already deteriorating for older-generation GPUs.

The interview’s inference from rental-price deflation was that realized market pricing is broadly consistent with a 4-6 year economic life rather than an 8-12 year life, and that claims of post-contract re-renting at 90%-95% of prior deal rates are likely cherry-picked. These are interpretive conclusions, but they are logically consistent with the mechanism described: rapid architectural iteration (Hopper to Blackwell and beyond) can erode willingness to pay for older accelerators and shift usage to lower-value inference workloads, pushing down rents even if physical life remains longer under lighter utilization. The interview’s emphasis on “free market” clearing prices as the ultimate arbiter is methodologically coherent; however, the specific forecast that a decisive rental-rate signal will emerge “within a year or 2” is inherently forward-looking and not fact-checkable at present.

Legacy data-center REITs were addressed with a mix of economic critique and accounting critique. Chanos described Digital Realty and Equinix as primarily colocation/real-estate providers and argued that stated returns appear low even under long depreciable lives of 15-20 years. Chanos then asserted that cash flow statements reveal “massive amounts of capex” that are labeled as “growth capex” rather than recurring maintenance, and argued that this classification is economically semantic because failures in systems like HVAC require replacement regardless of accounting label. This is an argument about maintenance intensity and accounting presentation rather than a discrete fact claim. Digital Realty’s own disclosures in a quarterly results release illustrate that capex and funding needs are sizable relative to operating cash flow proxies: for 2025 guidance, Digital Realty indicated adjusted EBITDA of $3.200B-$3.300B and development capex net of partner contributions of $3.000B-$3.500B, plus recurring capex and capitalized leasing costs of $320M-$335M, while also disclosing common equity issuance via an at-the-market program. These figures support the interview’s directional point that the model is capital intensive and frequently paired with external financing, even if they do not independently prove “capex greater than EBITDA” in every period under every definition.

Chanos asserted that these REITs have “20% vacancy rates” and are “growing revenues mid single digits.” The “20% vacancy” figure could not be corroborated from the accessible primary disclosures cited above because the referenced press release materials emphasize year-end occupancy change in basis points rather than disclosing a single portfolio vacancy percentage in the extracted sections, and because some supplemental files appear access-restricted. In contrast, the same press materials and conference-call transcript excerpts emphasize record commencements, low churn, and a large signed-but-not-commenced backlog, which can coexist with meaningful vacancy depending on the development pipeline and product mix. The interview’s specific vacancy percentage should be treated as unverified absent a directly cited occupancy statistic from company supplements or filings.

The interview stated that hyperscalers increasingly seek to move infrastructure assets off-balance-sheet, leasing from third parties and shifting capital intensity risk. A concrete example was provided involving Meta and a financing partner. Meta subsequently announced a joint venture with Blue Owl Capital for a multi-gigawatt “Hyperion” data-center campus in Richland Parish, Louisiana, explicitly framing the structure as a way to create capacity with partner capital, consistent with the interview’s observation that alternative capital structures are being used for large-scale data-center development.

Funding mechanics for neoclouds and miners were discussed through the lens of convertible bonds. Chanos asserted that market commentary often mischaracterizes “1% convert” issuance as “free capital” by focusing on coupon rather than the embedded option value and dilution. The explanation that a convertible embeds both a low-coupon bond and an equity call option is technically correct as a general description of convert structure and pricing. The interview also cited a specific case in which a company allegedly paid “3x” to retire previously issued converts, implying high effective cost; the specific example was not identified with enough clarity to validate in the cited sources, so the “3x” statement remains unverified in this summary.

The interview’s historical analogies focused on the 1999-2001 telecom and dot-com capex cycle, emphasizing that market prices can decline materially before reported fundamentals deteriorate and before order cancellations are visible in earnings. Chanos cited research by Andrew Odlyzko at the University of Minnesota, arguing that prevailing beliefs about internet traffic “doubling every 3 months” were quantitatively inconsistent and that traffic growth was closer to “doubling every year,” which would have implied dramatic over-ordering if capex plans were built on the higher growth assumption. Odlyzko’s work explicitly critiques the “doubling every three months” narrative and discusses the gap between mythic growth rates and more realistic annual doubling behavior, supporting the interview’s reference to this line of argument. The interview’s broader inference was that AI capex could similarly overshoot if demand growth expectations prove too optimistic relative to supply additions, and that capex can be paused abruptly (“we don’t need 8 data centers, we just need 3”) in a way that directly transmits into earnings volatility.

Chanos asserted that the current cycle is “worse than 1999-2000” because a larger share of effective end-demand is driven by unprofitable entities rather than by profitable Fortune 500 corporates and incumbent telcos. The interview also asserted that the combined capex of the 2 “core unprofitable entities” of the telecom bubble (fiber optic companies and competitive local exchange carriers) was about $100B from 1997-2001, or roughly $20B per year. Those figures were presented from lived market experience rather than cited sources in the interview, and no directly corroborating dataset was identified in the cited materials; the numeric comparison should be treated as unverified. The directional message that profitable incumbents drove much of the telecom buildout and later cut orders is broadly consistent with mainstream telecom-bubble narratives, but the interview’s specific decomposition and dollar totals require external historical capex aggregation to validate precisely.

The interview also discussed claims that LLM adoption is growing faster than prior technology waves. Chanos’s response emphasized base effects and the increasing difficulty of sustaining extreme growth rates once revenue scales to tens of billions. This is a generally valid growth arithmetic observation, but the interview did not present a specific dataset for LLM adoption, and therefore it remains an analytical framing rather than a verifiable claim.

On Nvidia, Chanos characterized the company as having “a good business” and a current “cash machine,” while expressing discomfort with the concept of vendor financing, drawing an analogy to Lucent and Nortel practices during the telecom bubble. The interview asserted that Nvidia’s vendor financing is “so far not material,” but also questioned why it exists if demand is as strong as management indicates. No cited source in this summary directly quantifies Nvidia vendor financing, so the “not material” assessment remains unverified here; however, the cautionary analogy highlights a historically recognizable pattern in capex bubbles where suppliers extend credit to pull forward demand.

The interview’s segment on private equity and private credit broadened the systemic risk framing beyond AI infrastructure. Private equity was characterized as “leveraged equity,” with returns that were described as not commensurate with leverage and with valuation marks that can mute perceived volatility. These are evaluative statements and depend on benchmark selection, leverage levels, and fee assumptions; no specific dataset was cited in the interview. The private credit critique was more detailed and historical: the interview argued that selling “equity rates of return” on “senior secured paper” resembles earlier junk-bond narratives promoted by Michael Milken, and that such narratives can rely on misleading historical return attribution (fallen angels vs new issuance) and denominator effects in default-rate reporting. These points align with well-known critiques in credit history and cohort default analysis, but the interview did not cite specific studies beyond referencing Edward Altman’s work conceptually; therefore, the summary treats these as analytical arguments rather than as newly established facts within the source material.

A specific structural risk raised in the private credit section was the ownership of regulated insurance entities by large private-credit sponsors and the potential for annuity liabilities to become indirectly exposed to aggressive credit strategies, framed as an “echo” of historical networks of regulated balance sheets absorbing risk assets. The interview referenced Apollo and Athene as an example and described the leverage math whereby “90% investment grade” can still imply that a large share of equity capital is exposed depending on statutory leverage. This is a capital structure arithmetic point and is directionally correct as a general principle, but the interview did not provide statutory capital ratios or asset compositions for specific insurers in the discussion.

The interview compared the current speculative tone to late 2021, noting meme stocks, NFTs, crypto rallies, and SPAC issuance, and then suggested that segments of 2025 exhibited similar behavior in AI, nuclear, and quantum equities, as well as “crypto treasury companies.” The interview asserted that the current cycle has had less public issuance than 2021 because more financing is occurring in private markets, creating a “catch 22” where public markets might clear at lower valuations than private marks. These statements are plausibly consistent with the growth of private capital markets over the past decade, but the interview did not cite issuance statistics, and no corroborating issuance dataset was retrieved in the cited sources; the claims remain qualitative.

The interview concluded by noting ongoing interest in idiosyncratic “accounting stories” beyond AI, including Live Nation and a critique of Erie-related insurance accounting. The Erie example asserted a structure in which an insurance entity is effectively “fobbed off” and the public company recharacterizes economics as service income with a 25% fee on premiums, with governance and operational substance described as non-arm’s-length. These are specific allegations that would require direct review of Erie’s statutory structure, related-party agreements, and filings to validate; such validation was not performed in the cited sources, so the claim remains unverified in this summary and should be treated as a thesis description rather than an established fact.
author: TheValueist

title:
Why $AMZN - Amazon Has Been the Laggard of the Magnificent 7 in 2025 📉

The Magnificent 7 (Apple, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, Nvidia, Tesla) collectively returned around 25% YTD as of mid-Decem
content:
Why $AMZN - Amazon Has Been the Laggard of the Magnificent 7 in 2025 📉

The Magnificent 7 (Apple, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, Nvidia, Tesla) collectively returned around 25% YTD as of mid-December 2025 💹, driven largely by AI leaders like Nvidia and Alphabet 🚀. However, Amazon $AMZN has significantly underperformed, with YTD gains of only ~3-6% for much of the year (briefly dipping to low single digits or flat earlier) 😩, making it one of the group's weakest performers—often ranked last or near-last alongside Tesla in periods of weakness.

This lag stems from:
- Heavy upfront AI investments pressuring short-term margins and free cash flow 💸
- Slower perceived AI momentum compared to peers like Nvidia (chips) or Microsoft/Azure 🔗
- Post-pandemic e-commerce normalization and intense competition in retail/cloud 🛒☁️
- Market rotation away from "value-oriented" mega-caps toward pure AI plays 🔄

Despite this, Amazon's fundamentals have strengthened dramatically in 2025 📈, setting up a compelling bullish rebound case heading into 2026 🔥.

The Bullish Case: Undervalued Catch-Up Potential in AI, Cloud, and Efficiency 🐂

Amazon is no longer just an e-commerce giant—it's a diversified powerhouse with three high-margin engines: AWS (cloud) ☁️, Advertising 📢, and improving Retail margins 🛍️. Recent results show reacceleration, making it arguably the most attractive "laggard-to-leader" story in the Mag 7 🌟.

1. AWS Reacceleration – The AI Growth Engine Firing Up 🚀
- Q3 2025: AWS revenue hit $33B, up 20% YoY—its fastest growth since 2022, beating expectations of ~18% 🎯
- Driven by surging AI demand (Trainium chips, partnerships like Anthropic/OpenAI deals) 🤖
- Backlog swelled to $200B+, signaling multi-year visibility 📅
- Capex is ramping ($125B guided for 2025, up significantly) to build AI infrastructure 🏗️, but this is an investment phase—margins expected to expand as utilization rises 📈
- AWS remains the cloud market leader; reacceleration counters earlier fears of losing share to Azure/Google Cloud 👑

2. Advertising: A Hidden High-Margin Gem 💎
- Q3 ads revenue: $17.7B, up 23-24% YoY 📈
- Now one of Amazon's most profitable segments, benefiting from e-commerce flywheel and Prime Video ads rollout 📺
- High-teens growth projected into 2026 🔮

3. Retail Efficiency and Margin Expansion ⚙️
- North America/International segments showed resilient growth (11-14% in Q3) 🌍
- Cost-cutting (regionalization, robotics, logistics optimization) driving operating margins higher ✂️
- Overall operating income projected to hit $100B+ in 2026 by some analysts (e.g., TD Cowen) 💰

4. Attractive Valuation – Cheapest in the Mag 7 🏷️
- Trading at a trailing P/E below 33x—one of the lowest historical levels and cheapest among peers 😮
- Consensus analyst rating: Strong Buy (44+ Buys out of ~45-60 analysts) 👍
- Average 12-month price target: $295-297 (implying ~30% upside from ~$230 levels in mid-Dec 2025) 🎉
- High targets up to $300-360; some see $300+ "within weeks" on momentum ⚡

5. Broader Tailwinds 🌬️
- AI monetization just beginning: Custom chips (Trainium/Inferentia), agentic AI, and ecosystem advantages (data from retail/ads) 🧠
- Diversification reduces risk vs. pure-play AI stocks 🛡️
- Analysts (e.g., Motley Fool, TD Cowen) picking Amazon as potential top Mag 7 performer in 2026 due to catch-up potential 🏆

In summary, Amazon's 2025 lag created a rare undervaluation in a high-quality growth compounder 😴➡️🔥. With AWS firing on all cylinders, ads booming, and margins expanding, the setup for outperformance in 2026 is strong 💪. Wall Street's overwhelming bullishness reflects this—it's a classic "buy the laggard with improving fundamentals" opportunity 🛒. Risks remain (heavy capex, competition, macro slowdowns) ⚠️, but the reward/risk skews positively for long-term investors 🌱.

Spotlight

Wynn

Analysts at Goldman Sachs just added Wynn to its conviction buy list. The firm is confident with Wynn’s “Wynn Al Marjan in the UAE in 1Q27, plus WYNN’s best-in-class Las Vegas assets, leverage to a higher-income consumers, a strong 2026 Las Vegas event calendar, and an improving backdrop in Macau should drive transformative upside at WYNN,” as quoted by CNBC.

Nvidia

Analysts at Morgan Stanley just reiterated an overweight rating on Nvidia, with a $250 price target. The firm says NVDA will maintain a dominant market share and that threats are becoming overstated.

“We continue to see NVIDIA maintaining dominant market share, as threats are becoming overstated, though we aren’t sure exactly what will turn sentiment around,” they said, as quoted by CNBC. “Customers’ biggest anxiety for the next 12 months is their ability to procure enough NVIDIA product generally, and Vera Rubin specifically.”

Alphabet

Analysts at Guggenheim say Alphabet could run even higher. The firm raised its price target on GOOG to $375 from $330 with a buy rating.

The firm is confident in Alphabet because of strong cloud backlog growth, which is being supported by enterprise AI demand. It’s also confidence with Google Gemini’s rise as a leading AI platform with rapidly growing adoption metrics, as noted by CNBC.

News

Polymarket Partnership Could Be a Game Changer for TKO Group Holdings (TKO)

Nov 17

(In Case You Missed It)

Polymarket/UFC/TKO

Polymarket recently announced a multi-year partnership making it the Official and Exclusive Prediction Market Partner for UFC and Zuffa Boxing, introducing real-time fan prediction metrics directly into UFC broadcasts and social media experiences.

This collaboration marks the first time major sports organizations have integrated prediction market technology into the live fan experience, creating new ways for audiences to interact and for TKO's sports brands to differentiate themselves.

We'll explore how this innovative fan engagement initiative could strengthen TKO Group Holdings' investment narrative by deepening audience connection and content value.

What Is TKO Group Holdings' Investment Narrative?

To see the value in TKO Group Holdings as a shareholder, you really have to believe in its ability to keep building out top sports entertainment brands like UFC and WWE into global, multi-platform franchises. A lot of the story is about continuing to grow earnings and revenue faster than the overall market, attract loyal audiences, and evolve with changes in media rights, streaming, and digital fan engagement. Recent announcements, such as the multi-year partnership with Polymarket, reflect TKO’s push to unlock new monetization avenues and deepen fan involvement.

However, it’s unlikely this news will materially change the core short-term catalysts, which are still dominated by performance in key international deals, broadcast partnerships, and the rollout of new events. The biggest risks remain legal challenges, compression of media rights value, and questions about board experience. The new fan engagement efforts add differentiation, but don’t directly reduce these core risks for now. On the flip side, investors should pay close attention to ongoing legal proceedings against TKO and UFC.

TKO Group Holdings' shares have been on the rise but are still potentially undervalued by 15%.

Ten retail investors in the Simply Wall St Community produced fair value estimates for TKO ranging from US$63.92 to a very large US$37,618.47. While community views can be widely split, ongoing legal challenges remain a topic several market participants keep circling back to as a concern for TKO’s future performance. Explore these diverse perspectives and see how the risks and opportunities stack up.

TKO Group Holdings, Inc. is an American sports and sports entertainment company. Established on September 12, 2023, the public company was formed by a merger between Endeavor subsidiary Zuffa—the parent company of mixed martial arts promotion Ultimate Fighting Championship —and the professional wrestling promotion World Wrestling Entertainment. TKO is led by CEO Ari Emanuel and president Mark Shapiro, both of Endeavor; Dana White and Nick Khan retained their roles as CEOs of UFC and WWE respectively upon the merger, while WWE co-founder Vince McMahon served as executive chairman until resigning from the company in January 2024 amid a sex trafficking scandal. The merger marked the first time that WWE has not been solely and primarily majority-controlled by the McMahon family, which founded the company and owned it for over 70 years. As of 2024, the UFC and WWE were the two most valuable combat sports organizations in the world according to Forbes. UFC was listed as the most valued mixed martial arts company with a revenue of $1.406 billion and WWE being the most valued professional wrestling promotion with a revenue of $1.398 billion in 2023. (Wikipedia)

TKO owns iconic properties including UFC, the world’s premier mixed martial arts organization; WWE, the global leader in sports entertainment; and PBR, the world’s premier bull riding organization. Together, these properties reach 210 countries and territories and organize more than 500 live events year-round, attracting more than three million fans.

TKO also services and partners with major sports rights holders through IMG, an industry-leading global sports marketing agency; and On Location, a global leader in premium experiential hospitality. (Credit: TKO Group)

News

September 2, 2025

Market regime change: Microsoft weakening whilst Alphabet strengthens Nvidia and OpenAI have become synonymous with the AI revolution, each offering its own breakthrough solutions. This has made Nvidia the most valuable company on the market. OpenAI remains private for now. However, the old guard of IT giants, such as Microsoft and Alphabet, are not standing on the sidelines of the AI race, although they are conducting it in different ways, which is affecting their shares differently.

Microsoft owns a stake in OpenAI, giving it access to the latest developments, but integrates them into its own programmes, including chatbots. For a long time, betting on Microsoft was an indirect bet on OpenAI with their well-known ChatGPT. This approach paid off earlier this year, as the share price recovered faster than many competitors after the April slump. From its lows at the start of April to its highs at the end of July, the stock soared 55%, already making its way to historic highs since the beginning of June.

For a long time, Alphabet shares lagged their competitor in terms of share price growth over the past five years. They were also weaker in their recovery after the April correction, adding 40% to their lows before peaking at the end of July.

However, since August, the markets have clearly shifted into a different mode, with MSFT falling 7.5% against GOOG's 13% growth. This divergence began even before the release of GPT-5, the latest model, which faced widespread criticism from users, forcing the company to revert to GPT-4, originally announced over two years ago. Negative sentiment was also fuelled by comments from OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, who acknowledged that the market is currently in a bubble due to inflated expectations.

Google Gemini is steadily developing and gradually gaining consumer support due to its convenient integration into the company's extensive ecosystem. This aspiration resonates with investors. It seems that market participants are seeking to diversify their bets on AI agents, creating demand for Alphabet shares at the expense of Microsoft.

September marks the end of the financial year in the US, and investors often use August and September to switch to new trends or restart existing ones. September is historically the worst month for stock indices, but it can also be a good entry point during a downturn.

It is only important to understand whether we are seeing the start of a trend reversal or a temporary correction. Signals of this should be sought in MSFT's dynamics. Technically, with the stock trading at $506, it remains within a corrective pattern as long as it stays above the $450–$470 range. The upper bound aligns with last year's peak and the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of the rally from the April lows to the July highs, while the lower boundary corresponds to the 200-day moving average. A break below this level would signal a deeper trend reversal.

GOOG shares are close to local overbought conditions, as the RSI on daily timeframes is approaching 75, near which the shares have experienced local corrections over the past six years. Therefore, there is a high chance that both shares will soon experience increased selling pressure; the only question is how deep this correction will be. (FxPro)

content:
Here are 10 powerful, high-performance prompts that  Grok respond to extremely well. when you want to learn AI deeply and turn that knowledge into real money. 
Copy-paste them directly to AI and watch the quality of output.

1: The “Full AI Career Accelerator” prompt
“Act as a world-class AI career coach who has helped 100+ people go from zero to making $10k+/month with AI. Create a complete 12-week learning and monetization roadmap for someone starting with zero technical background but strong work ethic. Include exact tools, daily study schedule, projects, and 5 specific monetization paths ranked by speed to first $1,000 earned.”

2: The “Million-Dollar AI Business Idea” prompt
“You are a serial entrepreneur who has built and sold 3 AI companies. Generate 10 original, non-obvious AI business ideas that can be started with less than $500 and have realistic paths to $1M+ annual revenue in 18–24 months. For each idea, include target customer, core value proposition, tech stack (using mostly no-code + APIs), and first 3 monetization steps.”

3:The “Master Any AI Skill in 30 Days” prompt
“Create an intensive 30-day mastery plan to go from beginner to top 5% worldwide in [specific skill, e.g., prompt engineering, LangChain development, AI video creation, building custom GPTs, fine-tuning LLMs, etc.]. Include daily tasks, resources, deliberate practice exercises, and final capstone project that can be immediately monetized.”

4:The “AI Side Hustle That Prints Money” prompt
“List the current top 10 highest-ROI AI side hustles in December 2025 that require less than 10 hours/week and have users already making $5k–$50k/month. For each, give real case studies (anonymized), exact tools used, pricing strategy, and a step-by-step launch plan I can execute this weekend.”

5: The “Turn This Skill Into $10k/month” prompt
“I currently know [list your skills/tools, e.g., Midjourney, ChatGPT, basic Python, Canva]. Act as a ruthless monetization expert and design 5 specific service offerings I can sell tomorrow using only what I already know, with pricing, target clients, outreach script, and delivery templates that can scale to $10k/month within 90 days.”

6:The “Prompt Engineering Masterclass” prompt
“Teach me prompt engineering like a $5000 private course. Start with fundamentals, then advanced techniques (Chain-of-Thought, Tree-of-Thought, self-consistency, ReAct, etc.), then 50+ copy-pasteable “golden prompts” for learning, coding, content creation, and business. End with 10 real client deliverables I can sell as a prompt engineer for $500–$5000 each.”

7:The “No-Code AI App Empire” prompt
“Guide me step-by-step to build and launch a portfolio of 5+ no-code AI tools (using Bubble, Softr, Glide, Make, etc. + OpenAI/Claude APIs) that solve painful problems and can be sold or monetized via subscriptions. Prioritize ideas that already have proven demand on Reddit/IndieHackers in 2025.”

8:The “AI Consulting Cash Machine” prompt
“Role-play as a top-tier AI consultant charging $800/hour. Build me a complete ‘AI Consultant in a Box’ package: positioning, niche selection (choose the most profitable one right now), lead magnet, $2k–$10k offer stack, cold outreach templates that actually work in 2025, and case study templates even if I have zero clients yet.”

9:The “Steal These AI Money Blueprints” prompt
“Show me 10 detailed case studies of normal people (not famous) who started using AI in 2024–2025 and are now making $20k–$200k+/month. Include exactly what they sell, tools they use daily, how they get clients/customers, and the one breakthrough moment that 10x’d their income.”

10:The “Future-Proof AI Money Plan” prompt
“Looking 12–36 months ahead, map out the highest-leverage AI skills and business models that will be worth 10x more in 2027–2028 than today. Give me a prioritized learning sequence, early-mover opportunities, and specific projects I can start building now that will compound into million-dollar outcomes.”

this is the cleanest path to $100k+ right now

no face no complex funnels no $2k offers

just slides → telegram → $
content:
how to make multi 6-figs with tiktok slides, telegram funnel and a $97 info product

this is the cleanest path to $100k+ right now

no face no complex funnels no $2k offers

just slides → telegram → $97 product

let me break down the system

why this specific combo prints

tiktok slides: 
– easiest content to mass produce 
– algorithm pushes them hard 
– 50k-500k views per video is normal 
– zero filming or editing skills needed

telegram funnel: 
– owns the audience (no platform risk) 
– higher engagement than email 
– feels exclusive (increases perceived value) 
– instant communication with buyers

$97 product: 
– impulse buy price point 
– converts 3-5% in telegram 
– low enough to sell via chat 
– high enough for real profit

this stack works because each piece solves a weakness:

tiktok = attention (but you don't own it) 

telegram = ownership (but you need traffic) 

$97 product = monetization (but you need audience)

together = $100k+/year machine

the math to multi 6-figs

let's run real numbers

goal: $200k/year

at $97 per sale: 2,062 sales needed 

172 sales per month

40 sales per week

here's how the funnel makes this easy:

100 tiktok slide videos posted per month average 

100k views each = 10M monthly views 

0.5% click link in bio = 50k profile visits 

15% join telegram = 7,500 new telegram members/month

7,500 × 3% purchase rate × $97 = $21,825/month

that's $261,900/year

from slides you made in canva

the tiktok slide content strategy

not all slides perform the same

what works:

format 1: "things [audience] doesn't know about [topic]"

example slides: 
"things broke people don't know about credit cards" 
"things new investors don't know about index funds" 
"things freelancers don't know about taxes"

3-5 facts per video each fact = one slide simple text, no fancy graphics

these get shared like crazy

format 2: "if i had to start [thing] from $0 again"

"if i had to build an ecom brand from $0 again" 
"if i had to learn coding from $0 again"

step-by-step breakdown 10-15 slides actionable timeline

people screenshot and save these

format 3: mistakes/lessons

"7 mistakes that kept me broke" 
"5 lessons that made me $100k"

one mistake per slide quick explanation how to avoid it

controversy and relatability = engagement

format 4: comparison slides

"$1k/month vs $10k/month freelancer" 
"beginner investor vs experienced investor"

side-by-side comparisons highlights what changes at each level

aspirational content converts

the posting schedule:

volume beats everything

post 3-4 slides per day minimum

why this works: 
– tiktok algorithm tests each video 
– more videos = more lottery tickets 
– 1 in 10 hits big (100k+ views) 
– hitting big puts you on FYP 
– FYP = your other videos get pushed

batch creation:

sunday (3 hours): 
– research 30 topics (reddit, quora, twitter) 
– write scripts for each 
– create canva templates (5 different styles to rotate) 
– export all 30 videos

monday-sunday: 
– schedule 4 posts per day 
– engage with comments (15 min/day) 
– monitor what hits, make more of that

total time: 4-5 hours per week

the telegram funnel mechanics

this is where the magic happens

why telegram over email:

email open rate: 20-30% 

telegram message seen rate: 70-90%

telegram feels personal people check it constantly notifications are immediate community aspect increases trust

how to build it:

step 1: create telegram channel (not group)

channel = you broadcast, they receive 

group = chaos, too much noise

your channel is value delivery + sales

step 2: the tiktok to telegram bridge

tiktok bio: "daily [niche] tips in my telegram (free) → [link]"

keep it simple one clear CTA free = no friction

every video mentions: "more of this in my free telegram (link in bio)"

don't pitch hard just mention it exists

15% of profile visitors will join

step 3: telegram content calendar

monday: tactical post (how to do X) 
tuesday: mistake breakdown (what not to do) 
wednesday: case study or example
thursday: Q&A or poll 
friday: tactical post 
saturday: story or lesson 
sunday: SALES POST

ratio: 6 value posts : 1 sales post

they don't feel sold to they feel helped

then when you pitch the $97 product?

they buy

step 4: the welcome sequence

when someone joins telegram:

message 1 (immediate): "welcome! here's what you'll get here:

daily actionable tips

real examples

strategies that actually work dropped every day, no fluff"

message 2 (day 2): send your best free resource "here's the guide i mentioned → [link]"

message 3 (day 4): soft pitch "btw i have a full course on this if you want to go deeper it's $97, details here → [link]"

message 4 (day 7): full sales pitch (see below)

the $97 product structure

what to sell:

option 1: video course

15-25 videos (5-15 min each)

covers one specific problem deeply

includes templates/worksheets

hosted on whop or teachable

option 2: notion database

step-by-step system in notion

templates to copy

examples and case studies

checklists and frameworks

option 3: community access

private telegram group (different from channel)

weekly calls or AMAs

direct access to you

ongoing support

pick one format

solve one problem

price at $97

the weekly sales post formula

every sunday in telegram:

subject: "quick question"

body: "been thinking about [common problem your audience has]
most people try to solve it by [wrong way]
doesn't work because [reason]

what actually works:

[tease 3 key points from your course]

i put together a full system on this

covers:
[outcome 1]

[outcome 2]

[outcome 3]

normally $197, running it at $97 right now

if you've been struggling with [problem], this is the fastest fix

link: [checkout page]
[your name]"

this converts at 3-5% of active telegram members

the conversion math

month 1:

7,500 new telegram members
3% buy = 225 sales

225 × $97 = $21,825

but here's the compound effect:

month 2:

previous 7,500 members

new 7,500 members

total: 15,000

3% buy = 450 sales

450 × $97 = $43,650

month 6:

45,000 total members

weekly sales post to all

3% × 45,000 = 1,350 buyers

1,350 × $97 = $130,950/month

that's $1.57M/year run rate

from tiktok slides

the scaling optimizations

once the basic system prints:

play 1: multiple tiktok accounts

run 3-5 accounts simultaneously all pointing to same telegram all selling same product

3x accounts = 3x traffic = 3x sales

play 2: add payment plan

$97 one-time OR $37/month × 3

increases conversions by 30-50%

play 3: order bump at checkout

"add bonus module for $27"

35% take rate = extra $9.45 per customer

play 4: upsell after purchase

email sequence to $97 buyers: "ready for advanced version? $297"

10% conversion = extra $29.70 per customer

play 5: backend monetization

once telegram hits 10k+ members:

sponsored posts ($500-$2k each)

affiliate deals for relevant products

premium tier at $47/month

1-on-1 consulting at $500-$1k

adds $5k-$15k/month extra

the content repurposing play

one piece of research = content everywhere

create one deep breakdown

turn it into:

5 tiktok slide videos

3 telegram posts

1 twitter thread

1 instagram carousel

1 youtube short

same content, 11 different pieces

maximum leverage

the niche selection

best niches for this model:
- making money
- freelancing
- ecom
- investing
- side hustles
- passive income
- self improvement
- productivity
- habits
- fitness
- dating
- career advancement
- salary negotiation
- promotions
- skill building
- resume tips

avoid:
- entertainment (no buyer intent)
- news (not evergreen)
- anything without clear monetization

most people won't do this because it requires:
– posting 3-4x daily for months 
– building telegram to 10k+ before real money 
– being okay with no immediate results 
– consistency over intensity

most people quit at:

month 1: "only 500 telegram members, this sucks" 

month 2: "still only $2k/month, not worth it"

they quit right before it compounds

reality:

month 1-2: building 
month 3-4: momentum 
month 5-6: printing 
month 7+: scaling

the people at $20k+/month stuck around when it was $500/month

the tech stack

what you actually need:

tiktok: free 
canva: $13/month 
telegram: free 
whop: free (5% transaction fee) 
notion: free

total cost: $13/month
no excuses
the automated workflow
here's what hands-off looks like:
sunday: batch 30 tiktok slides (3 hours) monday-sunday: auto-post 4 per day daily: telegram value post (15 min) sunday: sales post in telegram (10 min) daily: reply to DMs (30 min)

total weekly time: 7 hours

once you're at $20k/month

hire VA for $800/month to:

create the slides

schedule posts

handle customer support
your time: 2 hours/week reviewing
$20k/month passive

from slides about basic shit people need to know

Bonus - Templates, swipe files, resources

content:
Biz News

Company/Brand Spotlight

December 2025: Past, Present and Future

Wynn

Analysts at Goldman Sachs just added Wynn to its conviction buy list. The firm is confident with Wynn’s “Wynn Al Marjan in the UAE in 1Q27, plus WYNN’s best-in-class Las Vegas assets, leverage to a higher-income consumers, a strong 2026 Las Vegas event calendar, and an improving backdrop in Macau should drive transformative upside at WYNN,” as quoted by CNBC.

News

(Approx 48 hours Ago)

Nvidia

Analysts at Morgan Stanley just reiterated an overweight rating on Nvidia, with a $250 price target. The firm says NVDA will maintain a dominant market share and that threats are becoming overstated.

“We continue to see NVIDIA maintaining dominant market share, as threats are becoming overstated, though we aren’t sure exactly what will turn sentiment around,” they said, as quoted by CNBC. “Customers’ biggest anxiety for the next 12 months is their ability to procure enough NVIDIA product generally, and Vera Rubin specifically.” 

Alphabet

Analysts at Guggenheim say Alphabet could run even higher. The firm raised its price target on GOOG to $375 from $330 with a buy rating.  The firm is confident in Alphabet because of strong cloud backlog growth, which is being supported by enterprise AI demand. It’s also confidence with Google Gemini’s rise as a leading AI platform with rapidly growing adoption metrics, as noted by CNBC. 

 News  

Polymarket Partnership Could Be a Game Changer for TKO Group Holdings (TKO) 

Nov 17  (In Case You Missed It)

Polymarket/UFC/TKO 

Polymarket recently announced a multi-year partnership making it the Official and Exclusive Prediction Market Partner for UFC and Zuffa Boxing, introducing real-time fan prediction metrics directly into UFC broadcasts and social media experiences.  This collaboration marks the first time major sports organizations have integrated prediction market technology into the live fan experience, creating new ways for audiences to interact and for TKO's sports brands to differentiate themselves.  We'll explore how this innovative fan engagement initiative could strengthen TKO Group Holdings' investment narrative by deepening audience connection and content value.

What Is TKO Group Holdings' Investment Narrative?

To see the value in TKO Group Holdings as a shareholder, you really have to believe in its ability to keep building out top sports entertainment brands like UFC and WWE into global, multi-platform franchises. A lot of the story is about continuing to grow earnings and revenue faster than the overall market, attract loyal audiences, and evolve with changes in media rights, streaming, and digital fan engagement. 

Recent announcements, such as the multi-year partnership with Polymarket, reflect TKO’s push to unlock new monetization avenues and deepen fan involvement.  

However, it’s unlikely this news will materially change the core short-term catalysts, which are still dominated by performance in key international deals, broadcast partnerships, and the rollout of new events. The biggest risks remain legal challenges, compression of media rights value, and questions about board experience. The new fan engagement efforts add differentiation, but don’t directly reduce these core risks for now. On the flip side, investors should pay close attention to ongoing legal proceedings against TKO and UFC.

TKO Group Holdings' shares have been on the rise but are still potentially undervalued by 15%.  Ten retail investors in the Simply Wall St Community produced fair value estimates for TKO ranging from US$63.92 to a very large US$37,618.47. While community views can be widely split, ongoing legal challenges remain a topic several market participants keep circling back to as a concern for TKO’s future performance. Explore these diverse perspectives and see how the risks and opportunities stack up.  

News Flashback

September 13, 2025

Jones Financial Companies Lllp Acquires 77,883 Shares of TKO Group Holdings, Inc. $TKO Jones Financial Companies Lllp significantly increased its stake in TKO Group Holdings by 149,775%, now owning 77,935 shares valued at approximately $11.9 million.

Several institutional investors, including Vanguard and Invesco, have also raised their stakes in TKO Group, indicating a strong interest in the company, which is now 89.79% owned by institutional investors.  TKO Group recently declared an increased quarterly dividend of $0.76, up from $0.38, reflecting a strong return for investors despite a high dividend payout ratio of 125.62%.  Jones Financial Companies Lllp increased its position in shares of TKO Group Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:TKO by 149,775.0% during the 1st quarter, according to the company in its most recent 13F filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. The institutional investor owned 77,935 shares of the company's stock after acquiring an additional 77,883 shares during the period. Jones Financial Companies Lllp's holdings in TKO Group were worth $11,909,000 at the end of the most recent quarter.  

Several other hedge funds and other institutional investors have also made changes to their positions in the business. Hemington Wealth Management grew its stake in TKO Group by 425.0% during the 1st quarter. Hemington Wealth Management now owns 168 shares of the company's stock worth $25,000 after buying an additional 136 shares during the last quarter. N.E.W. Advisory Services LLC acquired a new position in TKO Group during the first quarter worth $26,000. Sentry Investment Management LLC acquired a new position in TKO Group during the first quarter worth $25,000. Bartlett & CO. Wealth Management LLC acquired a new position in shares of TKO Group in the first quarter worth $27,000. Finally, Farther Finance Advisors LLC raised its holdings in shares of TKO Group by 129.8% in the first quarter. Farther Finance Advisors LLC now owns 216 shares of the company's stock worth $33,000 after acquiring an additional 122 shares during the last quarter. Institutional investors and hedge funds own 89.79% of the company's stock.  Insider Buying and Selling at TKO Group  In related news, Director Nick Khan sold 45,168 shares of the company's stock in a transaction on Monday, July 21st. The shares were sold at an average price of $170.82, for a total value of $7,715,597.76. Following the completion of the sale, the director owned 156,494 shares in the company, valued at $26,732,305.08. This represents a 22.40% decrease in their position. The transaction was disclosed in a filing with the Securities & Exchange Commission. Over the last 90 days, insiders sold 73,725 shares of company stock valued at $12,767,807. Corporate insiders own 61.30% of the company's stock.  Wall Street Analyst Weigh In  TKO has been the subject of a number of recent analyst reports. Baird R W raised TKO Group to a "strong-buy" rating in a research report on Friday, September 5th. Zacks Research upgraded TKO Group from a "strong sell" rating to a "hold" rating in a research note on Tuesday, September 2nd. Bank of America upped their target price on TKO Group from $200.00 to $210.00 and gave the company a "buy" rating in a report on Tuesday, August 12th. Robert W. Baird began coverage on TKO Group in a report on Friday, September 5th. They set an "outperform" rating and a $225.00 target price for the company. Finally, Roth Capital raised their target price on TKO Group from $208.00 to $210.00 and gave the company a "buy" rating in a research report on Tuesday, August 12th. One analyst has rated the stock with a Strong Buy rating, fourteen have issued a Buy rating and four have given a Hold rating to the company's stock. According to data from MarketBeat, the stock has a consensus rating of "Moderate Buy" and a consensus price target of $192.21.

News Flashback

TKO Group Stock Up 0.0%  Shares of NYSE:TKO traded up $0.09 during midday trading on Friday, hitting $202.33. 897,072 shares of the stock were exchanged, compared to its average volume of 683,611. TKO Group Holdings, Inc. has a 52-week low of $114.01 and a 52-week high of $204.10. The business's fifty day simple moving average is $178.94 and its two-hundred day simple moving average is $165.22. The company has a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.28, a quick ratio of 1.30 and a current ratio of 1.30. The company has a market capitalization of $40.12 billion, a PE ratio of 83.61 and a beta of 0.79.  TKO Group (NYSE:TKO) last issued its quarterly earnings results on Wednesday, August 6th. The company reported $1.17 earnings per share (EPS) for the quarter, missing the consensus estimate of $1.23 by ($0.06). TKO Group had a net margin of 5.40% and a return on equity of 2.82%. The business had revenue of $1.31 billion during the quarter, compared to analyst estimates of $1.23 billion. During the same period in the prior year, the business posted $0.72 earnings per share. The company's revenue for the quarter was up 53.7% compared to the same quarter last year. As a group, research analysts predict that TKO Group Holdings, Inc. will post 3.88 earnings per share for the current fiscal year. 

TKO Group Increases Dividend

The business also recently declared a quarterly dividend, which will (was) be paid on Tuesday, September 30th. Stockholders of record on Monday, September 15th will be paid a $0.76 dividend. This is an increase from TKO Group's previous quarterly dividend of $0.38. The ex-dividend date of this dividend is Monday, September 15th. This represents a $3.04 dividend on an annualized basis and a dividend yield of 1.5%. TKO Group's dividend payout ratio (DPR) is currently 62.81%. 

TKO Group Profile  TKO Group Holdings, Inc operates as a sports and entertainment company. The company produces and licenses live events, television programs, and long-form and short-form content, reality series, and other filmed entertainment on digital and linear channels and via pay-per-view. It is involved in the merchandising of video games, apparel, equipment, trading cards, memorabilia, digital goods, and toys, as well as sale of travel packages and tickets.

News  Flashback

TKO Bullish Friday And Weekend Media Man Edition! 

TKO Group Holdings, Inc. is an American sports and sports entertainment company. Established on September 12, 2023, the public company was formed by a merger between Endeavor subsidiary Zuffa—the parent company of mixed martial arts promotion Ultimate Fighting Championship —and the professional wrestling promotion World Wrestling Entertainment. TKO is led by CEO Ari Emanuel and president Mark Shapiro, both of Endeavor; Dana White and Nick Khan retained their roles as CEOs of UFC and WWE respectively upon the merger, while WWE co-founder Vince McMahon served as executive chairman until resigning from the company in January 2024 amid a sex trafficking scandal. The merger marked the first time that WWE has not been solely and primarily majority-controlled by the McMahon family, which founded the company and owned it for over 70 years. As of 2024, the UFC and WWE were the two most valuable combat sports organizations in the world according to Forbes. UFC was listed as the most valued mixed martial arts company with a revenue of $1.406 billion and WWE being the most valued professional wrestling promotion with a revenue of $1.398 billion in 2023. (Wikipedia)

TKO owns iconic properties including UFC, the world’s premier mixed martial arts organization; WWE, the global leader in sports entertainment; and PBR, the world’s premier bull riding organization. Together, these properties reach 210 countries and territories and organize more than 500 live events year-round, attracting more than three million fans.

TKO also services and partners with major sports rights holders through IMG, an industry-leading global sports marketing agency; and On Location, a global leader in premium experiential hospitality. (Credit: TKO Group)

News Flashback
September 2, 2025

Market regime change: Microsoft weakening whilst Alphabet strengthens 

Nvidia and OpenAI have become synonymous with the AI revolution, each offering its own breakthrough solutions. This has made Nvidia the most valuable company on the market. OpenAI remains private for now. However, the old guard of IT giants, such as Microsoft and Alphabet, are not standing on the sidelines of the AI race, although they are conducting it in different ways, which is affecting their shares differently.  Microsoft owns a stake in OpenAI, giving it access to the latest developments, but integrates them into its own programmes, including chatbots. For a long time, betting on Microsoft was an indirect bet on OpenAI with their well-known ChatGPT. This approach paid off earlier this year, as the share price recovered faster than many competitors after the April slump. From its lows at the start of April to its highs at the end of July, the stock soared 55%, already making its way to historic highs since the beginning of June.  For a long time, Alphabet shares lagged their competitor in terms of share price growth over the past five years. They were also weaker in their recovery after the April correction, adding 40% to their lows before peaking at the end of July.  However, since August, the markets have clearly shifted into a different mode, with MSFT falling 7.5% against GOOG's 13% growth. This divergence began even before the release of GPT-5, the latest model, which faced widespread criticism from users, forcing the company to revert to GPT-4, originally announced over two years ago. Negative sentiment was also fuelled by comments from OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, who acknowledged that the market is currently in a bubble due to inflated expectations.  Google Gemini is steadily developing and gradually gaining consumer support due to its convenient integration into the company's extensive ecosystem. This aspiration resonates with investors. It seems that market participants are seeking to diversify their bets on AI agents, creating demand for Alphabet shares at the expense of Microsoft.

September marks the end of the financial year in the US, and investors often use August and September to switch to new trends or restart existing ones. September is historically the worst month for stock indices, but it can also be a good entry point during a downturn.  It is only important to understand whether we are seeing the start of a trend reversal or a temporary correction. Signals of this should be sought in MSFT's dynamics. Technically, with the stock trading at $506, it remains within a corrective pattern as long as it stays above the $450–$470 range. The upper bound aligns with last year's peak and the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of the rally from the April lows to the July highs, while the lower boundary corresponds to the 200-day moving average. A break below this level would signal a deeper trend reversal.  GOOG shares are close to local overbought conditions, as the RSI on daily timeframes is approaching 75, near which the shares have experienced local corrections over the past six years. Therefore, there is a high chance that both shares will soon experience increased selling pressure; the only question is how deep this correction will be. (FxPro)

Polymarket recently announced a multi-year partnership making it the Official and Exclusive Prediction Market Partner for UFC and Zuffa Boxing, introducing real-time fan prediction metrics directly into UFC broadcasts and social media experiences.

This collaboration marks the first time major sports organizations have integrated prediction market technology into the live fan experience, creating new ways for audiences to interact and for TKO's sports brands to differentiate themselves.

We'll explore how this innovative fan engagement initiative could strengthen TKO Group Holdings' investment narrative by deepening audience connection and content value.

What Is TKO Group Holdings' Investment Narrative?

To see the value in TKO Group Holdings as a shareholder, you really have to believe in its ability to keep building out top sports entertainment brands like UFC and WWE into global, multi-platform franchises. A lot of the story is about continuing to grow earnings and revenue faster than the overall market, attract loyal audiences, and evolve with changes in media rights, streaming, and digital fan engagement. Recent announcements, such as the multi-year partnership with Polymarket, reflect TKO’s push to unlock new monetization avenues and deepen fan involvement.

However, it’s unlikely this news will materially change the core short-term catalysts, which are still dominated by performance in key international deals, broadcast partnerships, and the rollout of new events. The biggest risks remain legal challenges, compression of media rights value, and questions about board experience. The new fan engagement efforts add differentiation, but don’t directly reduce these core risks for now. On the flip side, investors should pay close attention to ongoing legal proceedings against TKO and UFC.

TKO Group Holdings' shares have been on the rise but are still potentially undervalued by 15%.

Ten retail investors in the Simply Wall St Community produced fair value estimates for TKO ranging from US$63.92 to a very large US$37,618.47. While community views can be widely split, ongoing legal challenges remain a topic several market participants keep circling back to as a concern for TKO’s future performance. Explore these diverse perspectives and see how the risks and opportunities stack up.
