TKO Group Holdings, Inc. is an American sports and sports entertainment company. Established on September 12, 2023, the public company was formed by a merger between Endeavor subsidiary Zuffa—the parent company of mixed martial arts promotion Ultimate Fighting Championship —and the professional wrestling promotion World Wrestling Entertainment. TKO is led by CEO Ari Emanuel and president Mark Shapiro, both of Endeavor; Dana White and Nick Khan retained their roles as CEOs of UFC and WWE respectively upon the merger, while WWE co-founder Vince McMahon served as executive chairman until resigning from the company in January 2024 amid a sex trafficking scandal. The merger marked the first time that WWE has not been solely and primarily majority-controlled by the McMahon family, which founded the company and owned it for over 70 years. As of 2024, the UFC and WWE were the two most valuable combat sports organizations in the world according to Forbes. UFC was listed as the most valued mixed martial arts company with a revenue of $1.406 billion and WWE being the most valued professional wrestling promotion with a revenue of $1.398 billion in 2023. (Wikipedia)

TKO owns iconic properties including UFC, the world’s premier mixed martial arts organization; WWE, the global leader in sports entertainment; and PBR, the world’s premier bull riding organization. Together, these properties reach 210 countries and territories and organize more than 500 live events year-round, attracting more than three million fans.

TKO also services and partners with major sports rights holders through IMG, an industry-leading global sports marketing agency; and On Location, a global leader in premium experiential hospitality. (Credit: TKO Group)

News

September 2, 2025

Market regime change: Microsoft weakening whilst Alphabet strengthens Nvidia and OpenAI have become synonymous with the AI revolution, each offering its own breakthrough solutions. This has made Nvidia the most valuable company on the market. OpenAI remains private for now. However, the old guard of IT giants, such as Microsoft and Alphabet, are not standing on the sidelines of the AI race, although they are conducting it in different ways, which is affecting their shares differently.

Microsoft owns a stake in OpenAI, giving it access to the latest developments, but integrates them into its own programmes, including chatbots. For a long time, betting on Microsoft was an indirect bet on OpenAI with their well-known ChatGPT. This approach paid off earlier this year, as the share price recovered faster than many competitors after the April slump. From its lows at the start of April to its highs at the end of July, the stock soared 55%, already making its way to historic highs since the beginning of June.

For a long time, Alphabet shares lagged their competitor in terms of share price growth over the past five years. They were also weaker in their recovery after the April correction, adding 40% to their lows before peaking at the end of July.

However, since August, the markets have clearly shifted into a different mode, with MSFT falling 7.5% against GOOG's 13% growth. This divergence began even before the release of GPT-5, the latest model, which faced widespread criticism from users, forcing the company to revert to GPT-4, originally announced over two years ago. Negative sentiment was also fuelled by comments from OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, who acknowledged that the market is currently in a bubble due to inflated expectations.

Google Gemini is steadily developing and gradually gaining consumer support due to its convenient integration into the company's extensive ecosystem. This aspiration resonates with investors. It seems that market participants are seeking to diversify their bets on AI agents, creating demand for Alphabet shares at the expense of Microsoft.

September marks the end of the financial year in the US, and investors often use August and September to switch to new trends or restart existing ones. September is historically the worst month for stock indices, but it can also be a good entry point during a downturn.

It is only important to understand whether we are seeing the start of a trend reversal or a temporary correction. Signals of this should be sought in MSFT's dynamics. Technically, with the stock trading at $506, it remains within a corrective pattern as long as it stays above the $450–$470 range. The upper bound aligns with last year's peak and the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of the rally from the April lows to the July highs, while the lower boundary corresponds to the 200-day moving average. A break below this level would signal a deeper trend reversal.

GOOG shares are close to local overbought conditions, as the RSI on daily timeframes is approaching 75, near which the shares have experienced local corrections over the past six years. Therefore, there is a high chance that both shares will soon experience increased selling pressure; the only question is how deep this correction will be. (FxPro)

News

Oil

September 3

News from OPEC prevents oil prices from rising Oil came under pressure on Wednesday, losing more than 2% on reports by Bloomberg that OPEC+ plans to raise quotas again at its next meeting. Last month, the cartel removed all additional self-imposed restrictions that major producers such as Saudi Arabia, Russia, and Kazakhstan had taken on.

This new move is an open demonstration of the fight for market share, rather than an attempt to support prices. First and foremost, it is a fight against the US, which is actively promoting its energy through policy, imposing sanctions on oil-producing countries and including oil and gas purchases in trade deals.

If the reduction is indeed confirmed, it promises to be an impressive factor of pressure on quotations, overturning the upward price trend of the previous couple of weeks.

Earlier, oil was supported by a reduction in commercial stocks in the US and the return of risk appetite to stock markets, thanks to signals of a September rate cut.

With its reversal on Wednesday, oil confirmed the strength of resistance in the form of the 200-day moving average and the previous consolidation area. Although oil has exceeded this curve several times over the past year, it still acts as a trend resistance line.

Three attempts by Saudi Arabia and its staunch allies to switch from supporting prices to fighting for market share in 2008, 2014, and 2020 drove the price below $30, devaluing it by more than three-quarters. But in both cases, the increase in production coincided with an economic and market downturn. This is not happening now, which supports prices and allows OPEC+ to increase quotas.

However, for many countries, increasing quotas is much easier than increasing production. Countries such as Russia and Iran cannot simply sell oil and increase production due to sales restrictions and equipment limitations. This will keep prices from falling, keeping them within a downward range rather than turning into a collapse like we saw in late 2014 and early 2020. On balance, this supports our previous forecast for WTI falling to $55 by the end of September and to $50 by the end of the year, barring any economic shocks. It could also fall to the $30 range if there are risks of a looming global recession.

Gold miners advanced, with Northern Star adding 1.2 per cent, Newmont up 0.6 per cent and Evolution Mining rising 3 per cent. TKO Group Holdings, Inc. is an American sports and sports entertainment company. Established on September 12, 2023, the public company was formed by a merger between Endeavor subsidiary Zuffa—the parent company of mixed martial arts promotion Ultimate Fighting Championship —and the professional wrestling promotion World Wrestling Entertainment. TKO is led by CEO Ari Emanuel and president Mark Shapiro, both of Endeavor; Dana White and Nick Khan retained their roles as CEOs of UFC and WWE respectively upon the merger, while WWE co-founder Vince McMahon served as executive chairman until resigning from the company in January 2024 amid a sex trafficking scandal. The merger marked the first time that WWE has not been solely and primarily majority-controlled by the McMahon family, which founded the company and owned it for over 70 years. As of 2024, the UFC and WWE were the two most valuable combat sports organizations in the world according to Forbes. UFC was listed as the most valued mixed martial arts company with a revenue of $1.406 billion and WWE being the most valued professional wrestling promotion with a revenue of $1.398 billion in 2023. (Wikipedia)

TKO owns iconic properties including UFC, the world’s premier mixed martial arts organization; WWE, the global leader in sports entertainment; and PBR, the world’s premier bull riding organization. Together, these properties reach 210 countries and territories and organize more than 500 live events year-round, attracting more than three million fans.

TKO also services and partners with major sports rights holders through IMG, an industry-leading global sports marketing agency; and On Location, a global leader in premium experiential hospitality. (Credit: TKO Group)

News

September 2, 2025

Market regime change: Microsoft weakening whilst Alphabet strengthens Nvidia and OpenAI have become synonymous with the AI revolution, each offering its own breakthrough solutions. This has made Nvidia the most valuable company on the market. OpenAI remains private for now. However, the old guard of IT giants, such as Microsoft and Alphabet, are not standing on the sidelines of the AI race, although they are conducting it in different ways, which is affecting their shares differently.

Microsoft owns a stake in OpenAI, giving it access to the latest developments, but integrates them into its own programmes, including chatbots. For a long time, betting on Microsoft was an indirect bet on OpenAI with their well-known ChatGPT. This approach paid off earlier this year, as the share price recovered faster than many competitors after the April slump. From its lows at the start of April to its highs at the end of July, the stock soared 55%, already making its way to historic highs since the beginning of June.

For a long time, Alphabet shares lagged their competitor in terms of share price growth over the past five years. They were also weaker in their recovery after the April correction, adding 40% to their lows before peaking at the end of July.

However, since August, the markets have clearly shifted into a different mode, with MSFT falling 7.5% against GOOG's 13% growth. This divergence began even before the release of GPT-5, the latest model, which faced widespread criticism from users, forcing the company to revert to GPT-4, originally announced over two years ago. Negative sentiment was also fuelled by comments from OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, who acknowledged that the market is currently in a bubble due to inflated expectations.

Google Gemini is steadily developing and gradually gaining consumer support due to its convenient integration into the company's extensive ecosystem. This aspiration resonates with investors. It seems that market participants are seeking to diversify their bets on AI agents, creating demand for Alphabet shares at the expense of Microsoft.

September marks the end of the financial year in the US, and investors often use August and September to switch to new trends or restart existing ones. September is historically the worst month for stock indices, but it can also be a good entry point during a downturn.

It is only important to understand whether we are seeing the start of a trend reversal or a temporary correction. Signals of this should be sought in MSFT's dynamics. Technically, with the stock trading at $506, it remains within a corrective pattern as long as it stays above the $450–$470 range. The upper bound aligns with last year's peak and the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of the rally from the April lows to the July highs, while the lower boundary corresponds to the 200-day moving average. A break below this level would signal a deeper trend reversal.

GOOG shares are close to local overbought conditions, as the RSI on daily timeframes is approaching 75, near which the shares have experienced local corrections over the past six years. Therefore, there is a high chance that both shares will soon experience increased selling pressure; the only question is how deep this correction will be. (FxPro)

News

Oil

September 3

News from OPEC prevents oil prices from rising Oil came under pressure on Wednesday, losing more than 2% on reports by Bloomberg that OPEC+ plans to raise quotas again at its next meeting. Last month, the cartel removed all additional self-imposed restrictions that major producers such as Saudi Arabia, Russia, and Kazakhstan had taken on.

This new move is an open demonstration of the fight for market share, rather than an attempt to support prices. First and foremost, it is a fight against the US, which is actively promoting its energy through policy, imposing sanctions on oil-producing countries and including oil and gas purchases in trade deals.

If the reduction is indeed confirmed, it promises to be an impressive factor of pressure on quotations, overturning the upward price trend of the previous couple of weeks.

Earlier, oil was supported by a reduction in commercial stocks in the US and the return of risk appetite to stock markets, thanks to signals of a September rate cut.

With its reversal on Wednesday, oil confirmed the strength of resistance in the form of the 200-day moving average and the previous consolidation area. Although oil has exceeded this curve several times over the past year, it still acts as a trend resistance line.

Three attempts by Saudi Arabia and its staunch allies to switch from supporting prices to fighting for market share in 2008, 2014, and 2020 drove the price below $30, devaluing it by more than three-quarters. But in both cases, the increase in production coincided with an economic and market downturn. This is not happening now, which supports prices and allows OPEC+ to increase quotas.

However, for many countries, increasing quotas is much easier than increasing production. Countries such as Russia and Iran cannot simply sell oil and increase production due to sales restrictions and equipment limitations. This will keep prices from falling, keeping them within a downward range rather than turning into a collapse like we saw in late 2014 and early 2020. On balance, this supports our previous forecast for WTI falling to $55 by the end of September and to $50 by the end of the year, barring any economic shocks. It could also fall to the $30 range if there are risks of a looming global recession.

title: $BABA $BIDU The Bloomberg Intelligence “China AI 2026 Outlook” portrays a structural divergence between China’s AI reality and the equity market narrative. China is building a vast, state-directed AI
content:
$BABA $BIDU The Bloomberg Intelligence “China AI 2026 Outlook” portrays a structural divergence between China’s AI reality and the equity market narrative. China is building a vast, state-directed AI infrastructure that will likely deliver meaningful productivity gains to the real economy while leaving listed AI-exposed companies with low returns on capital, compressed margins and extended payback periods. The report argues that China’s AI strategy is explicitly economy-first and shareholder-last: private platforms such as Alibaba, Baidu and SenseTime have become quasi-public utilities subsidizing a national rollout of low-cost, open-source AI, while the US retains the technological lead in cutting-edge models and chips.  ￼ For investors, the implication is that the main winners of China’s AI push are Chinese consumers, industrial users and the state, not the minority shareholders of domestic AI software and cloud firms.

At the macro level, China’s “AI Plus” initiative marks a deliberate shift in the growth engine from property and low-end manufacturing to high tech and digital infrastructure. The plan seeks to embed AI across sectors from EVs, smartphones and logistics to agriculture, robotics and factory automation, backed by heavy investment in data centers and coordinated R&D across universities, startups and state-owned enterprises.  ￼ Bloomberg Economics estimates that high-tech related activity could rise from 14.3% of GDP in 2023 to nearly 19% in 2026, putting it roughly on par with the property sector.  ￼ This multi-year capital deepening is likely to raise China’s potential output and partially offset demographic headwinds, but because much of the capital is being deployed at low or unpriced returns by state actors or policy-constrained platforms, the social ROI will exceed the financial ROI. The equity market should therefore assign a materially higher risk premium to Chinese AI-exposed names than to comparable Western peers, even as China’s macro productivity trend improves.

Sector structure and monetization dynamics are central to the report’s thesis. China has rapidly built a vast and fragmented AI model ecosystem, with the number of registered large language models (LLMs) rising from 14 in October 2023 to 538 by September 2025.  ￼ China also has a deep software talent pool of about 7m developers, a roughly 3:2 numerical advantage over the US.  ￼ Yet this capacity has been channeled into open-source, low-cost models that are largely offered free or near-free to maximize adoption. DeepSeek’s aggressive API cuts – output-token pricing slashed to $0.0042 per 1k tokens, a 96–97% discount to GPT‑4o/GPT‑5 and Claude Sonnet – have triggered a nationwide price war and entrenched the view that basic AI capabilities should be commoditized and effectively free.  ￼ Consumer monetization is minimal: China’s top 10 AI chatbots generated less than $1m in iOS revenue over 12 months to October 2025, compared with $1.7b for ChatGPT over the same period, and more than 500 LLMs are competing in a saturated market with no clear “killer app.”  ￼ In such an environment, sustainable pricing power and high-margin revenue are structurally elusive.

The report’s bottom-up market sizing underscores how modest the commercial opportunity is relative to the capital being deployed. Bloomberg Intelligence estimates that by 2034, China’s domestic AI-related software, cloud and services market will reach only $54.8b, roughly equivalent to Tencent’s current value-added service revenue.  ￼ Within that, AI cloud computing (IaaS and PaaS) is expected to reach about $22.2b, AI software $16.5b and AI-related services $16.1b.  ￼ These figures are small relative to the capex plans of leading firms: consensus expects combined AI-related capex for Alibaba, Tencent, Baidu, SenseTime and Kingsoft Cloud to reach about $30b in 2025 and $32b in 2026, while Alibaba alone has announced at least 380b RMB (about $52b) of AI and cloud infrastructure investment over 3 years.  The implied prospective ROIC on AI-specific capex for Chinese internet and cloud platforms is therefore likely to sit well below their cost of capital. For investors, Chinese AI exposure resembles a regulated infrastructure build-out with policy-driven pricing, not the sort of high-margin software or platform economics seen in US hyperscalers.

AI cloud is where this disconnect is most visible. IDC estimates that China’s AI IaaS market totaled about $2.3b in 2024 and could grow at about 40% CAGR to $12.4b by 2029.  ￼ Alibaba Cloud is the largest AI cloud provider with roughly 36% market share, followed by Huawei, the state telcos and Tencent.  Yet Alibaba’s cloud intelligence segment, while growing, is still small in the context of group earnings and carries low margins: it is projected to be about 14% of revenue and 9% of adjusted group EBITA in fiscal 2026, with segment margins of around 9% vs group margins near 16%.  ￼ The September 2025 quarter illustrates the problem: overall revenue grew 5% year-on-year to about 248b RMB, but net profit fell 52% as capex surged to 31.5b RMB and rising depreciation and energy costs weighed on results.  Free cash flow swung from a positive 2.4b USD equivalent to a 2.6b USD outflow in a single fiscal quarter.  ￼ Tencent, by contrast, derives only low-single-digit percentages of revenue from external IaaS and uses AI primarily as an internal lever to improve engagement, targeting and efficiency, resulting in more modest capex intensity and higher incremental returns.  The implication is that Chinese AI cloud as a business line is too small and too commoditized to transform group earnings, but large enough to depress free cash flow for years.

The semiconductor bottleneck shapes both China’s AI strategy and global chip investment themes. US export controls on Nvidia’s advanced GPUs have forced Chinese AI firms to pivot to domestically produced accelerators from Huawei, Baidu and Cambricon, running on 7‑nm process nodes manufactured by SMIC using DUV multi-patterning, and to rely heavily on smaller, more efficient models and software optimization.  ￼ Huawei’s Ascend 910B is viewed as the closest domestic alternative to Nvidia’s A100, but it still lags in computational throughput, memory bandwidth and ecosystem support, particularly relative to Nvidia’s CUDA, which remains the global standard AI development stack.  ￼ The inability to access EUV lithography constrains China to mature-node production for the foreseeable future, with SMIC facing low yields and high costs at 7‑nm.  ￼ This keeps China about a generation, or roughly 6 months in model-performance terms, behind US frontier models, but also catalyzes innovation in low-cost architectures such as DeepSeek’s mixture-of-experts models, which reportedly trained its V3 model for about $5.6m, 50–100x cheaper than comparable US frontier models.  ￼ For global investors, this mixture of hardware constraints and software ingenuity implies that Nvidia’s China revenue will remain structurally capped and subject to political risk, while mature-node foundries and domestic Chinese chip and equipment vendors enjoy volume growth but likely low profitability due to state-directed pricing. At the same time, efficient-model innovation will gradually reduce the compute required per unit of capability, limiting the longevity of current super-normal margins in frontier AI hardware.

On the model and export side, competition is narrowing but still two-horse. LiveBench benchmarking indicates that OpenAI’s models retain roughly a 10% performance edge and occupy 9 of the top 15 LLM positions globally as of September 2025, while Alibaba’s Qwen is the only Chinese model in that top cohort.  ￼ DeepSeek’s R1 reasoning model was briefly ranked #2 globally early in 2025 but has since been overtaken as US players released new architectures.  ￼ Despite this, the sheer number of Chinese models and the radically lower cost structure mean China can lead in “good enough” AI, particularly for non-English use cases and constrained environments. DeepSeek’s founder has resisted monetization and external capital, keeping models free and open-source, which further reinforces China’s position as a supplier of low-cost AI infra to the developing world.  ￼ ByteDance’s Doubao model, for example, has built 159m monthly active users and is now being embedded as an always-on voice assistant in smartphones such as ZTE’s Nubia line, with plans to expand across OEMs.  This suggests a 2‑tier global AI market in which Western closed models dominate premium enterprise and complex reasoning use cases in developed markets, while Chinese open or semi-open models capture share in emerging markets via cheap endpoints and super-app distribution. Over time, this dynamic will act as a global price ceiling for commoditized AI services.

The report is notably skeptical about AI’s longer-term transformational potential and strongly dismissive of near-term artificial general intelligence (AGI) narratives. It emphasizes that current LLMs are advanced pattern-recognition systems trained via stochastic gradient descent that reproduce statistical correlations in text, images and code, but lack genuine understanding, causality, subjective experience or the neuroplasticity that characterizes human brains.  ￼ Once training is complete, their architectures are comparatively fixed; updates require explicit retraining, unlike human brains which continuously rewire in response to new stimuli.  ￼ Hallucinations and bias remain endemic because models optimize for coherent output rather than truth, necessitating cost-intensive guardrails and human oversight.  ￼ A March 2025 survey of 475 members of the Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence found that 76% judged it unlikely or very unlikely that scaling current approaches alone would deliver AGI.  ￼ This undermines corporate strategies, such as Alibaba’s, that explicitly target AGI as a medium-term ambition; the report argues that AGI will require fundamental scientific breakthroughs and new model architectures, likely decades away.  ￼ For investors, equity valuations that capitalize a large AGI-driven TAM or assume dominant long-run economic rents from “superintelligence” should be treated with heightened skepticism, particularly in China where AI’s realized role is largely incremental productivity enhancement.

Consumer AI monetization in China is, in practice, negligible. The report details that China’s leading AI chatbot apps collectively generated only about $537k of iOS revenue in the 12 months to October 2025, versus $1.7b for ChatGPT, with Baidu’s Ernie Bot contributing the bulk before the company made it free in April 2025 under competitive pressure from DeepSeek’s free offerings.  ￼ Other widely downloaded AI apps – ByteDance’s Doubao and Butterfly AI, Alibaba’s Quark, Tencent’s Yuanbao – predominantly employ freemium models with extremely low price points; ByteDance’s Jimeng text-to-video app earned just $586k in August 2025 from around 6.1m active users, while Butterfly AI made about $161k that month.  ￼ Typical Chinese subscription pricing levels are much lower than Western equivalents: IQIYI’s basic plan is around 25 RMB ($3.52) per month versus Netflix’s $7.99 ad tier, illustrating structural ARPU compression.  ￼ This suggests that Chinese consumer AI should be valued primarily as a feature that drives engagement and supports advertising or e-commerce, not as a standalone revenue stream. As a result, platforms like Tencent and ByteDance can justify AI investment through higher ad yield and retention, but pure-play consumer AI apps and “AI as a product” narratives in China lack a viable path to scale monetization.

Enterprise AI software and services offer somewhat better prospects but from a small base. The report forecasts China’s AI software revenue reaching $16.5b by 2034, supported by industrial computer vision (forecast around $3.4b) and machine-learning tools ($2.7b), while AI-related services in IT and business services could reach $16.1b over the same period, with growth front-loaded in the next 5 years.  ￼ Key demand will come from state-owned enterprises and SMEs looking to modernize production lines, logistics and office workflows. However, this spending is constrained by local-government fiscal stress and price sensitivity; much of the value will be captured by domestic integrators, automation firms and IT outsourcers rather than by the internet majors. For Alibaba, Tencent and Baidu, incremental enterprise AI demand is a tailwind for cloud and consulting revenue but lacks the scale to materially alter group-level earnings trajectories.

Within this structural backdrop, the report identifies Tencent and ByteDance as the relative corporate winners, and Alibaba, Baidu, SenseTime and smaller AI/cloud players as structurally challenged. Tencent’s strategy is to apply AI horizontally across its existing businesses – games, social networks, payments, advertising – to enhance content generation, personalization, and ad targeting, while keeping external AI infrastructure exposure modest. The report expects Tencent’s earnings to grow in the low- to mid-teens in 2026 and free cash flow to grow 10–15% annually between 2025 and 2028, supported by disciplined costs and high technical barriers to entry in its core franchises.  ￼ Tencent controls about 7.6% of China’s public cloud market, a fraction of Alibaba’s share, and faces less pressure to subsidize external cloud users.  ￼ As of late 2025, Tencent trades at roughly 24–25x trailing P/E with a market cap of about HKD 5.6t, valuations that appear reasonable for a wide-moat asset with resilient cash flows and measured AI capex.  This supports an overweight stance on Tencent within a constrained China allocation and makes it an attractive long leg against weaker domestic AI names.

Alibaba presents a far more problematic investment case. Alibaba is central to the national AI strategy through its cloud arm and Qwen model family, and has announced a very large AI and cloud capex plan. However, its AI initiatives are currently destroying free cash flow rather than enhancing earnings. Cloud Intelligence revenues are growing strongly – roughly 34% year-on-year in recent quarters – but margins remain low and the segment accounts for only mid-teens of group revenue.  Meanwhile, core commerce is under structural pressure from PDD and Douyin, and new ventures in on-demand delivery, logistics and AI devices (such as the Quark AI glasses launched at 1,899 RMB) will require further investment with uncertain profitability.  The report explicitly judges Alibaba’s quest for AGI as technologically unrealistic on current approaches, implying that the 380b RMB capex program will mostly fund incremental cloud infrastructure and models with commodity-like economics.  From a portfolio perspective, Alibaba equity is best seen as a cyclical China consumption and capital-return story with AI optionality that is unlikely to monetize fully; valuations that ascribe a premium for AI leadership without discounting prolonged FCF dilution appear vulnerable. Short Alibaba vs long Tencent or vs US hyperscalers is a logical relative-value expression, subject to liquidity and borrow conditions.

Baidu and SenseTime are structurally weaker and carry significant downside risk. Baidu’s core search ad business is under attack from ByteDance’s Douyin and other platforms, with Q3 2025 advertising revenue down 18% year-on-year, contributing to an 11b RMB net loss in the quarter.  AI cloud revenue is growing at about 20–50% from a small base but remains loss-making, and robotaxis and other AI ventures show no near-term path to break even. The report expects Baidu’s adjusted net income to fall more than 32% in 2025, and notes that Baidu’s adjusted EBITDA in 2024 was only about 12% of Tencent’s, underscoring its subscale position.  Layoffs across multiple business units in late 2025 further highlight management’s need to cut costs, even as AI investment is preserved for strategic reasons.  SenseTime, despite owning significant computer vision IP and operating a 5A-rated AI data center in Shanghai, is caught in a squeeze: generative AI revenue grew 73% in 1H 2025 and total revenue rose 36% to about 2.4b RMB, but the company remains materially loss-making, with adjusted net losses only halved and free cash flow negative.  Net cash at September 2025 stood near 1.6b RMB, and the report anticipates further fundraising within 12–18 months, implying dilution risk.  ￼ For a hedge fund, Baidu and SenseTime fit more naturally into a short or underweight basket than as longs, although the possibility of state support, takeovers or policy-driven rallies requires careful risk management.

Regulatory and geopolitical risk is a pervasive overlay. Tencent has already been placed on a US list of companies with alleged ties to the Chinese military, and recent reporting indicates that the Pentagon has recommended adding Alibaba and Baidu to the same Section 1260H roster.  While inclusion does not automatically trigger sanctions, it increases reputational risk and could constrain some Western counterparties from engaging with these firms. On the technology front, US export controls on advanced chips have significantly accelerated China’s drive for indigenous semiconductors and alternative ecosystems such as Huawei’s CANN and MindSpore, but at the cost of efficiency and with lingering performance gaps.  ￼ These pressures are likely to intensify, not fade, over the investment horizon. For investors, this argues for discounting Chinese AI-related equities for sanction and decoupling risk, and for preferring exposures in jurisdictions with clearer rule of law and lower policy unpredictability.

Market pricing currently reflects more hope than reality. The report notes that, as of October 1, 2025, consensus EPS estimates for the Hang Seng Tech Index had been cut 16% in September and 25% over 6 months, yet the index had rerated meaningfully, driven by liquidity, AI excitement and expectations of a “DeepSeek-driven” growth renaissance.  ￼ Consensus now foresees a 9% EPS decline in 2025 followed by a 28% rebound in 2026, assumptions that the report judges overly optimistic given intensifying margin pressure in EVs, food delivery, e-commerce and cloud, and the structural monetization challenges in AI.  ￼ In the case of Alibaba, share price gains have become decoupled from falling EPS estimates, a classic sign of sentiment-driven exuberance.  ￼ For a hedge fund, such divergence between earnings revisions and valuations – alongside high thematic enthusiasm – is typically a fertile setup for relative shorts or for index-level hedges.

Taken together, the investment implications are clear. Within China TMT, Tencent stands out as the relatively attractive core long: AI is used to enhance entrenched, profitable franchises rather than to chase speculative AGI, external AI infrastructure is a small part of the mix, and free cash flow growth remains solid. Alibaba is a more ambiguous exposure: it is central to China’s AI infrastructure build and has meaningful long-term optionality, but near- to medium-term economics are poor due to heavy capex, weak cloud margins and competitive pressure in commerce; positioning it as a structural AI winner appears misaligned with the fundamentals. Baidu, SenseTime and other subscale AI/cloud players are structurally disadvantaged and exposed to dilution, making them better suited to underweight or short baskets, subject to risk controls. The Hang Seng Tech Index is a blunt and inefficient vehicle for AI exposure, as it bundles together high- and low-quality assets and sectors where AI is an added cost rather than a profit driver; using it as a short leg against higher-quality global AI beneficiaries is a more coherent expression of the theme.

At a global level, China’s strategy of low-cost, open-source AI diffusion acts as a deflationary force on AI software pricing and ensures that much of the surplus from AI in China accrues to users and the state, not corporate equity. It supports long-term demand for electricity, data center infrastructure, transmission networks and mature-node semiconductors, benefiting select utilities, power-equipment makers and chip-equipment vendors, but undercuts the narrative of a global AI profit supercycle centered on Chinese platforms. For US and allied markets, the report reinforces the investment case for integrated hyperscalers and software incumbents that can embed AI deeply into existing products and workflows, and for frontier chipmakers whose economics are supported by genuine performance advantages rather than policy-driven capex. Overall, the evidence suggests that China will be pivotal in spreading AI technology worldwide, particularly in emerging markets, but much less pivotal in capturing the associated equity profits, which are more likely to accrue to US and allied firms operating at the technological frontier and with structurally stronger monetization models.  ￼
author: TheValueist

title:
YouTube is quietly terminating thousands of channels right now.

And most creators still have no idea why.

Here’s the detailed breakdown of what we know triggers bans moving into 2026, and how to do
content:
YouTube is quietly terminating thousands of channels right now.

And most creators still have no idea why.

Here’s the detailed breakdown of what we know triggers bans moving into 2026, and how to do your best to stay alive:

1) Never mass-upload AI-generated or repetitive videos
The #1 termination reason right now.
If your uploads are:
• auto-generated
• repetitive
• stock-footage + AI voice
• or “AI slop”
YouTube labels it spam / inauthentic content.
Spam = channel deletion.

2) Never impersonate creators, brands, or celebrities
This is instant termination.
Use their face in a thumbnail?
Put their name in your handle?
Copy their branding or style?
If it’s not clearly parody → gone.
No grace period. No appeal. No second chances.

3) Never rely on reused or repurposed content
This is the silent demonetization killer.
If your channel is basically:
• TikTok reuploads
• Compilations
• “Reaction” videos with zero reaction
• Stock footage + AI narration
• Slightly edited versions of other creators’ videos
YouTube calls it repetitious content.
You lose monetization first. Then the channel.

4) Never run fake “LIVE” streams
Looping pre-recorded videos and pretending they’re live, especially around giveaways or crypto, triggers YouTube’s scam filters.
Once the system flags you, your entire channel gets removed.

5) Never link to shady or prohibited external sites
Malware, adult content, illegal downloads, sketchy crypto tools, or even sites associated with them, can get your video removed.
Repeat offenders get platform-wide termination.
Creators underestimate how aggressively YouTube scans outbound URLs.

6) Never copy scripts, pacing, or structure from viral videos
Ideas are fair game. Execution is not.
The algorithm can detect identical pacing, segment timing, and structural patterns across videos.
If yours matches someone else’s too closely:
copyright claim → strike → channel death.
This kills more “inspired by” creators than people think.

7) Never use unlicensed music, graphics, SFX, or footage
YouTube now cross-checks assets across massive databases.
Random TikTok sound? Struck.
Twitter clip? Struck.
Cool SFX pack you found online? Struck.
Three strikes = termination.
And repeated asset violations get the whole channel flagged as “high-risk.”

8) Never use misleading thumbnails
Fake celebrities, fake events, fake “OMG” reactions; YouTube auto-detects mismatch between thumbnail and video content in 2025/26.
Misleading = Community Guidelines strike.
Three of those ends your channell.

9) Never use unlicensed images in thumbnails
You don’t need to upload an entire video to get flagged, one thumbnail can do it.
Celebrity photos, film stills, news images, even if you add text, still require licensing.
Copyright owners can nuke the video or your entire channel.

10) Never paste the same comment across multiple videos
It doesn’t matter if you’re “just being supportive.”
Pattern repetition triggers bot detection.
Bots don’t get demonetized, they get deleted.
No warning. No time to fix anything. Just gone.

11) Never use 60+ hashtags on any upload
YouTube caps hashtags at 60 because anything above that looks like spam.
If you cross the line, the entire upload is flagged for “metadata abuse.”
Best case: your hashtags are ignored.
Worst case: your upload is removed and your channel loses trust.

12) Never keyword-stuff titles or descriptions
Old-school SEO creators still brutalize descriptions:
“MrBeast, finance, motivation, fitness, crypto, mindset…”
YouTube sees that as misleading metadata, a strikeable offense.
Three strikes → termination.

13) Never create “How to bypass YouTube” tutorials
Anything teaching viewers to:
• block ads
• hack features
• exploit YouTube systems
gets you an instant Guideline strike.
YouTube sees this as “platform integrity abuse.”
It’s one of the fastest ways to lose a channel completely.

14) Never forget the legal requirements for giveaways
You must include:
• official rules
• a privacy notice
• the line: “This giveaway is not sponsored by YouTube.”
Miss any one of these and YouTube can remove the video, and repeated violations can trigger channel-level penalties.

15) Never drop heavy profanity in the first 30 seconds
Age-restriction kills performance.
Repeat it, and YouTube labels your channel “unsuitable for advertisers.”
That alone can kill your income, and in some cases, your account.

16) Never post faceless political content
YouTube has cracked down hard on this category.
AI voices + stock footage + no on-screen identity = “non-verifiable source.”
Most channels like this don’t survive more than 90 days.

17) Never make guaranteed financial or business claims
“Guaranteed returns.”
“Passive income hacks.”
“Double your money in 30 days.”
These are flagged as deceptive practices, and repeated violations = termination.
This is one of the fastest-growing removal categories.

18) Never try to evade bans
If you get terminated and you:
• create a new channel
• reupload the same videos
• reuse branding or assets
YouTube connects the dots and deletes every connected account.
This is platform-wide, not just your YouTube login.

19) Never rack up multiple small violations
If your channel gets repeatedly flagged, even for minor stuff, you get placed into a “whole-channel abuse” category.
At that point, YouTube can terminate you without warnings, strikes, or appeals. 

Avoid these 19 things, and you'll be doing everything you can to set your channel up for safety, especially in a period where channel terminations are widespread.

Follow for more breakdowns like this.
author: 1of10media

title:
so im working on a tool that reverse engineers the youtube content system, here's what it says: 

Claiming a specific YouTube channel earned almost a million dollars based on a hypothetical calculatio
content:
so im working on a tool that reverse engineers the youtube content system, here's what it says: 

Claiming a specific YouTube channel earned almost a million dollars based on a hypothetical calculation (10x less than own earnings, $3 per 1000 views) without direct evidence or acknowledging ad revenue variability. This is presented as factual income for *that* channel.

📋 Policy: Unproven claims: Conspiracy theories → Limited/No ads

💰 Impact: Limited ads

💡 Rephrase claims about other creators' earnings to be more speculative or based on publicly available data, or state these are hypothetical maximums under best-case scenarios only. Avoid presenting hypothetical calculations as definitive earnings for other channels.

00:00-01:00
SPAM, DECEPTIVE PRACTICES & SCAMS
Misleading monetization promises
The video heavily centers around the promise of making significant money ($965,000, $1000+ from YouTube shorts) quickly and easily ('complete beginner using nothing but AI'). While not explicitly a scam, it verges on promoting unrealistic expectations for income generation.

📋 Policy: Misleading: Bait-and-switch content → Limited ads

💰 Impact: Limited ads

💡 Temper claims about income potential. Emphasize effort, skill development, and market variability. Include disclaimers that results are not typical.

00:45-01:05
SPAM, DECEPTIVE PRACTICES & SCAMS
External links to unverified tools/schemes
Suggesting joining an 'online business club' where the creator claims to give away up to $5,000 a month. This could be interpreted as promoting a potentially misleading scheme or requiring payment for access to earning opportunities.

📋 Policy: Scams: Pyramid schemes, fraud → Removal

💰 Impact: Limited ads

💡 Be transparent about the nature of the 'online business club,' if it's a paid membership, and clearly state that the $5,000 giveaway is a contest or specific promotion, not guaranteed income or a regular dividend. Ensure it complies with YouTube's contest rules if applicable.
author: heyyej_

title:
Netflix is about to get Blockbustered, and What the Streaming Service of the AI Era Will Look Like:

There seems to be agreement that the film industry is about to change in an enormous way, but how,
content:
Netflix is about to get Blockbustered, and What the Streaming Service of the AI Era Will Look Like:

There seems to be agreement that the film industry is about to change in an enormous way, but how, exactly, remains anybody’s guess. While there are too many unknown variables to calculate all of entertainment’s eventual outcomes, this essay argues that there is one possibility that looks increasingly feasible: streaming services going out of business. This piece explores both why and how this could happen, and then proposes a new business model for the world of film and TV.

An excerpt to prime you for what’s ahead:

“We predict that low-cost models providing Hollywood-level production will soon be accessible to most of the world. The number of filmmakers with access to state-of-the-art production capacity will go from a couple of thousand to tens of millions, and their creative freedom will be far greater than any artist has ever known.

As a result, we envision an internet flooded with an endless selection of phenomenal movies and TV.

When the supply of great entertainment becomes ubiquitous, the scarcity of the premier properties of streaming services will be no more, and as a consequence, the value propositions of these platforms will be destroyed.

The thesis is this: it will soon no longer make sense to pay for a streaming service. In a world of cheap and exceptional video models, stories will be free.”

In Rainbows

In the early 2000s, Radiohead was without obligation to a record label for the first time in their stardom. Simultaneously, their industry was undergoing major upheaval. Napster and other illegal streaming services meant CDs were no longer necessary to enjoy music; an internet connection was all you needed. From the start of the millennia to 2007, album sales in America took a 36% nosedive, from 785 million to 500 million per year.

The digitization of the medium threw the concept of paying for a CD out the window.

So in 2007, Radiohead stunned the world with their newest album’s distribution plan: In Rainbows was made available to download at any price, including nothing at all.

The decision to offer their work as pay-what-you-want (PWYW) was a strategic concession: Radiohead knew fans could find their music for free, regardless of how much they wanted to charge for the album. They had lost their pricing power, and tactfully admitted as much.

Their experiment paid off in spades: In Rainbows made more digital income than all of Radiohead’s previous albums combined. Spend per downloader shows us why:

62% of downloaders paid nothing.

17% paid $4 or less,

6% between $4-$8,

12% between $8-$12,

and 4% over $12.

79% of In Rainbows’ pay-what-you-want revenue came from the 16% of downloaders who paid over $8.

Put simply, the overwhelming majority of income came from the most passionate fans.

Today’s most obvious example of this phenomenon is on OnlyFans, where only 4.2% of subscribers pay any money at all. In spite of that low proportion, the platform still generated over $7.2 billion in gross revenue last year.

Music’s streaming transition shows us that as the internet expanded distribution, the cost of access plummeted to $0. Today, we stream as much music as we want for a few bucks a month, and artist revenues reflect that.

The way this applies to film is inverted: the supply of music was great, but it was without internet distribution. Today, film has internet distribution, but the supply of properties remains small.

We predict that low-cost models providing Hollywood-level production will soon be accessible to most of the world. The number of filmmakers with access to state-of-the-art production capacity will go from a couple of thousand to tens of millions, and their creative freedom will be far greater than any artist has ever known.

As a result, we envision an internet flooded with an endless selection of phenomenal movies and TV.

When the supply of great entertainment becomes ubiquitous, the scarcity of the premier properties of streaming services will be no more, and as a consequence, the value propositions of these platforms will be destroyed.

The thesis is this: it will soon no longer make sense to pay for a streaming service. In a world of cheap and exceptional video models, stories will be free.

Our trajectory supposes that these stories will be resigned to YouTube’s maximally extractive, minimally paying advertising model, but this risks reducing the revenues of filmmakers in the same way that musicians had their bottom lines slashed by streaming. Weak incentives for artists would in turn brutally underserve the potential of AI’s limitless creative bounds.

It is in this vein we propose a new system for Hollywood. In Rainbows informs – in spirit and application – the PWYW architecture we suggest for the “video model web”.

Netflix vs. YouTube and Sora 2

The domestic box office has not grossed more than $10 billion since 2019. The death of the theatres is often blamed on a lack of originality (nine of the top ten grossing films of 2024 were sequels), but @ballmatthew  suggests that this is demonstrably not the case in his essay What A Century (Plus a Pandemic) Does to Moviegoing and Why It Matters. What he shows is that per capita theatre admissions in the US have been declining since 1940. The reason: entertainment options have grown (TV, sports, concerts, video-games, social media, etc.), fewer movies are being made, and the ones that do are going from theatres to streaming faster.

People are reallocating their limited time and money away from theatres and into other things – meaning the root of less movie-going is economic, not creative.

This makes intuitive sense: for the price of a movie ticket, you can buy access to Netflix’s entire library for a month. For free you have YouTube and TikTok’s exponentially larger – albeit not as cinematic – catalogue to choose from. Outside of media, concerts continue to get better and sports continue to expand.

“Movie theatres are dead” is trite, though, and not the point of this essay. While the theatres are not in a great spot, they should not be the most worried. The most worried should be the streaming services.

The media landscape still remains – however loosely – differentiated. Theatres are great for high-octane thrills like Mission Impossible, Netflix is awesome for “bingeing” the hit new show, and YouTube works perfectly for watching funny videos. There’s still a time and place for each of these options.

It’s merely this differentiation, though, that’s holding YouTube back from total domination of the entertainment world. The Wall Street Journal has done fantastic reporting on the video giant’s rise.

In their most recent update, they demonstrate how YouTube has become the far and away leader in providing video to American TVs, and that their lead is growing.

They also show that:

(1) TV is up 10% in the last two years in YouTube’s viewing by device, taking the #1 spot from mobile, and

(2) the share of YouTube videos 15 minutes or longer being watched is also up 10% across the same time span.

In practice, this means one thing: audiences are more often choosing YouTube to watch content intentionally and for longer (a.k.a. film and TV).

What happens, then, when content in theatres, on streaming, and on YouTube becomes less differentiated, and more like perfect substitutes, as AI video models get better and more widely adopted?

Answer: YouTube takes the entirety of the American household’s TV time, and the streaming services go out of business. And while we concede that this might be hyperbole, we’d still posit that it’s directionally correct.

Capital expenditures on AI continue to accelerate — it’s abundantly clear that models are still going to get astonishingly better. For the purposes of this essay, we’re assuming that video models will improve by at least ~15x better over the next five years. 

This a massive problem for Netflix because their subscription is justified on the basis of the scarcity of quality. When you open your TV to do a family movie or keep date night going, you’re still choosing Netflix over YouTube. The options for 2 hours of entertainment on the former are simply still much better! But if in three years an auteur in Manila (who never had the means to make the move to the City of Angels) has access to all of the same tools that Jordan Peele in Hollywood has, suddenly YouTube is not just going to have way more content to choose from, but, more importantly, the quality of that content will equal that of the streamers.

We’re starting to see small glimpses of this dynamic play out. Here’s a great post from @Tocelot that compares the budgets of three animated films nominated at this year’s Oscars:

In North America, there are ~800 films and ~600 scripted TV shows released annually. If we say that AI will only enable 10,000 new filmmakers globally, and they each release just one new film or show per year, this would be a ~7x increase on current yearly output.

Newly equipped storytellers can’t and won’t wait to get distribution through streaming services, and why would they? YouTube has the biggest audience in the world. Despite its dismal revenue splits, its mostly open nature is thermodynamically aligned with the internet, and the walled gardens of the streaming services are not.

@pmarca recently echoed similar thoughts on TBPN: “If you really want to get to a billion and then five billion people, you can’t do that with a paid offering…you need an indirect business model”. While he wasn’t speaking about the rise of AI video models directly, he agrees that at scale, access must be free.

Many might argue that YouTube’s foothold on distribution is too great and that the race to build the free-to-watch platform has long been over. They are probably right. But if they are wrong, it’s because one vector of attack remains open: how much they pay their artists.

YouTube Pays Peanuts

If we agree that the future of media will continue to get pulled into YouTube’s orbit much like music got sucked into the streaming services of its era, then we should also expect compensation to storytellers to get worse along the same lines.

YouTube’s payout model is sub-optimal in two ways:

Streaming as a platform model is singularly enabled by economies of scale, where a flat fee payout structure is used to compensate artists. While admittedly practical, this architecture is forced into a grossly incorrect assumption that reduces the producer surplus of every artist or creator: that all views are equal. In Rainbows shows us how this is a critical error; it kneecaps the spend of power users, a.k.a. the most passionate fans.

YouTube pays peanuts. At a nearly 50% take rate and a payout of ~$2 per 1,000 views, compensation is grueling. Like musicians, most YouTubers don’t make any significant money from their streams, and often opt for inefficient proxies for monetizing fandom (apparel, merchandise, etc.).

Might we argue the basic premise that bottom lines can increase dramatically from where YouTube has optimized them at?

Let’s compare one of Mr. Beast’s latest videos to this summer’s Superman.

At 100 million views, Jimmy Donaldson’s video turned in about $200,000 with YouTube’s ~$2 per 1,000 views payout rate.

Superman did ~$400 million at the worldwide box office. Assuming a ticket price of ~$10, that means 40 million people went to see it.

So at 60% fewer viewers, Superman out-earned Mr. Beast by 2,000x, or $399.8 million. This mismatch is absurd.

While the future cost of accessing stories should be free, we also believe that there has to be a more competitive way of compensating storytellers. Not because we’re romantics, but because the math suggests there’s a market opportunity for someone who can better balance the scales.

So can we come up with a better way?

Shaan Puri recently said that “video is the language of the internet” (as I watched his podcast in video form), and while few would push back against this, shockingly little has been done to productize this asset.

If attention is the currency of the internet, then why is the good it flows to most (video) completely unproductive?

Which is just to say that if Mr. Beast’s core product are his videos, is it not confounding that these assets aren't assets at all?

That they’re uploaded to the ether and given to another platform to monetize altogether?

What if we could turn video on the internet into a consumer good? Might this be the way to keep stories free while providing strong enough incentives for them to be told?

Paying What You Want: Building the Streaming Service of the AI Era

To summarize thus far, we believe:

AI will increase the supply of video on the internet by orders of magnitude, rendering streaming services useless on the basis of being able to access equally good content elsewhere for free

YouTube, being the largest free-to-watch platform, will become the de facto home for all video on the planet

Advertising as a monetization model - while effective - produces vast deadweight loss that severely underpays storytellers

With these assertions in mind, our question becomes: can an adless, free, PWYW streaming platform increase creator earnings by a statistically significant margin to what YouTube pays?

We believe it can.

While the concept of paying what you want is interesting, it’s difficult to imagine a world where, on its own, it’s enough to produce meaningful revenue to filmmakers — charity only goes so far.

So then the challenge becomes whether or not one can create an ecosystem where contributions yield some amount of value to the user beyond intrinsic goodwill.

To do so, we propose a platform that allows stories to be watched by all and “owned” by some — turning film and TV into instruments for both fandom and cultural capital.

What’s created is a streaming service built for the AI era: where watching, sharing with friends, and having a stake in the stories and filmmakers you love are all built under one, synchronous umbrella.

It starts by making every video open to contribution for at least 1 cent, and up to an unlimited amount.

In exchange for contribution, the user receives a digital good: a card that represents the video they just contributed to.

The rarity of this digital card is directly relative to the contributions of all other users for that video. Displayed directly to the user’s profile upon contribution, these digital cards are a demonstration to their network of the artists they support and to what degree.

The majority of viewers on any given video will contribute nothing. But this is okay. This platform builds an avenue for, and captures the upside of the most passionate fans, not of the average.

Let’s use the Mr. Beast video from earlier to more clearly demonstrate the concept:

If I finished watching Mr. Beast’s video and I either:

(a) liked it and want to make sure he has the means to keep making more of them,

(b) liked it and want my friends to know that I liked it,

(c) liked it and think more people will come to like it (or him) over time, or

(d) all of the above (and more),

then on the platform we propose, I’d be incentivized to contribute some amount of money to the video. The video card (a) financially supports something I enjoy consuming, (b) functions as a signal of my personal taste to my friends, and (c) is an asset that belongs to me that I can choose to keep or sell over time.

The card I get for my contribution should be relative to the contributions of everybody else, so as to successfully function as a signal of fandom. A contribution less than the 50th percentile earns a Grey Card, between the 50th and 75th percentile earns a Brown Card, between the 75th and 90th a Green Card, and so on and so forth until the paramount Gold Card. What gets created is an easily interpretable visual system that quickly communicates how passionate the owner is of that video.

But we think we can create even more demand for paying what you want by going one level deeper. Actualizing video and giving it “physical” form by turning it into a digital artifact shouldn’t stop there — we think Video Cards should serve as the basis for the true prize: Storyteller Cards. For instance, if my total contributions to Mr. Beast video cards in 2025 were in the 95th percentile, I would earn his storyteller Red Card for that year.

As more filmmakers join the platform, releasing more stories and creating more cards, two things are born:

(1) A place where users can show their friends their taste / the things they really like to watch, and

(2) an ecosystem of digital assets that act as cultural encapsulations of their time, whose value can grow, stay the same, or depreciate as interest in the video or artist changes.

On (1), we think a lot of evidence suggests robust demand exists for platforms that allow people to share what they consume. Beli, an app where users post their rankings and reviews of restaurants went from 2.5M ratings in 2022 to 58M ratings in 2025. Strava, the app where runners share their workouts, has added 50M users from the 95M they had existing in 2022. Spotify’s annual “Wrapped” campaign, where people post their music taste for all to observe, is the largest growth bump for the streamer every year. Users love the chance to show others what they’re consuming or doing, and a platform that builds social signal around video consumption could have legs under this guise.

And on (2), while we believe this platform can exist wholly without speculation of any kind, we think this system makes online cultural capital investable in a powerful way. It creates a means by which a film or TV show’s cultural grip can be denominated in capital by giving it digital figure. What would an Oppenheimer Gold Card, or a 2023 Greta Gerwig Purple Card fetch in 25-50 years? How much contribution would a Logan Paul video inspire today? Natural laws indicate that the majority of the time, we will not contribute any money at all to the stories we watch. But for those stories that resonate in a special way, or that speak to us immeasurably, or inspire the way we live our lives, we’d likely be willing to contribute some amount more than $0. For example, if I could have collected Denis Villeneuve’s Arrival upon its release, a Gold Card might be something I would have treasured.

We see this concept as a way to create the baseball cards (or cultural artifacts) of the future. For as long as collectors choose to keep it in their digital possession, those viewers will have artifacts representative of the storytellers they love and that were popular in this epoch of culture forever.

Further, this framework is uniquely advantageous to unknown artists. Users are incentivized to seek out small filmmakers because if they are early and right, this system gives them the means to tangibly realize the acuity of their taste in the future. We can align both the filmmaker and the contributor around growth by building royalties into video and storyteller cards, allowing artists to reap the benefits of continued popularity while contributors maintain the option of selling their cards.

We think the most important takeaway of what we propose is that our system turns video on the internet into a real product - something that we believe became an inevitability from the moment moving picture was put on the web. While ad models allow for monetization, we think this is commerce for video in its most rudimentary form, chiefly because it doesn’t allow for price discovery. Under our model, stories on the internet finally become true assets to the filmmakers that create them.

Timing is a funny thing, and we’re not sure anybody quite grasps the potency of the “Why Now?”. We’ve spent much of the essay on the proliferation of video models, but equally important is the quiet rise of the financial infrastructure that can harness AI and act as an antidote to the slopification of the internet. Of course, we’re referring to the maturation of high throughput, low fee blockchain architectures, and the rapid adoption of stablecoins. Finally, purely digital commerce on crypto rails has become economically viable, turning concepts like the one we’ve outlined from theoretical pipe dreams to conceivable experiments.

The film industry today is siloed, unstable, and confused. The claims of a shift to short-form content as a consequence of dwindling attention spans seems plausible, but perhaps more a function of economics than the rewriting of any underlying biology. Movies (and TV) are alive and well, but stuck in the mire of an awkward transition to the internet. Where distribution has achieved maximum reach, production costs remain as (mostly) an insurmountable barrier to entry. Almost all in Hollywood oppose AI as a tool in the creative process, but we think it might just be the technology that brings the spirit back into the business of storytelling. As the capacity to make stories goes from the few to the many, the great films and television shows of our era will be determined by the selection among the infinite by the populace, and no longer by the executive decision of a slate by a small group of the ideologically homogenous. We simply must ensure that the economic infrastructure is in place to fully support the potential of this creative renaissance. If we can find a way, our children will work harder, love better, and dream higher, reestablishing the fundamental optimism necessary to the achievement and prosperity of a nation and race.

Few things could be as important.

Epilogue

I could keep going for hours about this topic, but this feels like the best place to stop. If you’d like to refute any of what I’ve laid out here, please shoot me an email at gptsiolis[at]gmail[dot]com.

I don’t plan on committing to any sort of writing cadence. I write whenever I feel like I have something valuable to share. With that said, I invite you to subscribe if you’d like to receive any of my essays directly to your inbox.

I think for my next piece, I’m going to approach a similar topic from a very different angle. It will be something like, “Going Long Gaming In A World Where AI Replaces Work But Doesn’t Achieve Superintelligence”. It will probably opine on the value and growth of live entertainment (Twitch, Kick, etc.) and its inevitable intersection with prediction markets to produce a new form of media as society enters what I envision as “The Era of Games”.

For a separate essay, I have some thoughts on “quantum” health. The Telepathy Tapes, Bryan Johnson’s Don’t Die campaign, Warren Buffett drinking 3 cokes a day, chronic illness, and quantum entanglement all point, to me, to the possibility that we are overlooking some key features to life and love.

About the author: George Tsiolis is an LA-based new grad with a Bachler of Science in Business Administration from USC’s Marshall School of Business (‘25). I was the first hire at Fountain, a digital art brokerage, roughly two years ago, and have been with the company since. Fountain has brokered some of the largest deals in digital art history, with many deals over $3M and two deals over $12M. I like soccer, movies, and libertarianism, among other things.
author: gptsiolis

title: $WMT $NVDA @RealJimChanos The earnings call conveyed a clear message that AI – and specifically agentic, conversational AI layered on top of Walmart’s data and automation footprint – is moving from ex
content:
$WMT $NVDA @RealJimChanos The earnings call conveyed a clear message that AI – and specifically agentic, conversational AI layered on top of Walmart’s data and automation footprint – is moving from experimentation to being a central pillar of Walmart’s growth and efficiency strategy. The discussion covered customer-facing generative/agentic use cases, internal productivity and cost reduction, data advantages, and the OpenAI partnership. There were no explicit references to data centers, cloud computing vendors, networking infrastructure, or Nvidia; the framing remained at the application, data, and business-model layers rather than the underlying hardware or cloud stack.

Customer-facing generative and agentic AI: Sparky, ChatGPT, and “agentic commerce”

The most substantive discussion around generative AI centered on the emerging paradigm of “agentic” shopping experiences and the tools Walmart is deploying to enable it. The term “agentic” comes from the analyst question (“do you think agentic will supercharge Walmart’s eComm growth?”), but management fully embraced it and used the Q&A to lay out a vision in which AI agents orchestrate end-to-end shopping.

Doug McMillon described the destination for Walmart’s eCommerce experience “as it relates to AI” as having 3 core attributes: more personalized and relevant; multimodal across voice, text, image, and video in a “more conversational” interface; and contextual, “understanding customer intent and anticipating needs to save them time.” He linked this explicitly to a richer front-end experience – “improved imagery, short-form video, live streaming, and interaction with influencers” – in which ads become “more contextual and helpful … surfacing as recommendations or sponsored bundles that add value.” This description aligns with a generative AI layer that synthesizes content, recommendations, and promotions into a coherent, conversation-driven shopping flow, rather than the legacy model of static search results and banner ads.

The call highlighted 2 concrete customer-facing AI constructs. First, Sparky, described by John Furner as “our digital agent that’s live in the app,” is already in market and “continues to hit new milestones.” Sparky is explicitly framed as an “agentic AI” system that will, over the next few months, “take more action on behalf of our customers.” Furner emphasized several types of actions: reminding customers about reorders and “nudges about staying in stock” at home; serving as a front-end layer over Walmart’s “forward-deployed inventory all around the country” to enable service “in minutes”; and working behind the scenes to “maintain the accuracy of our catalog” and “help us know where there are gaps in our assortment.” This is effectively a single agentic framework spanning both customer interaction and internal data integrity.

Second, McMillon called out the new OpenAI partnership as an example of when Walmart chooses to partner rather than build in-house. He explained that “this new partnership will allow customers and members to purchase items from Walmart and Sam’s Club directly through ChatGPT.” The initial implementation is “relatively simplistically with the checkout process,” but management expects it to “become more immersive, integrated, and seamlessly connected experiences that bring Walmart closer to customers in new ways.” Taken together with the Sparky comments, the call makes it clear Walmart intends to be present both in its own app (Sparky) and in third-party AI environments (ChatGPT), with both channels using generative models to orchestrate discovery, recommendations, and transactions.

The international “Carrito Listo” initiative in Chile is an important proof point of agentic commerce at scale. Kathryn McLay described it as a system where Walmart “actually create[s] customers’ orders for them, send[s] them a WhatsApp prompt to ask them if they’re interested in buying that basket,” and then presents a pre-built basket in the app containing “all the brands that they normally buy in the kind of intervals that they like to buy it.” Customers can then accept, modify, or reject the AI-generated basket. McLay stated that this capability has already become “up to about 20% of our eComm business in Chile.” The fact that a single agentic feature accounts for roughly 1/5 of eCommerce in that market indicates a high level of consumer acceptance and provides empirical support for the broader agentic strategy with Sparky and ChatGPT.

Personalization and data advantages as AI fuel

Management repeatedly connected Walmart’s AI roadmap to the depth and breadth of its data. McLay noted that Walmart has “all of the data from our customers who shop with us in-store every day, and all of that transactional data is really helpful for us to be able to predict baskets for our customers.” Carrito Listo in Chile is essentially a use case of this: using historical basket data to pre-assemble future baskets with the right brands and purchase intervals. The strong adoption there demonstrates that the data is rich enough to generate baskets that feel highly accurate and convenient.

On the membership side, Chris Nicholas highlighted that Sam’s Club’s member data is “richer than any other form of data we have across the enterprise because we have complete understanding of what the member wants.” That “complete understanding” is a function of closed-loop identity: Sam’s has a high share of member spend, identity-linked across online and offline, allowing for more precise personalization than in open-web environments. Nicholas stressed that this allows Sam’s to “personalize at an even deeper level,” which, in the context of the broader call, implies that Sam’s is a prime candidate for rolling out agentic experiences such as personalized recommendations, occasion planning, and dynamic pricing.

More broadly, McMillon said Walmart is “putting our data to work” by “building more capable tech products and platforms” and “deploying physical automation.” The language suggests that Walmart’s AI efforts are not a set of isolated experiments, but integrated into a broader data platform strategy. AI is portrayed as a way to activate existing data assets (transaction history, inventory, supply chain telemetry) to create better customer experiences and lower costs, rather than as a standalone initiative.

Internal AI: software development, automation, cost and efficiency

The call provided several data points that internal AI adoption is already significant, particularly in software development and supply chain operations.

On the software development side, McMillon stated that “when AI is used for software development, more than 40% of the new code is either AI-generated or AI-assisted.” This is a very high penetration rate for AI assistance and suggests that Walmart has broadly rolled out tools such as code copilots across its engineering organization. At that level of usage, AI is likely impacting development speed, defect rates, and the ability to customize and iterate on internal tools and customer-facing features, including Sparky and logistics systems. Given Walmart’s scale as a technology buyer and builder, 40%+ AI-assisted new code implies a material uplift in engineering productivity that should compound over time.

On the operations and supply chain side, AI and automation were repeatedly cited as key drivers of efficiency and SG&A leverage. McMillon described “investments to automate our supply chain” as “going well,” helping stores “receive and manage inventory better than before.” John David Rainey was more explicit, stating that “technology and AI have been enablers of efficiency gains” and that “we’re using AI across the organization to manage cost effectively and to accelerate our growth.” He connected this directly to automation metrics: in Walmart U.S., “more than 60% of our stores are now receiving some freight from automated distribution centers,” and “more than 50% of our eCommerce fulfillment center volume is now automated.” He also highlighted that shipping costs have been “down consistently for many quarters in the 30% range” and that Q3 delivered “another quarter where we saw double-digit improvements” in shipping costs.

When pressed later on operating leverage, Rainey used the supply chain as his primary example, indicating that the combination of AI and automation is substantial enough to contribute meaningfully to SG&A leverage at the enterprise level. The fact that Walmart is achieving both high-20s eCommerce growth and sustained reductions in per-order delivery costs indicates that AI-optimized routing, inventory placement, and labor scheduling are working at scale.

Furner linked AI directly to assortment management and inventory accuracy. He cited “digital agents that are helping us understand how our assortments can improve” and “investments we’ve made over the last few years … in terms of supply chain and inventory accuracy” as drivers of higher NPS and improved delivery performance. Behind-the-scenes, he noted that “agentic AI and other types of technology” are maintaining catalog accuracy and identifying assortment gaps. These capabilities are critical enablers for long-tail marketplace growth, where catalog quality and accurate attributes directly influence search and recommendation quality.

AI in workforce enablement and culture

The call also touched on AI as a workforce and culture initiative, not just a technology program. McMillon highlighted efforts to “help our associates build the skills they’ll need to thrive in an AI-powered workplace,” explicitly referencing “embracing OpenAI certifications” and “rolling out ChatGPT enterprise licenses.” That suggests a deliberate push to normalize AI usage across white-collar roles at Walmart, including engineering, merchandising, finance, and operations.

This matters for two reasons. First, broad-based AI literacy and tooling increases the likelihood of incremental, bottom-up productivity gains as employees embed AI into everyday workflows (analysis, content creation, decision support) beyond centrally managed use cases. Second, it reinforces the messaging to investors that Walmart is not treating AI as a siloed innovation lab but as an organization-wide capability. The language in the call positions Walmart as transitioning into a “people-led, tech-powered” company, and AI is the connective tissue between the workforce and the technology stack.

Financial impact and linkage to eCommerce and profit growth

The call repeatedly linked AI and automation to financial outcomes, both on the growth and margin sides. On the growth side, AI is portrayed as a key enabler of sustained high-20s eCommerce growth and of broader omnichannel engagement. Rainey cited that in Walmart U.S., “approximately 35% of store-fulfilled orders were expedited or delivered in under three hours,” with sales through these expedited channels up “nearly 70%.” That level of convenience is made possible by AI-driven inventory and routing optimization; without it, the cost to offer sub-3-hour delivery across a national footprint would be prohibitive. Faster delivery in turn supports the value proposition underlying membership (e.g., Walmart+), drives higher order frequency, and makes the ecosystem stickier for high-income households.

In Q3, eCommerce growth above 20% in all segments was achieved while maintaining – and in some cases expanding – margins. Consolidated adjusted operating income grew 8% in constant currency, faster than the 6% sales growth. Management explicitly credited improved eCommerce economics, advertising, and membership for this outcome. AI plays a role in each of these: in lowering cost to serve online orders, in improving ad relevance and attribution (which supports 53% advertising revenue growth), and in optimizing the design and marketing of membership benefits.
