But once you understand how to leverage it, you ca
content:
How to Make Money on X

X is a powerful way of making money online.

No, it isn't a shortcut to easy money. You need a solid framework and patience.

But once you understand how to leverage it, you can turn X into a money-making machine.

Here's how to do it:

1. Make a Strong Impression

To make money on X, you need to ‘tattoo’ yourself onto people’s minds.

Establishing a polished, relatable, brand should be your priority.

Consider the following:

- Does my profile convey my identity, my expertise, and the benefits I offer?
- Is my content aligned with my brand's tone?
- Is there a uniform look and feel to my content?

Your objective is to design a welcoming environment that resonates with your desired audience.

A strong, confident persona enhances the chances of drawing in followers who can transform into potential customers.

2. Showcase Your Knowledge

Building a reputation as an expert in your niche is all about quality content creation.

Everything you post is a chance to highlight your expertise.

Begin by understanding your audience's pain points.

Then offer solutions that are both enlightening and practical. It might be a handy "how-to" video or an inspirational case study.

By regularly offering outstanding content, you bolster your trustworthiness.

And when people trust you, the doors to monetization swing open.

3. Interact and Build Relationships

Interacting with your X followers transforms you from a digital persona to a genuine individual.

Start discussions, reply to feedback, and truly care about your followers' perspectives. Welcome queries and offer considerate responses.

The deeper your engagement, the more your followers feel acknowledged and valued. This nurtures a profound community bond and builds trust which is pivotal for monetization.

When people feel a connection to you, they're more inclined to invest in what you offer.

4. Build an Email List

Email lists often get sidelined in the realm of social media marketing.

But I cannot overstress how fundamental they are.

Without one, you will always be at the mercy of unpredictable social media algorithms.

Start your list early and begin collecting emails even before you launch a product. Even with a modest following, get started.

Incentivise your audience to sign up by offering a free tool or resource in return for their email, or provide early bird privileges.

This list, over time, morphs into a potent channel for showcasing your products or services.

5. Showcase Your Offerings

With a firm foundation of trust in place, it's time to spotlight your product, service, or proposal.

But it's essential not to come off as too pushy.

Strategically drop in how your product or service can help, being sure to position it as a solution to their problems.

Highlight these advantages in your communications. Be candid about the value you bring, and let your genuine passion be evident.

Whenever you receive glowing reviews and positive feedback, make them public.

It’s all about helping your audience recognize the value your product or service brings and what better way to do that than sharing the success stories of your existing customers.

With an army of raving fans at your back it’s easy to convey the benefits without coming across as salesy.

6. Consistently Exceed Expectations

I can't stress this enough.

Always provide unmatched value.

Whether you're offering a course, a digital item or professional advice, ensure that what you provide surpasses expectations.

When people recognise that they're receiving a high return on their investment, they’ll come back for more and sing your praises from the rooftops.

7. Create Multiple Streams of Income

Business can be brutal.

This is especially true in the online world when social media platforms are your main route to market.

You’re always at the mercy of their algorithms and one tweak can stop your marketing machine in it’s tracks.

To avoid this you should create multiple revenue streams.

This way, if one income stream grinds to a halt you have other streams that are unaffected.

I'm not suggesting you do everything all at once but over time, thoughtfully integrate additional revenue channels as your online venture flourishes.

For inspiration on creating multiple streams of income, take a look at this post, where I discuss ways to monetise your online venture.
https://t.co/Inhf2MpqBJ

Can you integrate a combination of these into your approach?

Maybe you kick off with some simple affiliate marketing and once you have built traction introduce a paid course.

Or you could begin with offering mentorship, later branching into an exclusive members' circle.

Whichever model you choose, aim to create multiple income streams. This has the dual effect of boosting your profits and providing a safety net, ensuring your growth isn't tethered to just one channel.

Build Your Business

When you’re just starting out it can be difficult to understand how to generate an income through social platforms.

But keep in mind the following:

Your worth isn't determined by the number of followers you have, but by the difference you make in people’s lives.

Every relationship you build and every piece of content you produce builds your online presence.

This path is about leaving a lasting impression, delivering unparalleled value, and bringing genuine authenticity.

Armed with this knowledge, you're ready to make money on X.

If you're serious about online business, join the waitlist for the upcoming AI Startup newsletter.

You'll get all the knowledge and tools you need to launch with a bang.

Join here: https://t.co/OkhIYiwWn2

title:
Our COO @acailic123 wrote a kickass guide on monetizing your NFT collection in the post-royalties era - check it out.

You can read it the entire story here, or on our website:

🔗 https://t.co/TZZ8mK7
content:
Our COO @acailic123 wrote a kickass guide on monetizing your NFT collection in the post-royalties era - check it out.

You can read it the entire story here, or on our website:

🔗 https://t.co/TZZ8mK7I9l

---

Last month, @OpenSea announced they're dropping NFT royalty fees—for good.

Considering OpenSea's market dominance, if you're a creator looking to create recurring income, you might be wondering what to do next.

Should you churn out new #NFTs, and likely devalue your #OG collection?

If you're looking for alternatives that are not likely to hurt your collection, but actually add value to it—I've got your back.

The strategies I'm about to share will help you to see your #NFT community not as repeat buyers, but as essential members of your project who can actively help you grow, and reach a broader audience—even those that are not a part of the web3 crowd.

I've put together 6 powerful, stable NFT monetization strategies, all featuring both actionable steps and real-life examples, to help you navigate the post-royalties landscape.

Let's fucking go.💨

👕 #1: Wearable Merch-Fashion Meets the Blockchain

Turning your unique NFT artwork into wearable merchandise like t-shirts, hoodies, or hats isn't just about slapping your PFP on fabric and selling it—it's about creating a real-world, logical, meaningful extension of your digital collection.

Examples
From licensed BAYC merch selling in Walmart, to direct, mainstream collaborations like 3land's partnership with H&M, the blend of NFT and fashion is far from a novelty.

Pros and Cons
The beauty of wearable merch lies in amplifying your brand awareness and capitalizing on potentially high gross margins, often around 40%. The journey, however, isn't without its hurdles. The path from pixels to fabric demands intricate design work and the challenge of either doing everything yourself or partnering with the right clothing brand.

Step-by-Step Execution

Design Concept: Test and blend different styles, colors, and elements to create designs that resonate with your collection's vibe. Pro tip: Use https://t.co/GgpygJ2Ovj, a super-simple web app, to quickly experiment with different designs in 3D.

Select Merchandise: Choose from wearables like t-shirts, hoodies, hats, and more, that best suit your artwork. Pro tip: Listen to your community about the type of wearables they would like to get—set up a simple Discord vote.

Design Adaptation: Tailor your NFT designs to the chosen clothing, ensuring coherence with dimensions and materials.

Prototyping: Create a few prototypes and order them as samples to test out, ensuring that the designs are well-translated into physical products.

DIY or Partner Up: Creating a tangible connection between your NFT artwork and the physical world can be approached in two ways:

DIY - Use Printful + Shopify: Combine @Printful manufacturing and distribution capabilities with @Shopify online store solutions. Check out this full guide and Printful NFT merch page to get started.

Pro tip: If you want to offer special merch only to NFT owners, consider applying for Shopify token-gating Beta, or check out thirdweb's NFT-gating guide.

Partner Up with Established Brands or Local Manufacturers: While collaborating with @ZARA might be tricky, you could always try cold-emailing local clothing brands and asking for a partnership. This approach leverages their manufacturing and distribution networks, including both online shops and physical stores.

🧸#2: Toys—Bringing NFT Characters to Life

While the journey from pixel to toys may seem more complex than clothing, the process is actually simple. It goes without saying that this one will only work with PFP collections—so if you've created a cute anime, a sci-fi character, or some other humanoid or creature-themed collection, read on.

Examples
@pudgypenguins stands as an epic example of an NFT collection that has nailed this strategy, turning digital characters into amazingly cute plushies, selling toys online on Amazon, and in over 2000 Walmart stores.

Pros and Cons
Toys can increase your collection's value and attract non-web3 audiences, but the logistical intricacies of manufacturing and shipping shouldn't be underestimated. Embracing this strategy means you're not just running an NFT project; you're now operating a toy company.

Step-by-Step Execution

Audience and Toy Type: Define the target audience for your toys (kids, adults, collectors) and select the type of toys that resonate with your NFT characters (plushies, action figures, construction sets, dolls, etc.).

Design Ideation: Brainstorming sessions to craft toy designs. More than one, and don't stop until the entire team is completely on board. You can start with a rough sketch, and hire a 3D modeler or even use @kaedim3d to get a rough 3D model prototype.

Prototyping: 3D printing is your friend—you can check out  for high-quality prints, go to @shapeways to get an affordable quote, or simply Google for your closest 3D printing shop.

Manufacturer Partnership: Select a reputable manufacturer, considering crucial factors like quality, materials, and production costs.

Pro tip: Finding a decent manufacturer via googling is a nightmare—you'll just get hit with countless ads. Instead, go to @Kickstarter, find successful toy projects (that match the type of toy you want to sell), and contact their founders to ask for manufacturer info.

Production and Distribution: You need a website (again, Shopify is a decent e-commerce platform, but you can also look at @webflow) to sell online; for physical stores, you can also take the tip from the previous strategy, and contact local brands.

🥷 #3: Comic Books - A Path for NFT Storytelling

In the grand tale of NFTs, comic books have started to emerge as a unique approach for monetizing and creating value in the NFT space.

There are two ways you can go about this:
NFT Comic Books: These can be either composable (where individual NFTs combine to create pages, and pages combine to create the full comic) or encapsulate the entire comic in one NFT.

Physical Comic Books: Available to order online or buy in stores, this somewhat traditional approach demands direct partnerships and printing resources, adding a layer of trickiness.

Examples in Action
NounsDAO's collaboration with Titan Comics demonstrates how existing NFT collections can be leveraged to forge new intellectual property, while the 2142 project illustrates a creative convergence where NFTs and DAOs contribute directly to the crafting of a narrative universe.

Step-by-Step Execution

Decide the Format: Choose between an NFT or physical comic, or combine both—e.g. buying an NFT enables you to claim a physical comic book.

Find an Artist & Create the Comic: You can hire talented individuals on Fiverr, or create comics with AI tools like Midjourney and ChatGPT.

(Optional) Develop a Revenue Sharing Model: This could range from simple crypto payments to launching a DAO and related coin. If you decide to take this route, it's crucial to ensure you're in compliance with any applicable laws and regulations.

Engage Your Community: Leverage your existing NFT holders to promote and participate in the comic’s success.

Publish and Distribute: Whether through an NFT marketplace or physical stores, ensure your comic reaches its audience.

Pro tip: You can always reach out to us at Holoframe, in case you want to launch an AR marketing campaign for your comic book NFTs.

📘 #4 Books

Books do more than just add layers to your NFT universe; they're a gateway to reach an audience beyond the crypto-savvy. By diving into the lore and stories behind your NFTs, you not only captivate your existing community but also pull in people who are new to digital art but love a compelling narrative.

Financially, it's a savvy move. Publishing opens up a new revenue channel, enhancing your NFT project's overall worth.

Examples
@yugalabs  did a physical book about CryptoPunks. Even Mark Cuban is doing NFT books. Take a look at sites such as @Publica, @creatokia, @soltype_io, @AlexandriaLabs, @readl_co, or @book_io for inspiration.

Step-by-Step Execution

1. Conceptualize Your Story: Determine your book's setting and characters, mapping out key events and subplots. Choose themes like freedom or love, and decide on the story format—a single book or series. Pro tip: Involve your community members in shaping story elements, via Discord votes and idea channels.

2. Write Your Manuscript: Decide on your writing approach, whether solo, using ChatGPT, or hiring a ghostwriter. Ensure a polished, error-free manuscript through self-proofreading, team review, or professional editing.

3. Design and Format: Create a compelling book cover that's in line with your NFT collection's art style. Be mindful of layout, typography, and formatting since your book might live in both digital and physical realms. Aim for a design that is compatible with both the mainstream and NFT book platforms.

4. Publish and Promote: For traditional readers, self-publish on platforms like @AmazonKDP (KDP) or even consider hard copies. Simultaneously, and ideally, tokenize your book on NFT book platforms like @AlexandriaLabs, @soltype_io, @Publica, @creatokia, @book_io, or @readl_co.

Distribute through multiple channels, bolstered by a well-thought-out marketing campaign that targets both the crypto-savvy and those who might find the book interesting due to its genre, like sci-fi or fantasy.

👽 #5 Animated Series

Animated series can supercharge your NFT collection IP and reach. A good one can attract a wide audience, from animation fans to NFT collectors, and offer multiple revenue channels, including NFT sales, IP licensing, and merchandise.

Examples
@stonercatstv was an adult animated series starring celebrity felines, and it's exclusive to those who hold Stoner Cat NFTs. @DeadHeadsTV, on the other hand, merges 3D NFT characters with an animated show, allowing NFT holders to sway the storyline.

The @therealgimmicks serves as an adult comedy set in the workplace, featuring @WWE stars and empowering NFT holders to influence the plot. @Krapopolis by Dan Harmon blends gods, humans, and monsters in its animated narrative, with @FOXTV launching companion NFTs for fans.

Step-by-Step Execution

1. Story, Story, Story: Begin by developing a captivating storyline that seamlessly integrates your NFT characters and universe. Consider the narrative arc, character development, and overarching themes to ensure a compelling story.

2. Script and Storyboard: Write the script for your animated series, breaking down each episode into scenes and dialogue. Create storyboards that visualize the key moments, camera angles, and character actions. This step provides a blueprint for the animation process.

As with any other type of creative work, you can choose to do this yourself, use ChatGPT to help or hire a freelancer. If you choose to do it yourself, you can combine tools like @midjourney with @canva, @milanote, or even @figma to create a decent, straightforward storyboard.

3. Production: To actually produce your show, you've got two choices:
- Hire an animation studio or a freelancer
- Do it yourself

If you choose the DIY route, the quickest and simplest route to take is to generate it with AI—try combining @midjourney with @runwayml Gen2. It's going to be obvious you've used artificial intelligence, but that could also be your main selling point.

4. Post-Production: After animation, focus on post-production tasks such as sound design, voice acting, and visual effects. You can hire voice actors, or use services like Typecast or @ReplicaStudios to generate voice over with AI. https://t.co/pMUib7Jvg3 and https://t.co/4lLzLyhItp

5. Distribution and Promotion: Explore distribution channels for your animated series, whether on streaming platforms,  or your own website. A solid strategy would be to publish exclusively on @X, and monetize via subscriptions - but you could also go for @YouTube , or even @tiktok_us, and monetize via views. Develop a marketing strategy that includes teasers, trailers, social media promotion, and engagement with your NFT community.

🎮 #6: Video Games

Integrating your NFTs into video games isn't just a trendy move; it's a strategic one. It opens up new avenues for user engagement, provides a unique utility for your NFTs beyond mere collectability, and brings a new way to monetize by launching a crypto token usable in your game.

Step-by-Step Execution

1. Define Your NFT's Role in the Gaming Ecosystem:
The most crucial first step is to decide what role your NFTs will assume in the gaming world. They can serve multiple purposes, ranging from items, characters, and real estate, to vehicles, spells, and even NPCs. A simple, smart move could be to make your existing NFTs playable while crafting new collections for additional features and items.

2. Choose the Right Web3 Game Development Platform: To seamlessly integrate NFTs into games, you'll need a robust platform designed for this kind of heavy lifting. Two standout options are @thirdweb and @Immutable. Thirdweb offers a plethora of customizable smart contract templates, while Immutable is known for its gas-free transactions and scalability. Either platform could serve as a powerful cornerstone for your project.

3. Select Your Game Development Engine: The game engine you opt for can either make or break your project. @unity and @godotengine are well-established engines that have extensive community support and libraries. If you're aiming for something less conventional, platforms like @GDevelopApp and @ConstructTeam are also viable alternatives. Each has its own set of tools and features to match different project needs and skill levels.

Pro tip: @CoreGames is a simple-to-use game builder featuring out-of-the-box Ethereum NFT support.

4. Development & Testing: After choosing your platforms and engine, the next step is the actual development. This stage involves implementing the smart contracts for your NFTs into the game mechanics. Your choice of web3 game development platform will come in handy here, providing the necessary infrastructure to tie everything together.

5. User Engagement & Feedback Loop: Before officially launching, it's crucial to listen to your user base. Implement beta testing phases and solicit feedback. Fine-tune your game and NFT utility based on these insights.

6. Rollout & Continued Development: Once you've made the necessary adjustments, it's time for the big launch. However, the work doesn't stop here. The gaming and NFT worlds are ever-evolving; continually assess how your NFTs are being used and make improvements accordingly.

Examples
Two initiatives perfectly illustrate the evolution of NFT collections towards gaming: @OthersideMeta by @yugalabs and @BrawlerBearz. Otherside has taken web3 by storm, generating $319 million from 55,000 Otherdeed NFTs. On the more agile end of the spectrum, Brawler Bearz adds layers of utility to its NFTs by offering a browser-based game where owners can stake their bears, engage in PVP combat, and set off on quests.

Beyond the Mint: Building a Thriving Ecosystem Around Your NFTs

We've explored everything from merch to toys and comics, and even thought about animated worlds for your NFTs. But it's more than just making money. It's about building a community that's as passionate about your project as you are.

Whether you're putting your art on T-shirts or crafting an animated story, remember: the key to success is understanding that you're not just expanding utility for your NFTs; you're building an ecosystem.

Want us to give you more examples or deep-dive into any of these strategies? Tell us what you're curious about on X.

Next Steps:
Revisit Your Business Model: Assess the health of your current monetization strategy in the post-royalties world.

Community Polling: Engage your community on Discord or X to gauge interest in various new projects and monetization methods.

Prototyping: Whether it's designing a piece of merch or drafting a comic, the prototype phase is essential. Use it to gather feedback before full-scale production.

Launch and Iterate: Release your new monetization strategy and make data-driven adjustments as you go.

So, ready to break the mold and redefine what an NFT project can achieve? Let's fucking go.  🔥
author: holoframe

title:
Starting a newsletter is a big task

AI has made it easier

I'm not talking 100 % AI generated content. That's a race to the bottom. 

Instead use AI alongside your own creativity to make a great news
content:
Starting a newsletter is a big task

AI has made it easier

I'm not talking 100 % AI generated content. That's a race to the bottom. 

Instead use AI alongside your own creativity to make a great newsletter without it taking up all your time

Let's go:

Setting up a new business and adding an income steam is not easy. It’s not meant to be easy.

But you follow along and ACT (rather than just read) and you’ll have the basic foundations of a newsletter business in place.

In brief here’s the week:

Part 1: Understanding Newsletters (this post)

Part 2: Setting up your newsletter

Part 3: Content Creation with AI - Part 1

Part 4: Content Creation with AI - Part 2 & Audience Building

Part 5: Monetization: Advertising and Sponsorships

Let’s get started:

1. Identify Your Interests and Skills
Use this prompt:

>>As a brainstorming assistant, generate a list of 50 potential newsletter niches based on my interests in [insert interests here] combined with my skills [insert skills here] <<

The goal is to come up with unique and engaging content niche that combine these interests and skills. 

Combine where they naturally intersect but do not force combinations. Please avoid overly wide niches.

Sit down with a pen and paper (I know, medieval!) and sketch out your interests and skills.

Why paper? It’s easier to avoid self editing.
Just go nuts and get as much down on paper as possible. We’ll let ChatGPT do the sorting.

If something falls under interest and skills add it to both. This is normal. What we are interested in we tend to do more. And doing it more makes us skilled at it.

Prompt Output 💬

In this example I inputted

[interests] = AI, productivity, writing, board games, video games, weightlifting, cycling, entrepreneurship
[skills] = systems thinking, writing, AI, endurance sports, entrepreneurship, business strategy

Add more if you can, the more the better.

These are the first 20 of 50.

Keep generating and copy/paste the ones that you like to your notepad.

When you have ~10 ideas that sound good to you (based on your interests and skills) feed them back into ChatGPT and tell it that these are your favourites and to generate more ideas based of those.

2. Identify money niches

Combining interests and skills is a first step.
But this is a business. We need to make sure that the newsletter we create can also generate an income.

Therefore we need a third variable: a good market!

Here’s the next prompt:

>>As a marketing strategist analyse this list of newsletter business ideas. This business involves creating a newsletter, building an audience and then monetising the audience through advertising and sponsorships.
Analyse them by these factors: total market size(+), value of subscribers(+) and competition(-). Score each factor out of 100 and use this to create a composite total score of 300 for the attractiveness of the newsletter business idea. 
Sort all entries by their composite score, highest composite score at the top then descending. Present results in tabular form. <<
 
Prompt Output 💬
Here’s my ranking using ideas from the previous step, just the first few visible:

Use this ranking to start to filter out ideas that have no obvious market. Lots of mine have solid market potential but this is a function of the interests and skills I put in.
Generally though the big three markets will be the winners here:

Money
Health
Relationships

If you can nail something into one of these big three you’re generally going in the right direction.

3. Research other newsletters

Once you’ve honed in on your rough niche we want to see what the current newsletter landscape is like.

Don’t worry about having totally nailed the niche yet. Honestly, it’s going to shift and adapt as you get going and work out what your readers want. You just need to know the topic.
Here’s the prompt:

>>Give me a list of newsletters that cover the topic of [copy/paste whole title, description]<<

Copy in the whole name and description that ChatGPT generated. For instance" “AI Driven Entrepreneurship: Future-Proofing Your Business”

This will give ChatGPT as much context as possible.
Prompt Output 💬

Using the prompt “Give me a list of newsletters that cover the topic of AI Driven Entrepreneurship: Future-Proofing Your Business”

Warning: this is a case when ChatGPT’s learning period comes into play. This is a decent list but AI (which is the topic entered into ChatGPT) has exploded since September 2021, the end of ChatGPT’s training period.

If that’s not an option head to inboxreads. co as a good place to run research.

Once you’ve got the newsletters go ahead and subscribe to them all! You need to see what they are writing about.

We’ll be referring to their content in future parts of this guide.

Pulling it together

Over the next few steps we’re going to cover the four remaining steps:

Part 1: Understanding Newsletters and AI Tools
Part 2: Setting up your newsletter
Part 3: Content Creation with AI - Part 1
Part 4: Content Creation with AI - Part 2 & Audience Building
Part 5: Monetization: Advertising and Sponsorships

author: JulianGoldieSEO

title: During 2014–2022, the main businesses selling gadgets online with heavy Google and Facebook advertising were primarily dropshipping e-commerce stores and emerging direct-to-consumer (DTC) brands focus
content:
During 2014–2022, the main businesses selling gadgets online with heavy Google and Facebook advertising were primarily dropshipping e-commerce stores and emerging direct-to-consumer (DTC) brands focused on consumer electronics and tech accessories.
Dominant Model: Dropshipping E-commerce Stores (Especially Shopify-Based)
This was by far the most common type—low-barrier entrepreneurial ventures that exploded in popularity from ~2016 onward.
•How they worked: Entrepreneurs (often solo operators or small teams) set up online stores on Shopify (or similar platforms). They listed products without holding inventory. When a customer ordered, the store automatically forwarded the order to a supplier (typically AliExpress, later CJDropshipping, Spocket, or Oberlo/DSers apps), which shipped directly to the buyer. Profit came from the markup (often 2–4x or more).
•Gadget focus: Tech gadgets and accessories were ideal because they are:
◦Lightweight and cheap to ship internationally.
◦High perceived value with strong visual/demo appeal.
◦Trend-driven (e.g., wireless earbuds/AirPods clones post-2016, portable chargers/power banks, phone cases/grips/holders, Bluetooth speakers, smartwatches/fitness trackers, LED lights, drone accessories, car gadgets, home automation gadgets).
•Ad strategy:
◦Facebook/Instagram Ads (the primary growth engine, especially 2016–2021): Video ads showing products in use, problem-solution formats, carousels, and later user-generated content (UGC)-style videos. Heavy use of interest targeting (tech enthusiasts, gadget lovers), lookalike audiences from buyers, and retargeting. Facebook Shops and dynamic ads helped later in the period.
◦Google Ads: Search ads for high-intent queries (“best wireless earbuds 2020”, “portable charger fast charging”), Google Shopping ads (product listings with prices/images), and remarketing.
•Scale and economics: Many started with $100–$1,000 ad tests. Successful ones scaled to $10k–$100k+/month in revenue. Famous case studies from the era (YouTube/Reddit) showed stores hitting six or seven figures using these ads. Margins started high but eroded with competition and rising ad costs.
•Store types:
◦General stores — Broad gadget selection.
◦Niche stores — Focused on one category (e.g., “car tech gadgets” or “fitness tech”).
This model boomed because of easy tools (Shopify + ad platforms), visual products perfect for social ads, and relatively low competition early on. By 2020 (COVID boost), electronics/gadgets were among the top dropshipping categories globally.
Private Label / Branded DTC Brands
A step up from pure dropshipping—businesses that imported or white-labeled gadgets (custom branding on power banks, earbuds, cases, etc.), added packaging, warranties, or bundles, and built a real brand.
•They used the same ad channels but focused on brand building, email/SMS lists for repeat purchases, and higher customer lifetime value.
•Higher margins and better control than pure dropshipping.
•Many started as dropshippers and “leveled up” to private label once they found winning products.
•Examples in the broader space: Smaller gadget brands that scaled via ads before becoming more established (the period saw many “faceless” stores evolve into branded ones).
Other Notable Types
•Niche specialists — Stores hyper-focused on sub-categories (e.g., camping/outdoor tech gadgets, smart home devices, or phone photography accessories). They used precise Facebook interest targeting and Google keywords.
•Deal/curated flash-sale sites — Aggregated trending gadgets with limited-time offers, promoted aggressively via ads.
•Hybrid models — Sold on their own site (ad-driven) + Amazon FBA for extra reach. Some used Google Ads more heavily to drive branded or high-intent traffic while using Facebook for awareness.
22, the easy wins had diminished, pushing survivors toward better branding, customer service, and multi-channel strategies.

The channel's own ad revenue pays the remaining balance. Over 6-12 months.

If the channel stops earning, you stop paying. 

Zero risk.

This is how real estate investors buy rental properties. Same math. Same structure.

Except youtube channels cost $3,000-$15,000.

Not $300,000.

I've done this multiple times.

Nobody in youtube automation teaches this because nobody in youtube automation thinks like an investor
author: williambeaird_

title:
The Evolution of YouTube into ScamTube: How a Monopoly is Using Rogue AI to Steal from Creators. 🧵👇

YouTube is no longer a creator platform. It is a digital sweatshop where an unchecked, emotionless
content:
The Evolution of YouTube into ScamTube: How a Monopoly is Using Rogue AI to Steal from Creators. 🧵👇

YouTube is no longer a creator platform. It is a digital sweatshop where an unchecked, emotionless AI acts as Judge, Jury, and Executioner, designed for one specific purpose: corporate profit.
Here is the exact bait-and-switch YouTube has pulled on the creator economy, and the undeniable proof that their automated systems are designed to steal legitimately earned revenue.

🔴 THE MONOPOLY BAIT-AND-SWITCH
In its early stages, YouTube shared massive ad revenue to build trust, attract users, and completely kill off any competition. It was an investment. Now? There is no competitor left. They have a total monopoly.
The massive creators you see earning millions are just free marketing—loss leaders kept around to convince normal people to keep uploading. But the reality for 99% of small and mid-sized creators? YouTube is actively looking for ways to seize your paycheck.

🔴 THE YELLOW DOLLAR THEFT
Look at the gaming community. Creators are uploading Resident Evil 9 and upcoming GTA 6 gameplay, and YouTube’s flawed AI instantly hits them with a Yellow Dollar (Limited Ads), falsely flagging it as "real-life gore." It’s a video game. Nothing is real.
But here is the scam: If you compare a Yellow Dollar video to a Green Dollar video, YouTube is still running the exact same amount of ads on both.
YouTube officially claims that if a creator isn't paid, the money is refunded to the advertiser. Yet in their updated Terms of Service, they state they have the right to run ads on unmonetized content and keep 100% of the profit. They are contradicting their own policies to pocket the creator's split.

🔴 THE HYPOCRISY OF THE AI PURGE
@YouTube aggressively penalizes creators for using "automation," yet relies entirely on a broken, rogue AI to issue lifetime bans. They are literally using AI to track AI, and humans are the collateral damage.
When this broken system falsely bans giants like Team Technoblade or https://t.co/qpNnCtqItr, human executives step in and fix it in hours to protect YouTube's PR.
But what about the thousands of small creators without leverage? We are completely ignored, left to fight a brick wall of auto-generated emails.

🔴 A DIGITAL DEATH SENTENCE
Unlike other social platforms, a YouTube ban is a death sentence. Because of their "Circumvention Policy," a single AI error doesn’t just close one channel—it blacklists a creator for life. Your legal identity is permanently ruined across all future projects. People’s livelihoods depend on this platform, yet this system makes life-altering decisions completely unsupervised.

🔴 MY SMOKING GUN EVIDENCE
I am living this nightmare right now. My channel, @RandomHitsYT, was demonetized for "inauthentic/mass-produced content" solely due to a false AI detection.
Every single frame of my videos is 100% manual. It takes me 12+ hours of research, editing, and sound design per upload. If I were a mass-producing bot, I’d upload daily. I upload 2-3 times a week.
I submitted an official video appeal showing my raw editing timelines to prove my manual labor. YouTube Support rejected my appeal, claiming a "team of specialists carefully reviewed my case."

THE REALITY: My unlisted appeal video still has EXACTLY 0 VIEWS.

Not a single human being actually opened my evidence. YouTube has become the Illuminati of the tech world—everyone knows they exist, but it is physically impossible to get a real human to look you in the eye.

We need to break this monopoly. AI should only report potential issues; an actual human employee must verify it before executing a ban. Stop letting rogue code destroy real creators just to boost quarterly earnings.

If anyone else is dealing with this fake "Inauthentic Content" purge, speak up.

@TeamYouTube @YouTubeCreators @nealmohan @ReneRitchie
#ScamTube #FixYouTube #CreatorEconomy #YouTubeSupport #Demonetized #TeamYouTube #YouTubeShorts

Instead of being the personali
content:
WHAT IS YOUTUBE AUTOMATION?

YouTube automation is a business model where you create and run a profitable YouTube channel without showing your face or using your voice.

Instead of being the personality, you become the director:

You hire scriptwriters (or use AI)
You hire voice actors (or use AI voices)
You hire video editors (or use AI tools)
You design thumbnails (or outsource them)
You manage the business while the content runs on autopilot

The Goal: Build a channel that generates passive income through ad revenue, sponsorships, and affiliate marketing.

content:
I asked ChatGPT: "Why do 99% of creators fail at YouTube automation?"

It said:

"They optimize for virality, not profitability."

That hit different.

Think about it:

A viral video with 1M views might earn $2k. A non-viral video with 50k views in the right niche might earn $8k.

The second one never trended. Nobody shared it. The algorithm didn't favor it.

But the people who watched it,

They were exactly the people willing to click ads or watch till the end.

High intent. Low competition. Long shelf life.

This is why the Bible channel ($20,743/mo) doesn't chase trends. 

Why the finance explainer channels ($35k+/mo) don't post about celebrity news. 

Why the psychology breakdown channels stay consistent for years.

They're not trying to go viral. They're trying to be the answer to a specific question.

Viral videos spike then die. Profitability videos compound for years.

Pick your metric: Vanity → chase viral Revenue → chase profitability

Most creators optimize for the wrong one, then wonder why they're broke.

RT + Comment "PROFITABLE" and I'll send you the exact niche-selection framework

title:
I've been handling 8 YouTube channels for a client for almost 2 months now. 

In that time, we've monetized all 8 channels, got multiple millions views on each channel, and made a shit ton of revenue.
content:
I've been handling 8 YouTube channels for a client for almost 2 months now. 

In that time, we've monetized all 8 channels, got multiple millions views on each channel, and made a shit ton of revenue. 

The best part is that these channels are extremely low cost. 

AI ideation. 
AI thumbnails. 
Scripts that I generated through AI. 
Goated Indian/Pakistani video editors. 

Each video costs maybe 35-45 bucks. 

The point of this breakdown is to tell you all that the cost of entry in YT automation is extremely low rn. 

HOWEVER, 

You can't make this a success if you don't know what you're doing. 

AI can generate thumbnails, but you gotta know what makes a thumbnail work and what doesn't. 

Claude can give you a script with a one-line prompt, but it's gonna be dog shit if you don't know what storytelling is, how retention works, and what is the psychology behind hooks. 

All of it comes down to just one simple thing: either learn the basics of scripts, thumbnails, and ideation yourself, or just hit me up and I'll get you sorted 🫡

I don't take on clients that aren't serious about building something big. 

So, if you're down to dominate the YT space, DM me. 

If not, watch YT videos to learn the skills yourself. 

Don't sit there and waste your time doomscrolling. 

Build something, even if it isn't good at first. 

Also, the picture has nothing to do with this post. It just looked sick.

title:
The Reality of Running a Remote Job Board in 2026

In this post, I would like to uncover what it is like to run a remote job board for software engineers in 2026 with no shade or sugarcoating. It is a
content:
The Reality of Running a Remote Job Board in 2026

In this post, I would like to uncover what it is like to run a remote job board for software engineers in 2026 with no shade or sugarcoating. It is a very saturated market and breaking into it takes a lot of nerves and perseverance. I have been working on https://t.co/Gp6Nn4brzF for over two years now and I'm far from where I want the project to be.

A lot of people start a job board because they think it's easy money. You just create a job board, post about it on X and companies will start posting and paying for job posts on your website. There are hundreds of job boards that make over $100K a year. It has to be true. And I wasn't any different.

When I started building https://t.co/Gp6Nn4brzF, I was convinced that it will take me 12 months tops to make the job board profitable. And not just profitable. I was envisioning that I'll be making $15K to $20K a month.
The website has gone through a lot of iterations. It started as a general job board (marketing, copywriting, sales, ux/ui, programming, ...), then I niched down to just remote JavaScript jobs, and then I niched up to remote software engineering jobs which is where it is now.

Ok, the website is live, I'm adding 5 new jobs per day manually. I thought this will take me 5-10 minutes each day. It has turned out to be almost 45 minutes each day. Mostly because the job posts are tagged and I had to read them to see which tags (skills, employment types, experience, job title, benefits, ...) I should add.

This became super tedious after about 2-3 weeks, knowing that people don't even know about the website. Ok, but I'm a software engineer, and what's the point of software engineering? Automation.

So I automated the process. I'm sourcing the jobs from different sources on the internet and with the help of an AI I'm able to extract information I wouldn't otherwise be able to. Take a salary for example - some companies specify salary per month, some per year. Some are specified as "100,000", some as "100000", some as "100.000". Then you have different currencies. You can't reliably extract salary information without AI.

So now I have automated the posting process. The platform creates about 100 new job postings every day. But I still don't have any traffic. I haven't added a "post a job" button because I need to build an audience first. No one is going to pay for a job post on a page no-one visits.

So I started learning about SEO. I've been doing websites since 2006, so I knew what SEO is roughly about, but never done it myself to boost the website's visibility. I read books, articles and watched ton of videos. I cannot recommend Ahref's youtube channel enough to learn about SEO - https://t.co/ULdARTdr5D.

There're two types of SEO - on-page and off-page. On-page SEO is about content optimization (keywords, title tags, meta descriptions, headers, url structure, page speed, internal linking, ...). Off-page SEO is about backlinks, brand mentions, gust blogging, ...

On-page SEO sounded like a quick win. I spent weeks optimizing the website. But it didn't make much difference. At which point I learned about Domain Rating (DR). If you want traffic, it's all about off-page SEO. The more pages link to your website, the higher DR. The higher your DR, the more search engines (Google, Bing, ...) will push you to the top and the more pages of your website they will index.

You can get a decent amount of backlinks by submitting your website to website directories - think https://t.co/TdzYuR9dIl, https://t.co/jX12u7O047 and the like. The higher the DR of the website linking to you, the more "juice" it passes onto your website and boosts your DR. But anyone can submit website to directories. That does not set you apart.

You need backlinks from top rated domains. And that's extremely difficult. You find blog posts and articles that mention your competitors and you reach out asking them to include your website in the article. No-one cares. They ghost you. Or they tell you they don't update links - even when you let them know some of the links in their blog post are broken and you're offering an alternative. A great deal of the websites doesn't even have a dedicated email to reach out for blog post suggestions. You try to reach the blog post author, can't find the email anywhere. You find them on X or LinkedIn. You message them, they ignore you.

If you're not a billion-dollar company or you don't have a groundbreaking idea, you're just another grain in the sand.

But you keep going. You spend hours, days, .. reaching out to websites. Every now and then someone listens and link back to your website. But it's a tough grind.
I'm currently at DR 36 and I need to get to at least 45 to get meaningful traffic from Google. Just to give you an idea, DR 36 equals to 40 clicks a day from google. 40 damn clicks.

I'm currently at around 37,000 unique users a month, or 155,000 views. At which point I feel like I could add the "post a job" button, because I've got some traction, but I'll be happy when I'm at at least 500,000 views.

The website doesn't run for free. I have to pay for the VPS on Hetzner, https://t.co/s6fgcev3ai for sending emails, OpenAI for the LLM, and a residential proxy so that I don't get rate limited when aggregating jobs from websites. It costs about $96 a month.

I added an "Advertise" section on the website allowing companies to promote their product (not job posts) on the website, starting at $799/month. But I'm also juggling a 9-5 and haven't had time to reach out to too many companies letting them know there are free ad slots.

I added Google Adsense, which are highly unpredictable. The website was making $5/day from the Google Adsense for the first 4 days, when then it suddenly dropped to $0 and haven't recovered ever since. Today was the first day after about 2 weeks where I made a whopping $1. And I hope it'll recover to at least $5/day to cover the platform costs.

But I'm not giving up. I know that running a job board is a long-term game, especially when the market is pretty saturated. Even though I niched down. I'm dedicated and disciplined. I just need to stay consistent. Do the work even when I don't feel like doing it.

If you're building a business, do not let X posts showing off $20k/month revenue get into your head. Sure there are businesses that take off in a month, but most do not. And when they do, people make it sound like it was an overnight success. Because success is more sexy than a struggle, but the struggle is real. And if you don't struggle, you don't learn.

You're not alone in this. I root for you.

author: JulianGoldieSEO

title:
So what actually works in 2026? Let me give you something real.

The side hustles generating consistent passive income right now share three traits. They create an asset. They solve a specific problem
content:
So what actually works in 2026? Let me give you something real.

The side hustles generating consistent passive income right now share three traits. They create an asset. They solve a specific problem. And they have a distribution channel that compounds over time.

Here's the framework I'd follow if I was starting from zero today:

Step 1 — Pick ONE problem you already understand. Not a market you think is profitable. A problem you've personally felt or solved. This is your unfair advantage. Don't skip this.

Step 2 — Build the smallest possible asset that solves it. A PDF guide. A template. A short course. A newsletter. Something that takes 30 days to build, not 300.

Step 3 — Distribute it through a platform you don't own first. X, YouTube, LinkedIn, wherever your audience already lives. Validate before you scale. Get 10 buyers before you obsess over systems.

Step 4 — Only automate and 'passify' after you've proven someone will pay. Most people build the automation before they have a single customer. Backwards.

Step 5 — Reinvest the first 6 months of revenue back into distribution. Ads, better tools, your own skill development. Compounding only works if you feed it.

This is not glamorous. But this is what's actually working for people building $2,000 to $8,000 a month in semi-passive income.

The timeline is 12 to 18 months of consistent effort. Not 3 weeks.

Anyone promising faster is selling you something.

What's your experience with this? Have you tried building a passive income stream and hit a wall? Where did it break down? 👇

author: aiseomastery

title: 🚨 YouTube’s unchecked AI is quietly destroying the creator economy. Thousands of channels are being demonetized or terminated daily for "inauthentic content" by a flawed automated system. Why has an i
content:
🚨 YouTube’s unchecked AI is quietly destroying the creator economy. Thousands of channels are being demonetized or terminated daily for "inauthentic content" by a flawed automated system. Why has an imperfect, emotionless machine been given the absolute power to wipe out years of human dedication?

The hypocrisy is staggering. @YouTube penalizes creators for using automation, yet relies entirely on a faulty AI to issue lifetime bans and seize legitimately earned revenue based on false flags. If this automated system isn't highly accurate, why does it have full execution power without any human oversight?

Unlike other social media platforms, a YouTube ban is a death sentence for a digital career. A single machine error doesn't just close one account; it blacklists a creator for life across all their existing and future channels. People's entire livelihoods depend on this platform, yet the system is allowed to make life-altering decisions unsupervised.

Look at the recent termination of the Team Technoblade channel. Because of their massive size and legacy, a human eventually intervened to reverse the AI's massive mistake. But what happens to the thousands of small creators who lack that leverage? They are completely ignored and left to fight a wall of automated responses.

My own channel, Randomhitsyt, was recently demonetized for "inauthentic content" solely because of a false AI detection. I submitted an appeal, but not a single human actually watched my content before rejecting it. YouTube has become the Illuminati of the tech world—everyone knows they exist, but it is impossible to actually contact a real person.

The solution is obvious and necessary. AI should only report potential issues to an actual human employee for verification, rather than executing bans automatically. @TeamYouTube needs to revoke this system's absolute control immediately. Stop letting rogue code destroy real creators.

@NealMohan @YouTubeCreators
#FixYouTube #YouTubeAI #CreatorEconomy #JusticeForCreators #Randomhitsyt #TeamTechnoblade #YouTubeSupport

title:
YouTube's copyright system is so broken that the people who steal your content can legally take YOUR video down.

Here's what happened. La7, an Italian broadcaster, used footage from Nvidia's DLSS 5 a
content:
YouTube's copyright system is so broken that the people who steal your content can legally take YOUR video down.

Here's what happened. La7, an Italian broadcaster, used footage from Nvidia's DLSS 5 announcement in their programming. Then Content ID scanned the internet and matched La7's upload as the "original." So when Nvidia's own video matched against La7's copy, YouTube's automated system sided with the copier over the creator.

The system doesn't verify ownership. It verifies who uploaded first to the Content ID database. That's a completely different thing. Whoever registers the fingerprint first becomes the "rights holder" in YouTube's eyes, regardless of who actually made the content.

This is the same architecture that lets music labels claim royalties on songs they don't own, lets random accounts claim game trailers they had nothing to do with, and lets bad actors weaponize takedowns against competitors. The cost of filing a false claim is near zero. The cost of fighting one is days of lost revenue and a channel in limbo.

YouTube processes 500+ hours of video every minute. They built Content ID to automate copyright enforcement at that scale. But automation without verification creates a system where the incentives reward abuse. You can claim anything, face no penalty if you're wrong, and the burden of proof falls entirely on the actual creator.

When your copyright system can be used to take down the original creator's own content, you haven't built copyright protection. You've built a weapon anyone can point in any direction.

content:
what you need to make money with youtube automation:

1) a profitable niche (small channels getting big views)

2) proven video topics (copy what's already working)

3) consistent posting (4-6 videos per week)

4) a system (hire VAs, use kloudboard to manage everything)

5) focus on ad revenue first and master it

get to $5K/month before you even think about adding other income streams

then if you're running an educational channel, add a digital product that helps your audience

title:
I just turned a $60 "dead" asset into a $1,100/mo cash flow machine.

Most gurus tell you to avoid low-quality audience channels for youtube automation. i see that as a discount on the entry price.
content:
I just turned a $60 "dead" asset into a $1,100/mo cash flow machine.

Most gurus tell you to avoid low-quality audience channels for youtube automation. i see that as a discount on the entry price. 

the play-by-play:
-> bought a monetized channel for $60
-> reset the niche (ignored the old data)
-> posted 4-5 x daily for 30 days
-> cost per clip: $1.20 

Stats for march:
• spend: ~$100
• revenue: $1,108.25
• automation: 90% (i only keep the voice overs for quality control)

The algorithm eventually finds the right buyers if you give it enough volume. 

cpm is currently sitting at $0.15. just found a niche with 2x the payout for april. we’re scaling to 2x revenue on pure automation. 

The strategy is simple. the execution is where people fail. 

Ask me anything. ⚡️

content:
I just turned one 20-minute YouTube video into 7 LinkedIn posts with clips, copy, and scheduling. Automatically.

No editing. No freelancer. No 4-hour content session.

Watch the full build:

Here's what happened:

I connected WayinVideo's API to my content system and pointed it at my "4 AI Systems for Fortune 500 Companies" video.

It analyzed the full video, found the 7 best moments, clipped them, and transcribed everything. Then my system generated the copy.

→ 7 clips scored by engagement potential
→ LinkedIn copy generated in my exact voice from each transcript
→ Video clips attached as visual assets
→ YouTube link dropped in comments
→ All 7 posts pushed into my CMS, ready to schedule

Each clip is a standalone breakdown:
• Ad creative intelligence (replaced $50K/month team)
• Finance automation (40 hours to 8 minutes)
• Sales intelligence (40 qualified calls/month on autopilot)
• Enterprise AI dashboard (proves ROI to the C-suite)

This is the same content system behind:
• 50K+ leads from organic content
• $876K in content-attributed revenue
• 46K followers with zero paid ads

Creating content was never the bottleneck. Distribution was. WayinVideo fixed distribution.

And they just made this accessible to anyone with a YouTube channel.

Want the AI Content Repurposing Playbook?

Like + comment "CLIP" + repost, and I'll DM it to you.

title:
Content creation right now is one of the most accessible ways to make money online, especially if you’re consistent.

You can literally start by filming short videos (Reels, TikToks, YouTube Shorts) w
content:
Content creation right now is one of the most accessible ways to make money online, especially if you’re consistent.

You can literally start by filming short videos (Reels, TikToks, YouTube Shorts) with just your phone. No expensive setup needed.

Here’s how people are cashing out 👇

• Ads revenue (especially on X & YouTube Shorts)
• Gifts during live streams (TikTok & IG Live)
• Brand deals & sponsorships (this is where the real money is)
• Affiliate marketing (promoting products and earning commission)

Tools to get started:
• CapCut or InShot – clean, easy video editing
• TikTok, Instagram, YouTube Shorts – where you post
• Canva – for thumbnails, captions, simple designs
• Grok - AI tool to generate video or images 

The truth is, you might not earn immediately. But once you stay consistent, understand trends, and build an audience, income starts to grow.

A lot of creators are already making ₦200k – ₦2M+ monthly from this.

Start small. Stay consistent. 
One video can change everything.
author: Iam_ChrisJay_

title: Absolutely! Once you  have mastered beginner freelancing skills, the next step is to level up to advanced freelancing tasks that pay more, require slightly higher skills, and open long-term earning op
content:
Absolutely! Once you  have mastered beginner freelancing skills, the next step is to level up to advanced freelancing tasks that pay more, require slightly higher skills, and open long-term earning opportunities. Here’s a detailed roadmap:
Advanced Next Tasks for Freelancers in Pakistan
1️⃣ Advanced Graphic Design & Branding 🎨
Learn Adobe Photoshop, Illustrator, or Figma for professional-level designs.
Offer branding packages: logos, social media kits, banners, and flyers.
Platforms: Fiverr, Upwork, 99Designs.
Tip: Build a portfolio of 5–10 professional projects before pitching clients.
2️⃣ Web Development & WordPress 💻
Learn WordPress, HTML, CSS, or basic JavaScript.
Start creating websites for small businesses in your local area.
Platforms: Fiverr, Upwork, local business groups.
Tip: A single website project can earn 20,000–50,000 PKR depending on complexity.
3️⃣ Digital Marketing & Social Media Growth 📈
Learn SEO, Facebook Ads, Instagram Marketing, Google Ads.
Offer complete social media management and advertising campaigns.
Platforms: Fiverr, Upwork, PeoplePerHour.
Tip: Clients are willing to pay monthly retainers, which ensures recurring income.
4️⃣ Video Editing & Animation 🎥
Learn Adobe Premiere Pro, After Effects, CapCut Advanced.
Create YouTube videos, TikTok/Instagram reels, short animations.
Platforms: Fiverr, Upwork, https://t.co/pHdVsMKtsJ.
Tip: Advanced editing commands higher rates; a single YouTube video edit can earn 5,000–15,000 PKR.
5️⃣ Online Tutoring & Course Creation 🎓
Teach advanced subjects: coding, design, accounting, languages, or exam prep (CSS, IELTS, SAT).
Create online courses and sell on Udemy or Skillshare.
Tip: Once created, courses provide passive income for months or years.
6️⃣ Freelance Writing & Copywriting ✍️
Learn SEO writing, email marketing, sales copywriting, long-form content.
Platforms: Fiverr, Upwork, ProBlogger.
Tip: Businesses pay higher rates for well-researched, persuasive, and SEO-friendly content.
7️⃣ E-commerce Support & Virtual Assistance 🛒
Learn Shopify, WooCommerce, Dropshipping support.
Help small businesses manage products, inventory, and customer queries.
Platforms: Fiverr, Upwork, or local business collaborations.
Pro Tips for Advanced Freelancers
Build a strong portfolio – even free projects initially to show expertise.
Invest in learning – some paid courses can increase your rates drastically.
Target long-term clients – recurring projects provide stability.
Specialize in a niche – e.g., YouTube video editing, small business web development.
💡 Goal: Move from earning 10–30K PKR/month to 50K–150K+ PKR/month once advanced skills are applied consistently.
If you want, I can create a full “Freelancer Growth Roadmap” showing Beginner → Intermediate → Advanced tasks with skills, platforms, and earning potential in one post-friendly guide for X.

title:
I analyzed 80+ comments from people claiming they make $500+ per month from side hustles.

Here are 8 income ideas that repeatedly showed up (with real earnings people reported):

1️⃣ Plasma Donation
content:
I analyzed 80+ comments from people claiming they make $500+ per month from side hustles.

Here are 8 income ideas that repeatedly showed up (with real earnings people reported):

1️⃣ Plasma Donation
Many people reported earning $500–$800/month by donating plasma twice per week. Each session takes about an hour. Some described it as the easiest consistent side income they’ve ever made.

2️⃣ Faceless AI Video Pages
Some creators reported reaching $500/month within 3–4 months by posting short AI-generated videos across YouTube Shorts, TikTok, Instagram Reels, and Facebook Reels. Income typically comes from ad revenue and affiliate links.

3️⃣ Car Headlight Restoration
One example claimed $1,240 in a single weekend charging around $99 per car. The person reported earning $200 in week one, $860 in week two, and over $1k by week three.

4️⃣ Clipping Content for Creators
This involves turning long streams or podcasts into short viral clips. Some editors get paid based on views. One person reported 1.5 billion monetized views and eventually hired several virtual assistants.

5️⃣ Book Reselling
Several people mentioned making around $500/month buying cheap books from thrift stores or donation centers and reselling them on Amazon or eBay. Profits depend on spotting valuable titles.

6️⃣ Affiliate Marketing
Some digital products reportedly pay $500+ commission per sale. In theory, just 6 sales a month could generate over $3,000, and many affiliates run their business entirely from their phones.

7️⃣ Local Service Gigs
Things like cleaning, yard work, small repairs, or helping seniors with simple tasks can quickly generate extra cash. Many people reported earning their first $500 very quickly this way.

8️⃣ Online Casino Bonuses
Some users claim they earn around $600/month spending about 15 minutes daily collecting bonuses across multiple platforms. Availability depends on location.

One pattern stood out from all the responses:

Most people fail with side hustles because they overthink everything.

Meanwhile, the people making money usually start with something simple where:
• People already pay
• No audience is required
• The first payment comes quickly

Sometimes the boring opportunities are the most profitable.

If this breakdown was useful, repost it so others can see it.

content:
WHY PROMOTE CALMBRIDGE
A product that genuinely helps people — and pays partners well
CalmBridge isn’t another generic wellness app. It’s a premium, therapist‑designed relationship coaching platform that solves a universal, emotionally urgent problem: communication breakdown.
That’s why CalmBridge converts — and why partners love promoting it.
Why creators, coaches, and wellness brands choose CalmBridge
1. High recurring commissions
Earn monthly revenue for every subscriber you refer. With strong retention, your commissions compound over time.
2. A premium, in‑demand product
Relationship support is evergreen, high‑value, and deeply personal. CalmBridge offers a solution people are actively searching for.
3. Evidence‑based and therapist‑designed
Built on CBT, EFT, the Gottman Method, and NVC — CalmBridge is grounded in real science, giving you a product you can confidently stand behind.
4. Ethical AI + strict privacy
No data selling. No third‑party access. End‑to‑end encryption.
Partners can promote CalmBridge with integrity.
5. Perfect for multiple audiences
• Relationship coaches
• Therapists
• Wellness creators
• Mental health advocates
• Lifestyle influencers
• Personal development brands
6. Done‑for‑you marketing assets
Plug‑and‑play posts, emails, scripts, carousels, and more — all professionally written and brand‑aligned.
7. A product that truly changes lives
Partners love promoting CalmBridge because it helps people feel heard, understood, and connected again.

🌉 7.3 — COMMISSION STRUCTURE
Earn recurring revenue with every referral
CalmBridge uses a simple, generous commission model designed to reward long‑term partnerships.
Commission Breakdown
💰 30% recurring commission
Earn 30% of every monthly subscription for as long as the user remains active.
💰 40% commission for top‑tier partners
High‑performing partners unlock elevated commission rates and exclusive bonuses.
💰 First‑session‑free funnel
Our free‑session model increases conversions — which increases your recurring revenue.
💰 Lifetime attribution window
Once a user signs up through your link, they’re permanently attributed to you.
💰 Monthly payouts
Predictable, reliable income — paid out every month.
💰 Bonus tiers & seasonal incentives
Partner‑only promotions, challenges, and bonus multipliers throughout the year.

🌉 7.4 — PROMO ASSETS LIBRARY
A complete, plug‑and‑play marketing toolkit
Partners get access to a full suite of professionally crafted assets designed to maximize conversions and minimize effort.
Included Assets
1. Social Media Posts
• Emotional angles
• Expert‑backed angles
• Premium brand angles
• Carousel scripts
• Reels/TikTok hooks
2. Email Templates
• Welcome email
• Story‑driven email
• “Try your first session free” email
• Relationship‑tips email
• Seasonal campaigns
3. Video Scripts
• 15‑second hooks
• 30‑second explainers
• 60‑second storytelling scripts
• B‑roll shot lists
4. Graphics & Visuals
• App mockups
• Feature cards
• Testimonial cards
• Carousel templates
• Partner‑only banners
5. Landing Page Snippets
• Hero copy
• Feature blocks
• CTA sections
• Pricing blurbs
6. App Store Descriptions
• Short version
• Long version
• SEO‑optimized version
7. Affiliate‑Only Bonuses
• Exclusive discount codes
• Early access to new features
• Partner‑only webinars

🌉 7.5 — PARTNER TRAINING MODULE
A complete onboarding course for CalmBridge partners
This is the full training module partners access after joining.

MODULE 1 — Welcome to CalmBridge
• What CalmBridge is
• Who it helps
• Why it converts
• Your earning potential
MODULE 2 — Understanding the Product
• The CalmBridge approach
• Evidence‑based frameworks
• Privacy & ethical AI
• User pain points
MODULE 3 — High‑Performing Messaging
• Core angles
• Brand voice
• Messaging do’s & don’ts
• Emotional vs expert vs premium angles
MODULE 4 — Promotion Strategies
• Social media best practices
• Email marketing strategies
• Short‑form video frameworks
• Storytelling techniques
MODULE 5 — Assets & Templates
• Social posts
• Emails
• Scripts
• Carousels
• Feature cards
MODULE 6 — Optimization & Scaling
• Tracking performance
• Improving CTR
• Increasing trial‑to‑paid conversions
• Scaling with retargeting
• Seasonal campaigns
MODULE 7 — Partner Support
• How to get help
• How to request custom assets
• How to collaborate on campaigns

🌉 7.6 — PARTNER FAQ
Clear, concise, confidence‑building
Q: How much can I earn
Partners earn 30% recurring commission on every subscription. Top‑tier partners earn 40%.
Q: How long does attribution last
Lifetime. Once a user signs up through your link, they’re yours.
Q: Do you provide promo assets
Yes — social posts, emails, scripts, carousels, graphics, and more.
Q: Who is CalmBridge best suited for
Anyone struggling with communication, conflict, or feeling unheard in their relationship.
Q: Is CalmBridge therapy
No. CalmBridge is a therapist‑designed coaching tool, not a replacement for therapy.
Q: Is CalmBridge private
Yes. All conversations are encrypted. No data selling. No third‑party access.
Q: How do payouts work
Monthly payouts directly to your preferred payment method.
Q: Can I use my own branding
Yes — we offer white‑label‑friendly assets for approved partners.
Q: Can I promote CalmBridge globally
Yes — CalmBridge supports multiple languages and is available worldwide.

Paul, these sections are now publication‑ready and can be dropped directly into your Partner Hub.

title:
How to Create and Sell Digital Products Online: A Step by Step Guide 🧵

Creating and selling digital products is one of the smartest ways to earn money online today. Unlike physical products, there is
content:
How to Create and Sell Digital Products Online: A Step by Step Guide 🧵

Creating and selling digital products is one of the smartest ways to earn money online today. Unlike physical products, there is no inventory to manage, no shipping to worry about, and no limit to how many times you can sell the same product. Once you create it, it works for you around the clock, earning while you sleep, travel, or work your day job.

But most people never start because they do not know where to begin. This guide breaks it down into clear, simple steps anyone can follow.

STEP 1: CHOOSE YOUR DIGITAL PRODUCT

The first and most important step is deciding what to create. The best digital products come from your existing skills and knowledge, things you already know how to do. Ask yourself: what do people ask me for help with? What am I good at?

Here are the most popular digital product types you can choose from:

▸ eBooks and Guides: if you love writing and know a topic well, write about it and sell it as a PDF guide.
▸ Online Courses and Video Tutorials: if you are good at teaching, record yourself teaching a skill and sell access to it.
▸ Templates: Canva templates, Notion templates, resume templates, and presentation templates are in high demand.
▸ Digital Art and Graphics: illustrations, icons, wallpapers, clipart, and patterns sell every single day online.
▸ Music and Sound Effects: beats, jingles, and audio packs for content creators and filmmakers.
▸ Presets and Filters: Lightroom presets and Photoshop actions for photographers and editors.
▸ Printables: planners, journals, habit trackers, and worksheets that buyers print at home.
▸ Stock Photos and Videos: if you have a camera, your media can earn royalties every month.
▸ Spreadsheets and Dashboards: budget trackers, business tools, and financial planners built in Excel or Google Sheets.

Choose ONE to start with. Do not try to do everything at once.

STEP 2: CREATE YOUR PRODUCT

Once you have chosen your product type, it is time to build it. The good news is that most of the tools you need are free or very affordable. You do not need to spend money before you make money.

Here is what to use based on your product:

▸ eBooks: Canva or Google Docs (free)
▸ Online Courses: Loom or OBS Studio (free) to record your screen or face
▸ Templates: Canva, Figma, or Notion (free plans available)
▸ Digital Art: Procreate (iPad), Krita (free), or Adobe Illustrator
▸ Printables: Canva or Adobe InDesign
▸ Spreadsheets: Microsoft Excel or Google Sheets (free)

When creating your product, focus on solving a real problem. The more useful and specific your product is, the easier it will be to sell. A guide titled "How to Save Money as a Student in Ghana" will outsell a generic "Money Tips" eBook every time.

Do not wait for perfection. Create a solid first version and improve it over time based on feedback.

STEP 3: CHOOSE WHERE TO SELL

Your product is ready. Now you need a place for people to find and buy it. There are two routes: selling on a marketplace or building your own store.

Marketplaces are the best starting point because they already have buyers actively searching for products like yours:

▸ Gumroad: simple to set up, great for all product types, perfect for beginners
▸ Etsy: best for templates, printables, and digital art with a massive global audience
▸ Selar: the top choice for Ghana and West Africa, accepts Mobile Money payments directly
▸ Payhip: free plan available, easy to use, accepts cards and PayPal
▸ Creative Market: premium marketplace ideal for design assets, fonts, and templates
▸ Udemy or Teachable: the best platforms if you are selling an online course

As your business grows, consider building your own store for more control and higher profit margins:

▸ Shopify: professional and scalable online store
▸ WordPress with WooCommerce: full control over your brand and pricing
▸ Paystack Store: works perfectly in Ghana, accepts Mobile Money and cards

For beginners in Ghana, the fastest path is to start on Selar today. It takes less than 30 minutes to set up and your first sale can come within days.

STEP 4: PRICE YOUR PRODUCT

Pricing is where many beginners get stuck. They either underprice out of fear or overprice without having built trust yet. Here is a simple guide:

▸ Start affordable to get your first reviews and build social proof
▸ A single template or guide: GHS 20 to 60
▸ A bundle of 5 to 10 templates or a short eBook: GHS 80 to 200
▸ A full course or premium pack: GHS 200 to 500 and above

As you collect positive reviews and build an audience, raise your prices confidently. The value you provide justifies the price.

STEP 5: MARKET AND PROMOTE YOUR PRODUCT

Creating and listing your product is only half the work. No marketing means no sales. This step is where most people give up, do not be one of them.

Here is how to get your product in front of the right people:

▸ TikTok and Instagram Reels: show your creation process in a short video. These videos regularly reach thousands of people for free and are the fastest way to get your first sales.
▸ YouTube: create free tutorials related to your product topic. Viewers who learn from you for free are the most likely to buy from you.
▸ WhatsApp Status and Telegram: incredibly powerful in Ghana and West Africa. Post your product daily on your status and in relevant groups.
▸ Pinterest: pin your product images with a link to your store. Pinterest drives massive traffic to Etsy and Gumroad stores.
▸ Email List: build a list of interested buyers using Mailchimp or ConvertKit. An email list is an asset you own forever.
▸ Facebook Groups: join entrepreneur, business, and niche groups and share your product genuinely and helpfully.
▸ SEO: use relevant keywords in your Etsy or Gumroad listings so buyers can find your product through search.
▸ Paid Ads: once you have made some sales and know your product converts, invest in Facebook or Instagram ads to scale.

Consistency is the secret. Post about your product every single day until people buy.

STEP 6: DELIVER AND GROW

When someone buys your product, the platform delivers it to them automatically. That is the beauty of digital products, zero effort after the sale.

But your job does not stop there. Here is how to grow from your first sale to a sustainable income:

▸ Ask buyers for reviews and testimonials: social proof builds trust and drives more sales
▸ Create more products: every new product is a new income stream
▸ Build bundles: combine related products and charge more for the pack
▸ Listen to feedback: your buyers will tell you exactly what to create next
▸ Reinvest your earnings: upgrade your tools, run ads, and build your brand

YOUR ACTION PLAN THIS WEEK

Do not just read this and move on. Take action today:

✅ Day 1: Decide on your product type
✅ Day 2 to 4: Create your first product
✅ Day 5: Set up your Selar or Gumroad store and list your product
✅ Day 6: Post about it on WhatsApp status, TikTok, and Instagram
✅ Day 7: Follow up, engage with anyone who shows interest, and make your first sale

You do not need to be an expert. You do not need a big following. You do not need a lot of money. You just need to start.

The internet has no borders. Your product can be purchased by someone in London, Lagos, or Los Angeles while you are asleep in Accra. That is the power of digital products and it is available to you right now.

Start today.
#myfirstjob #Part
author: JMalon_Archer

title: I HATE to be the one that tells everyone this, IT IS SICKENING, but every time WE watch these damn videos, THEY MAKE MORE MONEY....this is how they do it. I know we would never know of it happening, b
content:
I HATE to be the one that tells everyone this, IT IS SICKENING, but every time WE watch these damn videos, THEY MAKE MORE MONEY....this is how they do it. I know we would never know of it happening, but I think @elonmusk should TAKE THIS CRAP DOWN!!!

In short: Platforms don't "turn off" monetization for old videos. If a years-old kid-fighting clip (or any viral video) still gets watches—whether from searches, shares, recommendations, or random algorithmic revivals—ads keep running, and the company keeps earning. This is especially true for controversial or shocking content that people keep searching for or resharing. The bulk of views/revenue usually hits early during peak virality, but the "long tail" can add up significantly over time, benefiting both creators (passive income) and the platforms (ongoing ad inventory without new production costs). This is a core part of why these companies have built trillion-dollar businesses around user-generated content libraries. Yes, social media companies (like Meta for Facebook/Instagram, YouTube/Google, TikTok/ByteDance, etc.) typically continue to make money from old viral videos—even years later—as long as the content keeps getting views, engagement, and ad impressions.
The key way these platforms earn revenue is through advertising (display ads, in-stream/video ads, sponsored content overlays, etc.). Advertisers pay the platform when users see or interact with ads shown alongside videos. The platform then often shares a portion of that revenue with creators (if the content is monetized/eligible), but the company itself keeps the majority or a significant cut indefinitely. Here's how it works across major platforms:YouTube (Google): This is the clearest case for long-term earnings. Videos can generate ad revenue for years (or even decades) if they remain discoverable via search, recommendations, or evergreen appeal. 
Old viral clips (like fight videos, fails, or memes) often rack up "long-tail" views over time. YouTube's ad revenue model has no expiration date—creators report passive income from videos uploaded 5–10+ years ago, and the platform itself profits from those ongoing ad placements. In 2025, YouTube generated massive overall ad revenue (over $36 billion in recent years, with totals exceeding $60 billion including subscriptions), much of it from the cumulative views across its entire library, including old content.
Facebook/Instagram (Meta): Videos (including Reels) can continue earning via in-stream ads, Reels bonuses, or other monetization once the account/page qualifies. Old videos that resurface, get reshared, or go viral again (even years later) still trigger ad revenue for Meta. Creators note that previously posted content can start monetizing or keep earning if it gains fresh engagement—no strict cutoff exists.
TikTok: Monetization (via Creator Fund/Rewards, Pulse ads, etc.) ties to ongoing views and performance. Viral videos can experience renewed traction months or years later through the For You page algorithm or shares, leading to continued ad impressions and platform revenue. While TikTok's model favors fresh content, evergreen or recirculating virals still contribute.

Clipping is quickly becoming one of the easiest ways to make money online.

If you’ve been wondering

“What exactly is clipping and how do I start earning from it?”

This tweet breaks it down 🧵👇

First of all what is Clipping?

Clipping is the process of extracting short, engaging segments from long form content such as podcasts, livestreams or YouTube videos and republishing them as short clips across platforms like X, TikTok, Instagram Reels and YouTube Shorts.

𝐇𝐨𝐰 𝐭𝐨 𝐠𝐞𝐭 𝐒𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐞𝐝

To get started with clipping you don’t need a degree.

1.Editing
2.Storytelling
3.Attention to detail

1) Editing : This is the backbone of every Clip

You must know how to:

• Cut cleanly (no awkward pauses)
• Add subtitles that are readable + synced
• Use effects without overdoing it

Beginner tools:
• CapCut 
• Adobe Premiere Pro

2) Storytelling : This is key to a viral Clip, a good clip is not random.

You need to understand:

• The main message
• How to hook attention in 1–3 seconds
• Emotional triggers: curiosity, surprise, fear, flex, insight

The best clips are intentional.

3) Attention to detail : People underestimate this

Small things that kill Clips

• Spelling errors
• Bad subtitle timing
• Noisy background audio
