title:
I'm 18 years old and I've made more from X than most people make at their jobs in 3 years.

not from luck. not from going viral once. not from some course I bought.

from understanding how this app ac
content:
I'm 18 years old and I've made more from X than most people make at their jobs in 3 years.

not from luck. not from going viral once. not from some course I bought.

from understanding how this app actually works while everyone else treats it like a diary.

let me show you exactly what I've learned so you can stop fucking around and start making real money from this platform.

the brutal truth about X

99% of people on this app will never make a dollar from it.

not because they're dumb. not because the algorithm hates them. not because they don't have good ideas.

because they have no fucking clue what they're doing.

they post random thoughts. they pray for virality. they check their notifications 50 times a day hoping something changed.

nothing changes. because hope isn't a strategy.

the 1% who actually print money from X? they treat it like a business. they have systems. they understand the game.

I'm about to explain the entire game.

why X is different from every other platform

let's get this straight first.

youtube: you need to produce videos for months before anything happens. equipment. editing. hours of work per video. algorithm takes 6-12 months to notice you.

instagram: you need to be attractive or have aesthetic content. reels die in 48 hours. the audience is there to scroll pretty pictures, not buy shit.

tiktok: teenagers with no money watching dances. your video gets 100K views and you make zero dollars because nobody watching has a credit card.

linkedin: corporate zombies jerking each other off about "thought leadership" while accomplishing nothing.

X is different.

X has founders. CEOs. investors. people who make purchasing decisions. people with actual money looking for solutions to actual problems.

and you can reach them by typing words on your phone.

no camera. no editing. no production budget.

just words that make people stop scrolling.

the barrier to entry is on the floor. the earning potential is through the roof. and most people are still sleeping on it.

the account architecture

before you post anything, your profile needs to work.

most profiles are garbage. confusing bio. no banner. pinned tweet from 8 months ago about something random.

someone visits your profile and has no idea what you do or why they should care. so they leave.

here's what your profile needs:

bio: [what you do] + [who you help] + [proof]

"I help B2B founders turn X into their #1 client acquisition channel. $2M+ generated for clients."

not "entrepreneur | dreamer | coffee lover"

nobody gives a fuck about your coffee.

banner: show your best result. client logos. revenue screenshot. something that makes visitors think "oh shit this person is legit."

pinned tweet: either your best performing content or a CTA capturing leads. this is prime real estate. don't waste it on some motivational quote.

your profile should convert visitors to followers at 25-30% minimum. if it's below that, your profile is broken.

the content that actually makes money

here's where everyone fucks up.

they think content that gets likes makes money. wrong.

content that gets likes and content that makes money are often completely different.

I helped a client get a tweet to 6 million views. want to know how many sales that day? zero. not even a low ticket product. nothing.

then we switched to content that averaged 7K-30K views. fraction of the reach.

he closed three low ticket sales and two 4-figure deals in a week.

less views. way more money.

why? because viral content attracts everyone. converting content attracts buyers.

viral content: hot takes, drama, controversial opinions, relatable jokes

everyone engages. nobody buys. they're there for entertainment.

converting content: specific problems, real solutions, proof of results, speaking directly to your ideal customer

fewer people engage. but the ones who do are ready to spend money.

you need both. but most people only chase viral and wonder why they're broke.

the content mix that prints

here's the ratio I use:

20% viral potential (broad appeal, hot takes, keeps the account interesting)

30% authority building (case studies, results, proof you know what you're doing)

30% problem-aware content (directly addresses your buyer's pain points)

20% CTA content (lead magnets, offers, clear calls to action)

most accounts are like 80% trying to go viral and 20% everything else.

flip it. watch revenue change.

the posting schedule

timing matters more than people think.

I had a client from the UK posting at 10am his time. felt natural to him.

except 10am london is 5am new york. he was posting bangers while 80% of X's active users were sleeping.

moved his schedule to 3pm-11pm UK time. same content. same account.

went from 180 average views to 1,400 average views.

8x improvement from changing nothing except when he hit post.

the times that work (EST):
- 7-8am: catch the morning scroll
- 11am-12pm: lunch break engagement
- 3-4pm: afternoon check
- 7-8pm: evening scroll
- 9-10pm: before bed browsing

post 4-5 times daily across these windows. schedule in advance using typefully or tweethunter.

if you're outside the US, adjust to hit these EST windows. that's where the buyers are.

the engagement game

posting is maybe 40% of the game. engagement is the other 60%.

most people skip this because it feels like work that doesn't benefit them directly.

those people stay broke.

here's the engagement routine:

morning (30 mins): before you post, go reply to 10-15 tweets from bigger accounts in your niche. not "great post bro" bullshit. actual valuable replies that showcase your expertise.

one good reply on a viral tweet can drive more profile visits than a week of your own content.

after posting (15 mins): respond to every comment on your posts within the first hour. the algorithm watches early engagement. fast replies signal activity and boost reach.

evening (15 mins): another round of replies. check DMs. continue conversations.

this daily engagement is non-negotiable if you want to grow.

the DM machine

your DMs are where deals close.

most people treat DMs as an afterthought. check once a day. give short answers. never initiate.

those people leave money everywhere.

when someone comments on your CTA post or engages consistently with your content, that's a warm lead. DM them.

not some desperate pitch. start a conversation.

"hey saw you grabbed the guide. curious what made you want to check it out?"

let them talk. understand their situation. then position how you can help.

I close deals entirely through DMs. no sales pages for some offers. no webinars. just conversations that convert.

the lead capture system

every day you post without capturing leads, you're leaving money on the table.

followers can disappear. algorithm can change. account can get suspended.

email subscribers are yours forever.

set up a simple lead magnet:

1. create a free PDF guide solving one specific problem (use google docs, takes a few hours)
2. set up a landing page on beehiiv (free) or stan store ($29/month)
3. pin a tweet offering it for a comment
4. DM everyone who comments with the link
5. add them to an email sequence

this runs 24/7 collecting leads while you sleep.

I've seen accounts build lists of 5,000+ subscribers in a few months doing nothing but this.

the monetization path

once you have attention and leads, here's how money flows:

level 1: low ticket ($27-$97)
- PDF guides
- template packs
- mini courses
- easy yes for people who just discovered you

level 2: mid ticket ($200-$500)
- full courses
- group programs
- intensive workshops
- for people who trust you and want more

level 3: high ticket ($1,000-$10,000+)
- coaching
- consulting
- done-for-you services
- for people who want you to solve their problem directly

most people try to sell high ticket with no audience and no trust. doesn't work.

build the ladder. let people ascend.

the compound effect

here's what most people don't understand.

month 1: feels like nothing is happening. 50-100 views per tweet. minimal engagement. wondering if this is worth it.

month 2: small improvements. maybe a tweet breaks 1K. starting to see patterns in what works.

month 3: momentum building. consistent growth. first real money coming in.

month 6: snowball rolling. content performs predictably. leads flowing daily. revenue consistent.

month 12: completely different life. audience that trusts you. income that doesn't require trading hours for dollars. options you didn't have before.

but most people quit at month 1 or 2 because they expected overnight results.

the compound effect is real. but it's back-loaded. you don't see it until you've put in the work.

why most people fail

let me be real about this.

most people reading this won't do anything. they'll like the tweet. maybe save it. then go back to posting random thoughts and hoping for magic.

that's fine. more opportunity for the people who actually execute.

the ones who fail all share common traits:

they post inconsistently. three tweets one day, nothing for a week.

they never engage. just broadcast and expect results.

they give up too early. quit before the compound effect kicks in.

they chase viral instead of building systematically.

they have no offer. just want followers for the sake of followers.

they treat X like social media instead of a business tool.

if you do the opposite of all that, you win.

the reality check

I'm 18. I run an agency charging $2.5K/month to manage X accounts for founders and business owners.

I've helped clients generate millions of impressions and close tens of thousands in deals.

not because I'm special. not because I got lucky.

because I figured out how this platform works and I execute consistently while most people fuck around.

you could do the same thing. you probably have more experience and expertise than I do in your actual field.

you just haven't applied it to X yet.

title:
🧠 Success, Stupidity, and the Algorithm

Has creativity died, or has the game simply changed?
Introduction

I still remember the days when, during my father’s time, hardly anyone in Pakistan took tele
content:
🧠 Success, Stupidity, and the Algorithm

Has creativity died, or has the game simply changed?
Introduction

I still remember the days when, during my father’s time, hardly anyone in Pakistan took television news seriously. Most people preferred tuning in to BBC’s Sairbeen — listening to its detailed analyses and commentaries with great interest.

My generation, on the other hand, grew up listening to state-sponsored narratives crafted by Punjab’s official writers and propagandists — and we believed them.
Now, several generations coexist in the digital space simultaneously. The noise is deafening. Chaos everywhere — Al-Aman al-Hafeez (God protect us).

I have two central questions to explore:
1️⃣ What exactly is the Zombie Mindset?
2️⃣ Why did so many professional journalists fail after losing their jobs and moving to social media?

We are living in an age where virality matters more than intellect or effort. A random sentence or a few seconds of video can garner millions of views, while scholars, thinkers, and journalists who have dedicated their lives to knowledge are buried under the avalanche of digital noise.

This raises a profound question: is the problem the audience’s taste — or the creators’ failure to understand the new rhythm of the times?

🎭 Scene One: The Economy of Accidental Fame
Social media has created a new model — accidental success.
Example # 1:
“How much for the ten-rupee biscuit pack, ji?”
YEAH 10 RUPEY BISCUT KA PACKET KITNEY KA HEY JI?
A casual line, a moment of unintentional humor — and suddenly, viral fame. No message, no purpose — just another hero born of meme culture.

Example #2:
Khaby Lame — The King of Silence
A Senegalese-Italian youth who, after losing his job in 2020, started posting TikTok videos. Without uttering a word, he mocked the absurd “life hacks” of the internet with mere facial expressions.
Today, with over 162 million followers and billions of likes, he is the world’s number-one TikToker, earning between 20 and 40 million dollars annually.

These are rare cases — yet millions are making Reels and TikToks, hoping for the same fate.

During India’s recent elections, Prime Minister Narendra Modi even boasted that millions of young Indians are earning through Reels.
But Indian journalist Ravish Kumar, in his program, exposed the truth — most are spending far more time than money earned. The illusion of success is far greater than the income itself.

🧩 Scene Two: Intelligence vs. Virality
Across South Asia, countless seasoned journalists left mainstream media to build independent YouTube or digital platforms. Yet most could not attract the audience they deserved.

Many of them had sacrificed their jobs for integrity and truth, only to find themselves unheard in the marketplace of algorithms.
Their research was deep — but the audience’s interest was shallow.
In Pakistan, for instance, Imran Riaz Khan built a successful digital identity around a specific narrative — Imran Khan and anti-establishment rhetoric.
Meanwhile, journalists like Wajahat S. Khan, who produce content of global and investigative quality, failed to command similar public attention.

This contrast reveals a painful truth: in today’s media world, it’s not content that sells — it’s the moment.
⚖️ Why Do Journalists and Bloggers Fail?
Here are a few critical reasons worth reflecting upon:

1️⃣ Fear and Financial Dependence:
Most journalists who lost their jobs are not financially independent. Burdened by family expenses, many slip into depression.
In South Asia, journalism is already under siege; when income collapses, so does courage. They can neither freely speak their conscience nor take bold positions. If they criticize the establishment, the state targets them; if they praise it, the public rejects them.
Many must think bitterly:
“Perhaps that biscuit-pack guy is better off than me.”

2️⃣ Repetitive Topics:
Political bloggers repeat the same narratives, same faces, same accusations. Viewers are tired of the echo chamber.
3️⃣ Shift in Audience Preferences:
People are exhausted by daily political bickering.
They now seek refuge in Punjabi comedy, light drama, and escapist entertainment.
For them, “change” now comes from laughter, not from news.
4️⃣ Ignorance of Digital Language:
Traditional journalists still speak in long sentences and heavy analysis.
But today’s viewer prefers short, fast, emotional content — 30 seconds, not 30 minutes.
5️⃣ Content Fatigue:
Those who post daily vlogs or repetitive commentary have lost their novelty.
Viewers feel they are offering nothing new — only consuming their time.
6️⃣ Late Arrival:
Those who joined the digital game late found the seats already taken. Algorithms had already chosen their favorite faces.
Newcomers remained trapped in the background noise.
🧠 The Mindset Question
This is the paradox of our times:
On one hand, the same social media awakened public consciousness — sparking movements that toppled governments during the Arab Spring, in Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, and Nepal.
On the other hand, it also turns meaningless clips into overnight sensations.
Social media is both the highest expression of awareness and the lowest point of collective distraction.
It creates enlightenment — and zombies.
So the question remains:
Should we understand this digital world as a field of awakening — or a carnival of artificial amusement?

🤖 The Algorithm: The New Master of Minds
Today, human beings no longer decide whose voice will be heard.
Machines do. Algorithms calculate what can hold attention the longest — and that becomes “success.” In this system, knowledge, ethics, and depth become unsellable commodities.

💭 Conclusion: The Death of Knowledge or the Rise of Style?
Unemployed journalists and creative thinkers face a double crisis:
Should they serve truth — or the algorithm?
When intelligent people start measuring their worth in views and clicks, it marks the intellectual poverty of a society.
And so, in the end, only one question lingers:
Do we want viral people — or thinking people?

title:
🚨Content Is King: 5 Steps To Make Content Creation Your Hustle

In today’s digital age, content creation isn’t just a creative outlet—it’s a legit side hustle that can grow into a full-blown income st
content:
🚨Content Is King: 5 Steps To Make Content Creation Your Hustle

In today’s digital age, content creation isn’t just a creative outlet—it’s a legit side hustle that can grow into a full-blown income stream. Whether you’re blogging, vlogging on YouTube, or posting quick hits on TikTok, consistent content can build an audience and open doors to monetization. 
Here’s how to make content creation your hustle, with practical steps to get started and grow. #HustleHub #MoneyVibes

🤔Why Content Creation?
Content creation lets you share your passion, expertise, or personality while earning money. The internet is hungry for authentic voices, and platforms like blogs, YouTube, TikTok, and Instagram make it easier than ever to reach a global audience. With dedication, one post a day can snowball into a loyal following, unlocking revenue through ads, sponsorships, or your own products.

Step 1: Find Your Niche
Your niche is your unique angle—what makes your content stand out? Ask yourself:
🔸What am I passionate about? Cooking, fitness, tech, travel, or maybe DIY crafts?
🔸What am I good at? Share skills or knowledge others want to learn.
🔸What’s trending? Check X, or your specific platform, for hot topics, but add your personal spin to avoid blending into the crowd.

Pro Tip: A niche like “budget travel for students” is more targeted than just “travel.” Specific niches attract dedicated audiences.

Step 2: Pick Your Platform
Choose a platform that suits your style and audience:
🔸Blog: Great for in-depth content like tutorials or reviews. Use WordPress or Medium to start free.
🔸YouTube: Ideal for visual learners who love tutorials, vlogs, or storytelling. Invest in a decent camera or just use your phone.
🔸TikTok/Instagram Reels: Perfect for short, engaging videos. Trends move fast, so stay active.
🔸X: Share quick tips, engage with followers, and promote your other content.

Pro Tip: Start with one platform to focus your energy, but cross-promote snippets on X to drive traffic.

Step 3: Commit to Consistency
One post a day is a proven way to grow. It keeps your audience engaged and signals algorithms to boost your visibility. Here’s a sample schedule:
🔸Monday–Friday: Post fresh content (e.g., a blog post, video, or Reel).
🔸Weekends: Share behind-the-scenes or repurpose content on X to keep the buzz going.

Pro Tip: Batch-create content (e.g., film multiple videos in one day) to save time.

Step 4: Monetize Your Hustle
Once you’ve built an audience (even a small one), you can start earning:
🔸Ads: Platforms like YouTube pay for ad views once you hit monetization thresholds.
🔸Sponsorships: Brands pay for shoutouts or product placements. Reach out or join platforms like AspireIQ.
🔸Affiliate Marketing: Promote products and earn commissions via links (e.g., Amazon Associates).
🔸Sell Your Own Stuff: Create digital products (eBooks, courses) or merch once your audience trusts you.

Pro Tip: Engage with your audience on X to build trust—reply to comments and ask for feedback.

Step 5: Grow and Adapt
Track what works using analytics (views, likes, shares). Double down on popular content and experiment with new formats. Stay active on X to spot trends and join conversations. The more you post, the faster you learn what resonates.

Tools to Get Started
🔸Free Editing: Canva for graphics, CapCut for video edits.
🔸Scheduling: Buffer or Later to automate posts.
🔸Analytics: Use platform insights or Google Analytics for blogs.

Final Thoughts
Content creation is a hustle that rewards consistency and authenticity. One post a day can build an audience, and with time, that audience can turn into cash. What’s your niche? Share your ideas on X and start creating!

#DigitalNomad #MakeMoneyOnline #ContentMarketing #SocialMediaTips #InfluencerLife #MonetizeYourPassion #GrowYourAudience

content:
AI Money Printer Review [Glynn Kosky]

The AI Money Printer is being marketed as a revolutionary, AI-powered system that allows users to generate income by leveraging short viral video clips. 

Read More: https://t.co/9LNpG36Zlk

Promising to turn 15-second clips into $69 payments repeatedly, this tool claims to be a plug-and-play profit machine that requires minimal effort. 

But does it live up to the hype? 

In this detailed review, we’ll break down the features, benefits, pros, cons, and whether it’s worth your time and investment.

What is AI Money Printer?
AI Money Printer is a software tool that uses artificial intelligence to identify, edit, and monetize short viral video clips. According to the sales pitch, it automates the process of finding trending content, editing it for maximum engagement, and distributing it across platforms to generate income. The system allegedly requires just two clicks to operate, making it accessible even for beginners.

Key Features of AI Money Printer
AI-Powered Viral Clip Finder: The tool scans the internet for trending, high-engagement video clips that have the potential to go viral.

Automated Editing: Once a clip is selected, the AI edits it to optimize for platforms like TikTok, Instagram Reels, or YouTube Shorts.

Monetization Integration: The system claims to integrate with platforms that pay creators for viral content, such as ad revenue sharing, affiliate marketing, or direct payments.

Plug-and-Play System: No technical skills are required. Users simply select a clip, let the AI edit it, and publish it to start earning.

Fast Turnaround: The tool promises quick results, with the ability to generate $69 payments repeatedly from short clips.

User-Friendly Interface: Designed for beginners, the system is marketed as easy to navigate and use.

Benefits of AI Money Printer
Passive Income Potential: If the system works as advertised, it could provide a steady stream of income with minimal ongoing effort.

No Content Creation Required: Users don’t need to create original content. Instead, they can leverage existing viral clips.

Time-Saving: The automation features save users time by handling the editing and distribution process.

Beginner-Friendly: The plug-and-play nature of the tool makes it accessible to people with no prior experience in content creation or monetization.

Pros of AI Money Printer
Automation: The AI handles most of the heavy lifting, from finding clips to editing and publishing.

Low Barrier to Entry: No technical skills or prior experience are required.

Scalability: Users can potentially scale their earnings by publishing multiple clips across different platforms.

Trend-Driven: By focusing on viral content, the tool taps into what’s already popular, increasing the chances of success.

Cons of AI Money Printer
Unrealistic Income Claims: Promises of earning $69 repeatedly with minimal effort may be exaggerated. Viral content is unpredictable, and consistent earnings are not guaranteed.

Copyright Concerns: Using someone else’s viral clips could lead to copyright infringement issues unless proper licensing is obtained.

Platform Dependency: The tool’s success relies heavily on third-party platforms (e.g., TikTok, YouTube) and their monetization policies, which can change at any time.

Upfront Cost: The tool may require an initial investment, and there’s no guarantee of a return.

Saturation Risk: If many users adopt the same system, the market could become oversaturated, making it harder to stand out.

Who is AI Money Printer For?
Beginners: Those new to online income generation who want a simple, automated system.

Content Creators: Individuals looking to supplement their income by leveraging viral content.

Side Hustlers: People seeking a passive income stream with minimal time commitment.

Is AI Money Printer Worth It?
The AI Money Printer sounds appealing, especially for those looking for an easy way to generate income online. However, it’s important to approach this tool with realistic expectations. While the automation and simplicity are attractive, the income claims may be overstated, and there are potential risks, such as copyright issues and platform dependency.

Before investing, consider the following:

Do Your Research: Look for unbiased reviews and testimonials from actual users.

Understand the Risks: Be aware of potential copyright issues and the unpredictability of viral content.

Start Small: If you decide to try it, begin with a small investment to test the system before scaling up.

Final Verdict
The AI Money Printer is an intriguing concept that leverages the power of AI and viral content to generate income. While it offers several benefits, such as automation and ease of use, it’s not without its drawbacks. The income potential may be overstated, and there are risks involved, particularly around copyright and platform policies.

Read More: https://t.co/9LNpG36Zlk

If you’re willing to take a calculated risk and have the time to experiment, the AI Money Printer could be a worthwhile tool to explore. However, it’s not a guaranteed path to riches, and success will depend on your ability to adapt and navigate the challenges of viral content monetization.

content:
The Creator Economy in 2025: What’s Shifted? 💸

Platform competition is intensifying, all thanks to creators like you. These platforms are dishing out cash to keep the content rolling, and the pay landscape in 2025 is set to be quite different from what we see now. Here's a rundown:

- **YouTube** has launched Shorts monetization, directly challenging TikTok in the short-form video arena.
- **TikTok** is trying to catch up with YouTube-like ad revenue sharing, though CPM rates are still a contentious issue.
- **Instagram** is ramping up with creator incentives like Reels bonuses, subscriptions, and shopping features, despite a dip in organic reach.

Let's break down the strengths, weaknesses, and earning potential:

**Platform Clash: YouTube vs TikTok vs Instagram 🥊**

1. **YouTube: The Steady Income Giant**
   - **Ad Revenue**: With CPMs ranging from $3-$15+, YouTube offers the best rates around, depending on your niche and viewer demographics.
   - **Shorts Monetization**: Now sharing ad revenue from Shorts, creators are seeing substantial returns, expected to increase.
   - **Additional Revenue Streams**: Memberships, Super Chats, and brand collaborations add to the income pie.

   **Pros of YouTube:**
   - Designed for creator earnings with videos earning over years, creating a passive income stream.
   - Lucrative in high-value niches like tech, finance, and education.
   
   **Cons:**
   - Requires high-quality, consistent content. Virality isn't as instant here.
   - Steeper learning curve for content optimization.

2. **TikTok: The Viral Hit Maker with Monetization Hurdles**
   - **Creator Fund**: Still pays out small amounts, typically $0.02-$0.04 per 1,000 views.
   - **Ad Revenue Sharing**: Programs like TikTok Pulse are emerging but are still in their infancy.
   - **Live and E-Commerce**: Live streaming and product sales can be lucrative with an engaged audience.

   **Why TikTok Excels:**
   - Unparalleled for going viral; one video can catapult you to fame.
   - Ideal for creators who leverage trends and rapid content creation.

   **Cons:**
   - Low earnings from the Creator Fund compared to other platforms.
   - Heavy reliance on brand deals, live gifts, or selling products for significant income.

3. **Instagram: The Brand Partnership Hub**
   - **Reels Bonuses**: Payouts can range from $1,000-$10,000+ for top performers.
   - **Subscriptions**: A boon for creators with a dedicated following.
   - **Affiliate and Shopping Tools**: Easy monetization through product endorsements.

   **Instagram's Edge:**
   - Preferred by brands for collaborations, maintaining a professional aesthetic.
   - Best suited for visual niches like fashion, fitness, or travel.

   **Cons:**
   - Organic reach has decreased, making growth tougher.
   - Reels earnings don't match up to YouTube's ad revenue potential.

**The Final Call: Which Platform Will Make You Money in 2025? 🏆**
- **YouTube** for sustainable, growing income due to high CPM and long-term video earnings.
- **TikTok** for explosive growth and visibility, though monetization requires creativity.
- **Instagram** if your income strategy revolves around brand deals and affiliate marketing.

**Your Game Plan for 2025: Where to Channel Your Energy 🎯**
1. **Identify Your Strengths:**
   - Long-form content? YouTube is your arena.
   - Quick, trendy videos? Thrive on TikTok.
   - Visual storytelling or a dedicated niche? Instagram's your place.

2. **Diversify Your Presence:**
   - Test content across platforms to find where you resonate most, then focus your efforts there.

3. **Look Beyond Platforms:**
   - Leverage brand partnerships, digital products, or affiliate marketing to boost your income.

**Your 30-Day Challenge ⚡**
- Choose one platform, commit to consistent posting for 30 days, and track your progress. Share your journey by tagging @ViralEngineAI on X!

**Quick Tips for Creators 🍪**
- **Capture Attention:** Make your first 5 seconds count with an engaging

content:
𝐓𝐢𝐤𝐓𝐨𝐤 𝐁𝐚𝐧 𝐋𝐨𝐨𝐦𝐬: 𝐈𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐜𝐭 𝐔𝐧𝐤𝐧𝐨𝐰𝐧 
The potential impact of a TikTok ban in the United States is a major point of debate as experts weigh the pros and cons. A ban would likely cause significant disruption to American youth culture and have substantial economic repercussions for both creators and companies.  According to multiple sources,  TikTok's influence on news consumption has been evident, with a recent survey by Pew Research showing that one in four people under 29 regularly used TikTok for news consumption. 

A ban could potentially benefit American rivals such as Instagram Reels and YouTube Shorts. Experts have predicted a "measurably positive impact" on the profits of these companies due to increased usage.  However, this is just a glimpse into the potential consequences of a ban. Some creators worry that they would lose around 50% of their income if TikTok were banned. This concern highlights the deep connection between TikTok and the lives of many users, who rely heavily on it for entertainment and earning potential.

The impact of such a ban could be felt globally as it potentially triggers cultural shifts in the way content is shared and consumed. The sudden removal of TikTok would leave a void, potentially leading to increased user engagement and a need for platforms like Instagram Reels or YouTube Shorts to adapt their strategies to meet the demand of users seeking alternative platforms for short-form video sharing.

title: This is the number one way to replace your 9-5 with a Freedom Business in 2024:
content:
This is the number one way to replace your 9-5 with a Freedom Business in 2024:                                                                                                                                                                                                              

Set yourself up for delayed gratification.

How the hell do you do that?

1️⃣ Choose the media type you love creating.

For me this is writing.

I started with Superhero Jacked, which was primarily SEO (2000+ articles now), and funneled into email marketing (I have collected over a MILLION emails organically).

Now in my personal brand I’m focused on writing on social platforms like L and X.

For my brother this is short form video.

We scaled him to 10K/m within 6 months and 100K within 12 months with minimal writing, and simple funnels, because that was best for him.

2️⃣ Choose a niche you wake up excited to learn about.

Too many people get into online business for the money, when in reality: there’s money everywhere.

There are people out there making upwards of 50K a month opening Pokemon cards.

Start with something you’ll LOVE creating, and a topic you LOVE learning about, and you’re already ahead of the game.

3️⃣ Choose a platform that matches your energy.

For me I started with SEO on my own website because I didn’t want to engage/network too much and I loved writing.

For my brother we had him on TikTok/Reels because his short form content made the most impact there.

For Max (we scaled to 200K+ subs on YouTube) a faceless YouTube was the best option because he wanted YouTube to host it and he liked the idea of earning primarily from the ad rev.

———

If you lead with these things you’re leading with delayed gratification because you’re focusing on building a business you can wake up excited to create for.

Six months to a year from now you’re going to wake up still excited to create, but you’ll have slowly gotten better and better at your content and will be bringing in an income that is overtaking your 9-5.

——
